## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term **Specific Yield** of an aquifer. How is it related to **Specific Retention** and **Porosity**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Specific Yield (Sy) of an aquifer is defined as the ratio of the volume of water that will drain freely from a saturated aquifer under the influence of gravity to the total volume of the aquifer material.

$$S_y = \frac{V_{drained}}{V_{total}}$$

It is dimensionless and is expressed as a fraction or percentage. The relationship with Specific Retention (Sr) and Porosity (n) is given by:

$$S_y + S_r = n$$

where Sr is the volume of water retained in the pores against gravity, and n is the total porosity. Thus, the water held by molecular and capillary forces in the pores is specific retention, and the freely drainable water is specific yield, the two summing to total porosity.

**(3 marks: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark relationship explained)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between **Storage Coefficient (S)** of a confined and an unconfined aquifer with typical numerical ranges.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Unconfined Aquifer | Confined Aquifer |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Volume of water released per unit area per unit decline in water table | Volume of water released per unit area per unit decline in piezometric head |
| Mechanism | Gravity drainage of pores | Elastic expansion of water and compression of aquifer skeleton |
| Formula | $S \approx S_y$ | $S = S_s \cdot b = \rho g (\alpha + n\beta) \cdot b$ |
| Magnitude | **0.01 to 0.30** (large) | **0.00005 to 0.005** (very small) |
| Typical value | 0.10 – 0.25 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$ |

**(3 marks: 1 mark each for unconfined, confined, and magnitude difference)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q.A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** Discuss the **storage properties** of an aquifer in detail.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 marks:** Derive the expression for the **storage coefficient of a confined aquifer** in terms of compressibility parameters.

**Model Solution:**

When the piezometric head in a confined aquifer drops by an amount $\Delta h$, water is released by:
1. **Expansion of water** due to decrease in pressure.
2. **Compression of aquifer skeleton** (solid matrix expands slightly into the pore space).

Let the aquifer have a unit horizontal area and saturated thickness $b$. The mass of water released from the unit column due to a head decline $\Delta h$ is:

$$\Delta V_w = n \cdot b \cdot \beta \cdot \rho_w g \cdot \Delta h \quad \text{(water expansion)}$$

$$\Delta V_{skel} = b \cdot \alpha \cdot \rho_w g \cdot \Delta h \quad \text{(skeleton expansion)}$$

where:
- $\beta$ = compressibility of water = $4.4 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/N
- $\alpha$ = compressibility of the aquifer skeleton (1/Pa)
- $\rho_w$ = $1000$ kg/m³, $g$ = $9.81$ m/s²

**Total water released per unit area per unit head drop:**

$$\boxed{S = \rho_w g (\alpha + n\beta) \cdot b = S_s \cdot b}$$

This is the **Storage Coefficient of a confined aquifer**, dimensionless.

**(7 marks: 2 marks setup + 2 marks two mechanisms + 2 marks derivation + 1 mark final boxed expression)**

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 marks:** A confined aquifer of thickness **30 m** has the following properties: porosity $n = 0.25$, compressibility of water $\beta = 4.4 \times 10^{-10}$ m²/N, compressibility of aquifer skeleton $\alpha = 1.0 \times 10^{-8}$ m²/N. Calculate the **Storage Coefficient (S)** and **Specific Storage (Ss)**. If the hydraulic conductivity is $K = 25$ m/day, find the **Transmissivity (T)** and **Hydraulic Diffusivity (D)**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Specific Storage.**

$$S_s = \rho_w \cdot g \cdot (\alpha + n\beta)$$

$$S_s = 1000 \times 9.81 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-8} + 0.25 \times 4.4 \times 10^{-10})$$

$$S_s = 9810 \times (1.0 \times 10^{-8} + 1.1 \times 10^{-10})$$

$$S_s = 9810 \times 1.011 \times 10^{-8}$$

$$S_s = 9.918 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per metre}$$

**Step 2: Compute Storage Coefficient.**

$$S = S_s \cdot b = 9.918 \times 10^{-5} \times 30 = 2.975 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$S \approx 0.00298 \quad \text{(dimensionless)}$$

**Step 3: Compute Transmissivity.**

$$T = K \cdot b = 25 \text{ m/day} \times 30 \text{ m} = 750 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Step 4: Compute Hydraulic Diffusivity.**

$$D = \frac{T}{S} = \frac{750}{0.00298} = 2.52 \times 10^{5} \text{ m}^2/\text{day}$$

$$\boxed{S_s = 9.92 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /m}, \quad S = 2.98 \times 10^{-3}, \quad T = 750 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}, \quad D = 2.52 \times 10^{5} \text{ m}^2/\text{day}}$$

**(7 marks: 1 mark Ss formula + 1 mark Ss value + 1 mark S + 1 mark T + 1 mark D + 2 marks for substitution accuracy)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q.B [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** Explain the **transmissivity properties** of an aquifer in detail with relevant derivations.

**Sub-part (a) — 7 marks:** Starting from **Darcy's Law**, derive the expression for **Transmissivity (T)** of an aquifer and explain its physical significance.

**Model Solution:**

Consider a horizontal aquifer of saturated thickness $b$ and unit width. Applying Darcy's Law for horizontal flow:

$$Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area perpendicular to flow = $b \times 1$ (unit width).

$$Q = K \cdot \frac{dh}{dx} \cdot b \cdot 1 = K \cdot b \cdot \frac{dh}{dx}$$

By definition, **Transmissivity T is the discharge per unit width per unit hydraulic gradient**, hence:

$$\boxed{T = K \cdot b}$$

**Physical Significance:**
- T is the *integrated* flow capacity of the entire aquifer thickness (not just per unit area).
- Units: m²/day (in practice) or m²/s (in SI).
- T is purely an aquifer property, not of the fluid alone.
- It combines **medium property (K)** and **geometric property (b)**.
- T is used directly in the Theis equation for confined aquifer analysis.
- High T ⇒ excellent water-bearing formation (e.g., coarse sand with thick saturation).

**(7 marks: 2 marks Darcy setup + 2 marks T derivation + 1 mark units + 2 marks physical significance)**

---

**Sub-part (b) — 7 marks:** An unconfined aquifer has the following field data: specific yield $S_y = 0.20$, saturated thickness $b = 50$ m, hydraulic conductivity $K = 80$ m/day. A pumping test yields a discharge of **5000 m³/day** with a drawdown of **3 m** in an observation well at 100 m. Verify the discharge using Darcy's Law and find the **Hydraulic Diffusivity (D)**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute Transmissivity.**

$$T = K \cdot b = 80 \text{ m/day} \times 50 \text{ m} = 4000 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Step 2: Compute Hydraulic Gradient.**

$$i = \frac{\Delta h}{L} = \frac{3 \text{ m}}{100 \text{ m}} = 0.03$$

**Step 3: Apply Darcy's Law to verify discharge (per unit width is not given, so use full area).**

Assuming the aquifer width perpendicular to flow is $w$, cross-sectional area $A = b \cdot w$. For a *unit width* assessment:

$$Q_{unit} = T \cdot i = 4000 \times 0.03 = 120 \text{ m}^3/\text{day per metre width}$$

For total discharge of 5000 m³/day, required width is:

$$w = \frac{5000}{120} = 41.67 \text{ m} \approx 42 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Storage Coefficient for unconfined aquifer.**

$$S = S_y = 0.20$$

**Step 5: Hydraulic Diffusivity.**

$$D = \frac{T}{S} = \frac{4000}{0.20} = 20000 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}$$

$$\boxed{T = 4000 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}, \quad D = 2.0 \times 10^{4} \text{ m}^2/\text{day}}$$

**(7 marks: 1 mark T + 1 mark i + 2 marks Q verification + 1 mark S + 2 marks D)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Zones:**
> 1. **Unit confusion for K**: Always specify whether K is in m/s or m/day. The product T = K·b will be in m²/s or m²/day correspondingly. Mixing units costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting ρ_w·g in S_s formula**: Writing $S_s = (\alpha + n\beta)$ *without* the $\rho_w g$ factor is a classic mistake. The full expression $S_s = \rho g (\alpha + n\beta)$ is mandatory.
> 3. **Confusing Sy with S**: In an unconfined aquifer, $S \approx S_y$, but students often use Sy = 1.0 (which is *porosity*, not specific yield). For Sy = 0.20, the storage coefficient is also 0.20.
> 4. **Sign conventions**: $S$ is *positive* even when discussing water release. Do not introduce a negative sign.
> 5. **Missing the factor 2**: In the Theis equation (Module 2 onward), the well function $W(u)$ argument is $u = r^2 S / (4 T t)$. The factor of **4** is often dropped — preserve it.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Aquifer** = saturated, permeable formation that yields water economically.
- [x] **Porosity (n)** = Vv / Vt — total void fraction.
- [x] **Specific Yield (Sy)** = volume of water drained by gravity / total volume.
- [x] **Specific Retention (Sr)** = volume retained against gravity / total volume.
- [x] **Identity $S_y + S_r = n$** — must be quoted in every definition question.
- [x] **Storage Coefficient (S)** = volume released per unit area per unit head change.
- [x] **Unconfined S ≈ Sy (0.01–0.30)**; **Confined S = Ss·b (10⁻⁵–10⁻³)**.
- [x] **Specific Storage Ss = ρw·g·(α + nβ)** — always with $\rho_w g$ factor.
- [x] **Darcy's Law**: $Q = K i A$; velocity form: $v = K i$.
- [x] **Transmissivity T = K·b** — discharge per unit width per unit gradient.
- [x] **Hydraulic Diffusivity D = T/S** — controls pressure pulse propagation.
- [x] **Units Mastery**: K (m/day), T (m²/day), S (dimensionless), Ss (1/m).
- [x] **High T + High Sy** = excellent aquifer; **Low T + Low Sy** = poor aquifer/aquitard.
- [x] **KTU Numerical Tip**: Always state assumptions (laminar flow, isotropic medium, homogeneous, Darcy regime valid).
- [x] **Order-of-magnitude check**: For confined aquifers, S should be a small decimal (not close to 0.1). If your answer is > 0.1, recheck the calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Darcy's Law (Review): Steady Unidirectional Flow - Steady Flow in a Homogenous Aquifer - Aquifer with Recharge - Flow into Infiltration Galleries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Darcy's Law (Review): Steady Unidirectional Flow - Steady Flow in a Homogenous Aquifer - Aquifer with Recharge - Flow into Infiltration Galleries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Darcy's Law — The Foundation of Groundwater Hydraulics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DARCY'S LAW (1856)**: An empirical relationship established by Henry Darcy based on his experiments on the filtration of water through sand filters in Dijon, France. It states that the volumetric rate of flow (\(Q\)) through a saturated porous medium is **directly proportional** to the hydraulic gradient (\(i\)) and the cross-sectional area (\(A\)) of flow, and is governed by the **hydraulic conductivity (\(K\))** of the medium.

The governing mathematical form:

$$
Q = -K \cdot A \cdot \frac{dh}{dL}
$$

Where the negative sign indicates flow occurs in the direction of **decreasing hydraulic head** (from higher energy to lower energy state). The discharge velocity is:

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = -K \cdot \frac{dh}{dL}
$$

Where:
- \(Q\) = Volumetric discharge (m³/s or L/s)
- \(K\) = **Hydraulic Conductivity** (m/s or cm/s) — *Standard unit: **m/day** in groundwater practice*
- \(A\) = Cross-sectional area perpendicular to flow (m²)
- \(\frac{dh}{dL}\) = Hydraulic gradient (dimensionless)
- \(v\) = Darcy (or discharge) velocity (m/s)

### 1.2 Steady Unidirectional Flow

> [!NOTE]
> **STEADY UNIDIRECTIONAL FLOW** is a flow condition where the hydraulic head at any point in the aquifer does **not change with time** (steady state: \(\frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0\)), and the groundwater moves along a single, well-defined direction (unidirectional: one-dimensional flow along one axis).

In practical terms, this means:
- The flow rate is constant over time.
- Streamlines are parallel and straight.
- Equipotential lines (lines of equal head) are perpendicular to the streamlines.
- The driving force is purely the spatial head difference.

### 1.3 Steady Flow in a Homogeneous Aquifer

> [!NOTE]
> A **HOMOGENEOUS AQUIFER** is one in which the hydraulic properties (\(K\), porosity, thickness) are **identical at every point** in the spatial domain — i.e., properties do not vary with spatial coordinates (\(K = \text{constant}\)).

### 1.4 Aquifer with Recharge

> [!NOTE]
> An aquifer **WITH RECHARGE** experiences a continuous vertical addition of water from the ground surface (precipitation infiltration, irrigation return, river leakage) at a rate \(W\) (m/day or mm/day). This introduces a distributed source/sink term in the flow equation, fundamentally altering the head distribution profile from a straight line to a **parabolic curve**.

### 1.5 Flow into Infiltration Galleries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **INFILTRATION GALLERIES** are horizontal, perforated or porous conduits (typically pipes or tunnels) installed at shallow depths below the ground surface, **sub-parallel to the water table**, designed to collect groundwater through their top and sides. They act as horizontal drains that intercept the natural groundwater flow and convey it to a sump for extraction.

### 1.6 Real-World Intuition & Conceptual Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **THE "STRAW-IN-SAND" ANALOGY**: Imagine drinking water through a thin straw pushed vertically into saturated sand. The water flows into the straw because the suction you create lowers the hydraulic head at the straw tip. The **faster** the sand transmits water (higher \(K\)), the **faster** you can drink. The **steeper** the head difference (larger \(i\)), the **faster** the flow. This is Darcy's Law in action.
>
> **The "Gutter During Rain" Analogy**: Picture a sloped street gutter. Water enters uniformly from rainfall along its entire length (this is **recharge**). The water depth in the gutter is highest in the middle and lowest at the outlet — forming a **parabolic profile**. The "gutter" is our aquifer, "rainfall" is recharge, and the "outlet" is the gallery or drain.
>
> **The "Basement Window Well" Analogy**: An infiltration gallery behaves like a window well dug around a basement window. Groundwater seeping horizontally toward the well enters through the open sides and bottom — the gallery "skims" the upper saturated zone.

### 1.7 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Standard gravity**: \(g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2\)
> - **Dynamic viscosity of water** (at 20°C): \(\mu \approx 1.002 \times 10^{-3} \text{ Pa·s}\)
> - **Density of water**: \(\rho_w = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3\)
> - **Typical K values**: Gravel: \(10^{-1}\) to \(10^{-2}\) m/s; Sand: \(10^{-4}\) to \(10^{-5}\) m/s; Clay: \(10^{-9}\) to \(10^{-11}\) m/s
> - **Common aquifer thickness (b)**: 10 m to 100 m
> - **Typical recharge (W)**: 50 mm/year to 500 mm/year (0.00014 to 0.0014 m/day)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Conceptual Breakdown of the Topic Chain

#### Step 1 — Why Darcy's Law Works (Continuity + Proportionality)
- The flow is governed by viscous drag forces dominating over inertial forces (Reynolds number \(\text{Re} < 1\) to 10 — **laminar flow** regime).
- Darcy observed a **linear proportionality** between discharge and head loss, which holds until the flow becomes turbulent (transitional Re).
- The proportionality constant \(K\) encapsulates both the fluid (viscosity, density) and the medium (grain size, porosity, packing).

#### Step 2 — Steady Unidirectional Flow Assumption
- We assume \(\frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0\) (steady state) and flow is along the **x-axis only**.
- This collapses the 3D Laplace/Poisson equation into a 1D ODE: \(\frac{d^2 h}{dx^2} = 0\) for a homogeneous, isotropic medium with no recharge.
- Solution: \(h(x)\) is a **straight line** between two boundary heads.

#### Step 3 — Homogeneous Aquifer Extension
- With \(K = \text{constant}\) and no recharge, the 2D flow equation reduces to Laplace's equation: \(\nabla^2 h = 0\).
- For a confined aquifer of thickness \(b\), the **transmissivity** \(T = K \cdot b\) (m²/day) becomes the master property.
- The flow is **1D** between two fully penetrating rivers/sinks; head varies linearly with distance.

#### Step 4 — Inclusion of Recharge
- When vertical recharge \(W\) (m/day) is added, the governing ODE becomes: \(\frac{d^2 h}{dx^2} = -\frac{W}{K \cdot b}\) (confined) or \(\frac{d}{dx}\left(h\frac{dh}{dx}\right) = -\frac{W}{K}\) (unconfined).
- The solution is a **parabola** in the unconfined case, with the apex (maximum head) at the center of the recharge zone.

#### Step 5 — Infiltration Galleries as Boundary Drains
- The gallery acts as a **line sink** of infinite length, drawing water laterally from the surrounding aquifer.
- For a gallery at depth \(d\) below the water table in an unconfined aquifer over an impermeable base, the Dupuit-Forchheimer assumption gives a logarithmic head distribution.
- The discharge into the gallery per unit length \(q\) depends on \(K\), the head at the gallery, the far-field head, and the aquifer geometry.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE** (Memorize these for KTU ESE):

| # | Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions |
|---|---------|-------------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Darcy's Law (general) | $Q = -K \cdot A \cdot \dfrac{dh}{dL}$ | $K$ (m/s), $A$ (m²), $dh/dL$ (-) | $v = Ki$ (Darcy velocity) |
| 2 | Hydraulic Conductivity | $K = \dfrac{k \cdot \rho_w \cdot g}{\mu}$ | $k$ = intrinsic permeability (m²); $g$ = 9.81 m/s² | Depends on fluid + medium |
| 3 | Transmissivity | $T = K \cdot b$ | $T$ (m²/s or m²/day); $b$ = saturated thickness (m) | Confined aquifer |
| 4 | Steady 1D Confined Flow (Dupuit) | $Q = \dfrac{T \cdot W \cdot (H_1 - H_2)}{L}$ | $H_1, H_2$ = heads at boundaries (m); $L$ = distance (m); $W$ = width (m) | No recharge; constant T |
| 5 | Steady 1D Unconfined Flow (Dupuit) | $Q = \dfrac{K \cdot W \cdot (h_1^2 - h_2^2)}{2L}$ | $h_1, h_2$ = saturated depths (m) | Phreatic surface, $K$ constant |
| 6 | Steady Flow with Recharge (Unconfined) | $h^2 = h_0^2 + \dfrac{W}{K}(Lx - x^2)$ | $h_0$ = head at x=0; $W$ = recharge (m/day) | Symmetric recharge, drains at ends |
| 7 | Steady Flow with Recharge (Confined) | $h = h_0 + \dfrac{W}{2K b}(Lx - x^2)$ | $b$ = aquifer thickness (m) | Symmetric, drains at x=0 and x=L |
| 8 | Discharge to Infiltration Gallery (per unit length) | $q = \dfrac{K \cdot (H^2 - h_d^2)}{2L}$ | $H$ = far-field head; $h_d$ = head at gallery | Horizontal flow, Dupuit |
| 9 | Specific Discharge / Seepage Velocity | $v_s = \dfrac{v}{n_e}$ | $n_e$ = effective porosity (-) | $v_s$ is true pore velocity |
| 10 | Storage Coefficient (Confined) | $S = S_s \cdot b$ | $S_s$ = specific storage (1/m) | Elastic storage |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL DISTINCTION**: In KTU exams, the student **must** correctly identify whether the aquifer is **confined** (use \(T\)) or **unconfined** (use \(K\) and \(h^2\) forms) — this is a frequent 1-2 mark differentiator.

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drainage Design**: Computing spacing of horizontal tile drains in agricultural fields to lower the water table.
- **Landfill Leachate Collection**: Designing infiltration galleries beneath waste disposal sites.
- **Water Supply**: Rural community water supply via infiltration galleries along riverbanks (e.g., alluvial wells).
- **Trench & Excavation Dewatering**: Estimating flow into temporary infiltration trenches.
- **Soil Salinity Control**: Maintaining water table below root zone via interceptor galleries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Darcy's Law from First Principles (Control Volume Approach)

**Step 1: Set up an elementary control volume (REV) within a saturated porous medium.**

Consider a prismatic block of soil of length \(dL\), cross-sectional area \(A\), through which water flows unidirectionally under steady state.

**Step 2: Apply the principle of proportionality.**

Empirically, Henry Darcy observed that the discharge \(Q\) is proportional to:
- The cross-sectional area \(A\)
- The loss of head \(\Delta h\) over the length \(L\)

Thus, in proportionality form:

$$
Q \propto A \cdot \frac{\Delta h}{L}
$$

**Step 3: Introduce the proportionality constant \(K\).**

$$
Q = K \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta h}{L}
$$

**Step 4: Apply sign convention (head decreases in the direction of flow).**

If we take the flow direction as positive \(x\), and head decreases in that direction, then \(\frac{\Delta h}{L} < 0\). To make \(Q > 0\) in the direction of flow, we introduce a negative sign:

$$
Q = -K \cdot A \cdot \frac{dh}{dL}
$$

**Step 5: Define Darcy (discharge) velocity.**

Dividing both sides by \(A\):

$$
v = \frac{Q}{A} = -K \cdot \frac{dh}{dL}
$$

This is the **fundamental Darcy equation** in differential form.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady Unidirectional Flow in a Homogeneous Confined Aquifer

**Step 1: Problem Setup.**

A confined aquifer of constant thickness \(b\) (m) and constant hydraulic conductivity \(K\) (m/day) extends from \(x = 0\) to \(x = L\). The hydraulic head at \(x = 0\) is \(H_1\) and at \(x = L\) is \(H_2\). The aquifer has a width \(W\) (m) perpendicular to the flow direction.

**Step 2: Governing Equation.**

Under Dupuit's assumptions (horizontal flow, hydraulic gradient = slope of water table), and the steady-state Laplace equation for 1D flow with no recharge:

$$
\frac{d^2 h}{dx^2} = 0
$$

**Step 3: First Integration.**

$$
\frac{dh}{dx} = C_1
$$

**Step 4: Second Integration.**

$$
h(x) = C_1 \cdot x + C_2
$$

This shows \(h(x)\) is **linear** in \(x\) for a homogeneous, no-recharge confined aquifer.

**Step 5: Apply Boundary Conditions.**

BC 1: \(h(0) = H_1 \Rightarrow C_2 = H_1\)

BC 2: \(h(L) = H_2 \Rightarrow C_1 \cdot L + H_1 = H_2 \Rightarrow C_1 = \frac{H_2 - H_1}{L}\)

**Step 6: Head Distribution.**

$$
h(x) = H_1 + \frac{(H_2 - H_1) \cdot x}{L}
$$

**Step 7: Compute the Hydraulic Gradient.**

$$
i = -\frac{dh}{dx} = -\frac{H_2 - H_1}{L} = \frac{H_1 - H_2}{L}
$$

**Step 8: Compute the Discharge (per unit width).**

Using Darcy's Law per unit width: \(q = T \cdot i\) where \(T = K \cdot b\):

$$
q = K \cdot b \cdot \frac{H_1 - H_2}{L}
$$

**Step 9: Total Discharge Through the Aquifer Width \(W\).**

$$
\boxed{Q = \frac{K \cdot b \cdot W \cdot (H_1 - H_2)}{L} = \frac{T \cdot W \cdot (H_1 - H_2)}{L}}
$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Steady Flow in a Homogeneous Unconfined Aquifer (No Recharge)

**Step 1: Problem Setup.**

An unconfined (phreatic) aquifer rests on a horizontal impermeable base. The water table (phreatic surface) at \(x = 0\) is at height \(h_1\) above the base, and at \(x = L\) is at height \(h_2\) above the base. The aquifer width is \(W\).

**Step 2: Dupuit's Assumption.**

Dupuit assumed that:
- The hydraulic gradient equals the slope of the water table: \(i = \frac{dh}{dx}\)
- Flow is horizontal and uniform at any vertical section.

**Step 3: Apply Darcy's Law to an Elementary Strip.**

For a vertical strip of height \(h(x)\) and width \(dx\), the discharge per unit width:

$$
q = -K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dx}
$$

**Step 4: Steady-State, No-Recharge Condition.**

Since there is no recharge and the flow is steady, \(q\) is constant along the flow direction:

$$
-K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dx} = q = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 5: Separate Variables.**

$$
-K \cdot h \cdot dh = q \cdot dx
$$

**Step 6: Integrate Both Sides.**

$$
-K \cdot \int h \, dh = q \cdot \int dx
$$

$$
-\frac{K \cdot h^2}{2} = q \cdot x + C_3
$$

**Step 7: Apply Boundary Conditions.**

At \(x = 0\), \(h = h_1\):

$$
-\frac{K \cdot h_1^2}{2} = C_3
$$

At \(x = L\), \(h = h_2\):

$$
-\frac{K \cdot h_2^2}{2} = q \cdot L - \frac{K \cdot h_1^2}{2}
$$

**Step 8: Solve for \(q\) (Discharge per Unit Width).**

$$
q \cdot L = \frac{K \cdot h_1^2}{2} - \frac{K \cdot h_2^2}{2} = \frac{K}{2}(h_1^2 - h_2^2)
$$

$$
\boxed{q = \frac{K \cdot (h_1^2 - h_2^2)}{2L}}
$$

**Step 9: Total Discharge.**

$$
Q = q \cdot W = \frac{K \cdot W \cdot (h_1^2 - h_2^2)}{2L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: In the unconfined case, the head enters as \(h^2\) (squared) because the cross-sectional area itself depends on the head — a nonlinear feature absent in the confined case.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Steady Flow in an Unconfined Aquifer with Recharge

**Step 1: Problem Setup.**

A horizontal unconfined aquifer receives uniform vertical recharge \(W\) (m/day) from precipitation. The aquifer is bounded by two parallel drains (or rivers) at \(x = 0\) and \(x = L\), separated by a horizontal impermeable base.

**Step 2: Volume Balance on a Control Slice.**

Consider a slice of width \(dx\) and width \(W\) across the flow. The inflow from the left is \(q(x) \cdot W\), the outflow to the right is \(q(x+dx) \cdot W\), and the recharge entering from above is \(W \cdot dx \cdot W\)? — correction: recharge entering is \(W_{\text{rech}} \cdot dx \cdot 1\) per unit width.

**Step 3: Continuity Equation (Steady State).**

$$
q(x+dx) \cdot W - q(x) \cdot W = W_{\text{rech}} \cdot dx \cdot W
$$

Per unit width:

$$
\frac{dq}{dx} = W_{\text{rech}}
$$

**Step 4: Substitute Dupuit's Discharge.**

$$
q = -K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dx}
$$

So:

$$
\frac{d}{dx}\left(-K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dx}\right) = W_{\text{rech}}
$$

**Step 5: Write in Equivalent Form.**

$$
-K \cdot \frac{d}{dx}\left(h \cdot \frac{dh}{dx}\right) = W_{\text{rech}}
$$

Using \(\frac{d}{dx}(h^2) = 2h \frac{dh}{dx}\), this becomes:

$$
-\frac{K}{2} \cdot \frac{d^2(h^2)}{dx^2} = W_{\text{rech}}
$$

Let \(u = h^2\):

$$
-\frac{K}{2} \cdot \frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} = W_{\text{rech}}
$$

**Step 6: Solve the ODE.**

$$
\frac{d^2 u}{dx^2} = -\frac{2 W_{\text{rech}}}{K}
$$

First integration:

$$
\frac{du}{dx} = -\frac{2 W_{\text{rech}}}{K} \cdot x + C_1
$$

Second integration:

$$
u(x) = -\frac{W_{\text{rech}}}{K} \cdot x^2 + C_1 \cdot x + C_2
$$

Returning to \(h\):

$$
h^2(x) = -\frac{W_{\text{rech}}}{K} \cdot x^2 + C_1 \cdot x + C_2
$$

**Step 7: Apply Boundary Conditions.**

Let the heads at both drains be equal: \(h(0) = h(L) = h_0\) (symmetric case).

BC 1: \(x = 0\), \(h = h_0\):

$$
h_0^2 = C_2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_2 = h_0^2
$$

BC 2: \(x = L\), \(h = h_0\):

$$
h_0^2 = -\frac{W_{\text{rech}}}{K} \cdot L^2 + C_1 \cdot L + h_0^2
$$

$$
0 = -\frac{W_{\text{rech}} L^2}{K} + C_1 L \quad \Rightarrow \quad C_1 = \frac{W_{\text{rech}} L}{K}
$$

**Step 8: Final Head Distribution.**

$$
\boxed{h^2(x) = h_0^2 + \frac{W_{\text{rech}}}{K} \cdot (L x - x^2)}
$$

This is a **parabolic** profile with maximum at the midpoint \(x = L/2\):

$$
h_{\max}^2 = h_0^2 + \frac{W_{\text{rech}} L^2}{4K}
$$

**Step 9: Discharge into Each Drain (per unit width).**

At \(x = 0\), all recharge from the half-domain \(0 \le x \le L/2\) goes to the left drain. The discharge per unit width into the drain:

$$
q_{\text{drain}} = W_{\text{rech}} \cdot \frac{L}{2} = \frac{W_{\text{rech}} L}{2}
$$

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Steady Flow into an Infiltration Gallery

**Step 1: Problem Setup.**

A horizontal infiltration gallery of length \(L\) (perpendicular to the page) is buried at depth \(d\) below the original water table in a homogeneous, isotropic unconfined aquifer. The gallery acts as a line sink that maintains a constant head \(h_d\) at its location. The far-field (undisturbed) water table is at height \(H\) above the impermeable base, at large horizontal distance from the gallery.

**Step 2: Apply the Continuity Equation (Radial-1D Approximation).**

For a horizontal gallery treated as a line source/sink, the flow is approximately radial in the vertical cross-section. The flow per unit length of gallery through a cylindrical surface of radius \(r\) centered on the gallery:

$$
q = K \cdot i \cdot A
$$

Where \(A = h(r) \cdot 1\) (height of saturated zone at radius \(r\), times unit length of gallery) and \(i = \frac{dh}{dr}\).

Thus:

$$
q = K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}
$$

**Step 3: Steady-State, Radial Continuity.**

$$
K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dr} = q = \text{constant}
$$

**Step 4: Separate and Integrate.**

$$
K \cdot h \, dh = q \, dr
$$

Integrating from gallery (radius \(r_d\), head \(h_d\)) to far field (radius \(R\), head \(H\)):

$$
K \int_{h_d}^{H} h \, dh = q \int_{r_d}^{R} dr
$$

**Step 5: Evaluate Integrals.**

$$
K \cdot \left[\frac{h^2}{2}\right]_{h_d}^{H} = q \cdot (R - r_d)
$$

$$
\frac{K}{2}(H^2 - h_d^2) = q \cdot (R - r_d)
$$

**Step 6: Solve for the Discharge per Unit Length of Gallery.**

$$
\boxed{q = \frac{K \cdot (H^2 - h_d^2)}{2(R - r_d)}}
$$

**Step 7: Special Case — Far-Field Distance.**

In the limit \(R \to \infty\), we cannot use the formula directly; instead, a more rigorous derivation using the Dupuit-Forchheimer assumption and logarithmic potential gives:

$$
q = \frac{\pi \cdot K \cdot (H^2 - h_d^2)}{2 \ln(R/r_d)}
$$

This is the **gallery discharge equation** commonly used in KTU examinations.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix (For Exam Problem Selection)

| Condition | Equation to Use | Key Parameter |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| Confined + No Recharge | $Q = \dfrac{T \cdot W (H_1-H_2)}{L}$ | Transmissivity $T = Kb$ |
| Unconfined + No Recharge | $Q = \dfrac{KW(h_1^2 - h_2^2)}{2L}$ | Saturated thickness $h$ |
| Confined + Recharge | $h = h_0 + \dfrac{W}{2Kb}(Lx - x^2)$ | Recharge $W$ |
| Unconfined + Recharge | $h^2 = h_0^2 + \dfrac{W}{K}(Lx - x^2)$ | Recharge $W$, squared head |
| Infiltration Gallery | $q = \dfrac{\pi K (H^2 - h_d^2)}{2 \ln(R/r_d)}$ | Radial geometry |

## 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### PART A: SHORT ANSWER QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**State and explain Darcy's Law. Define hydraulic conductivity and mention its typical range for different geological formations.**

**Model Answer:**

Darcy's Law, formulated by Henry Darcy in 1856, states that the volumetric flow rate of water through a saturated porous medium is proportional to the hydraulic gradient and the cross-sectional area of flow. Mathematically:

$$
Q = -K \cdot A \cdot \frac{dh}{dL}
$$

The negative sign indicates flow in the direction of decreasing hydraulic head. The constant of proportionality \(K\) is called the **hydraulic conductivity**, defined as the discharge per unit area per unit hydraulic gradient, with units of m/s or m/day. It depends on both the porous medium (grain size, porosity, packing) and the fluid (viscosity, density).

Typical ranges of \(K\):
- **Gravel**: \(10^{-1}\) to \(10^{-2}\) m/s
- **Coarse sand**: \(10^{-3}\) to \(10^{-4}\) m/s
- **Fine sand**: \(10^{-5}\) to \(10^{-6}\) m/s
- **Silt**: \(10^{-7}\) to \(10^{-9}\) m/s
- **Clay**: \(10^{-10}\) to \(10^{-12}\) m/s

> [!NOTE]
> **Validity Range**: Darcy's Law is valid for **laminar flow** (Reynolds number Re < 1 to 10). For coarse gravels with high gradients, deviations occur.

**Marks Distribution:** Definition (1), Equation (1), Typical range (1) = **3 marks**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between confined and unconfined aquifers with respect to steady flow analysis. State the governing equations used for computing discharge in each case.**

**Model Answer:**

In a **confined aquifer**, the upper boundary is an impervious confining layer, and the saturated thickness \(b\) remains constant regardless of the head. The flow is governed by **transmissivity** \(T = Kb\), and the discharge per unit width is:

$$
q = T \cdot \frac{H_1 - H_2}{L} = \frac{Kb(H_1 - H_2)}{L}
$$

The head distribution is **linear** with distance.

In an **unconfined aquifer**, the upper boundary is the water table (phreatic surface), and the saturated thickness equals the head \(h\) measured from the impermeable base. The flow equation, using the Dupuit assumption, is:

$$
q = \frac{K(h_1^2 - h_2^2)}{2L}
$$

The head distribution gives a \(h^2\) linear variation. The unconfined equation is **nonlinear** (in \(h\)) because the flow area depends on the head itself.

**Marks Distribution:** Confined definition + equation (1.5), Unconfined definition + equation (1.5) = **3 marks**

---

### PART B: LONG ANSWER QUESTIONS (14 MARKS)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) Derive the steady-state discharge equation for flow through a homogeneous confined aquifer between two fully penetrating rivers. (7 marks)**

**(b) An unconfined aquifer receives uniform recharge at rate \(W = 0.005\) m/day. Two parallel drains are installed 200 m apart. The hydraulic conductivity is \(K = 12\) m/day. The drain depth above the impermeable base is \(h_0 = 4\) m. Compute (i) the maximum water table height above the base, (ii) the discharge collected by each drain per unit length, and (iii) the location of the water table divide. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

**Step 1:** Consider a confined aquifer of uniform thickness \(b\), extending horizontally over a length \(L\) between two fully penetrating rivers. Let the head at river 1 (\(x = 0\)) be \(H_1\) and at river 2 (\(x = L\)) be \(H_2\), with \(H_1 > H_2\).

**Step 2:** Under Dupuit's assumption and steady state with no recharge, the Laplace equation reduces to:

$$
\frac{d^2 h}{dx^2} = 0
$$

**Step 3:** Integrating twice:

$$
h(x) = C_1 x + C_2
$$

**Step 4:** Applying BCs: \(h(0) = H_1 \Rightarrow C_2 = H_1\); \(h(L) = H_2 \Rightarrow C_1 = (H_2 - H_1)/L\).

**Step 5:** Hence:

$$
h(x) = H_1 + \frac{(H_2 - H_1)x}{L}
$$

**Step 6:** The hydraulic gradient: \(i = \frac{H_1 - H_2}{L}\)

**Step 7:** Using Darcy's Law through the cross-section \(A = b \cdot W\):

$$
Q = K \cdot b \cdot W \cdot i = \frac{K b W (H_1 - H_2)}{L}
$$

Equivalently, with \(T = Kb\):

$$
\boxed{Q = \frac{T W (H_1 - H_2)}{L}}
$$

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

**Given:** \(W = 0.005\) m/day, \(L = 200\) m, \(K = 12\) m/day, \(h_0 = 4\) m, unconfined aquifer.

**Step 1: Governing equation for unconfined aquifer with recharge:**

$$
h^2(x) = h_0^2 + \frac{W}{K}(Lx - x^2)
$$

**Step 2: Maximum head occurs at the midpoint \(x = L/2 = 100\) m** (by symmetry).

$$
h_{\max}^2 = h_0^2 + \frac{W}{K}\left(L \cdot \frac{L}{2} - \left(\frac{L}{2}\right)^2\right) = h_0^2 + \frac{W L^2}{4K}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute values:**

$$
h_{\max}^2 = 4^2 + \frac{0.005 \times 200^2}{4 \times 12} = 16 + \frac{0.005 \times 40000}{48} = 16 + \frac{200}{48} = 16 + 4.1667
$$

$$
h_{\max}^2 = 20.1667 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
h_{\max} = \sqrt{20.1667} = 4.491 \text{ m}
$$

**(i) Maximum water table height above the base = 4.491 m**

**Step 4: Discharge to each drain per unit length:**

Each drain collects the recharge from the half-domain of length \(L/2 = 100\) m:

$$
q_{\text{drain}} = W \cdot \frac{L}{2} = 0.005 \times 100 = 0.5 \text{ m}^2/\text{day per unit width}
$$

**(ii) Discharge per unit length per drain = 0.5 m²/day**

**Step 5: Water table divide location:**

The water table divide is the location of maximum head, which by symmetry is at the midpoint:

$$
\boxed{x_{\text{divide}} = L/2 = 100 \text{ m from each drain}}
$$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 MARKS) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

**(a) Explain the concept of an infiltration gallery. Derive an expression for the discharge per unit length collected by a horizontal infiltration gallery in a homogeneous unconfined aquifer under steady-state conditions. (7 marks)**

**(b) A horizontal infiltration gallery of length 50 m is installed 5 m below the ground surface in a homogeneous unconfined aquifer of \(K = 8\) m/day. The original water table is 3 m below the ground surface, and the gallery maintains a head of 1 m above the impermeable base. The aquifer thickness above the impermeable base is 10 m. The radius of influence is 40 m. Calculate the discharge collected by the gallery per day. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**Concept (2 marks):** An infiltration gallery is a horizontal perforated conduit installed at a shallow depth below the water table to collect groundwater through its sides and top, primarily used for water supply in alluvial formations and for dewatering.

**Derivation (5 marks):**

**Step 1:** Model the gallery as a horizontal line sink of infinite length, drawing water radially from a homogeneous, isotropic unconfined aquifer.

**Step 2:** Apply the Dupuit-Forchheimer radial flow assumption. At a radial distance \(r\) from the gallery axis, the saturated thickness of the aquifer is \(h(r)\).

**Step 3:** Darcy's Law for radial flow through a cylindrical surface of radius \(r\) and unit length:

$$
q = K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dr}
$$

**Step 4:** Under steady state, \(q\) = constant (continuity).

**Step 5:** Separate variables and integrate:

$$
K \cdot h \, dh = q \, dr
$$

$$
\int_{h_d}^{H} K h \, dh = \int_{r_d}^{R} q \, dr
$$

$$
\frac{K}{2}(H^2 - h_d^2) = q(R - r_d)
$$

**Step 6:** For a more rigorous analysis accounting for the radial geometry and Dupuit's logarithmic potential, the discharge per unit length is:

$$
\boxed{q = \frac{\pi K (H^2 - h_d^2)}{2 \ln(R/r_d)}}
$$

where \(H\) is the far-field head, \(h_d\) is the head at the gallery, \(R\) is the radius of influence, and \(r_d\) is the effective radius of the gallery.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Given:**
- Gallery length \(L = 50\) m
- Gallery depth below ground = 5 m; ground surface is at the top of the aquifer
- Original water table at 3 m below ground → water table at depth 3 m
- Gallery maintains head \(h_d = 1\) m above impermeable base
- Aquifer total thickness (above base) = 10 m
- Far-field head \(H = 10 - 3 = 7\) m above the base
- \(K = 8\) m/day
- Radius of influence \(R = 40\) m
- Effective gallery radius \(r_d = 0.5\) m (assume for perforated pipe)

**Step 1: Verify the heads.**

- \(H = 10 - 3 = 7\) m (original saturated water table height above base)
- \(h_d = 1\) m (head at the gallery)

**Step 2: Apply the gallery discharge equation:**

$$
q = \frac{\pi K (H^2 - h_d^2)}{2 \ln(R/r_d)}
$$

**Step 3: Compute numerator:**

$$
H^2 - h_d^2 = 49 - 1 = 48 \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
\pi K (H^2 - h_d^2) = \pi \times 8 \times 48 = 1206.37 \text{ m}^3/\text{day·m}
$$

**Step 4: Compute denominator:**

$$
\ln(R/r_d) = \ln(40/0.5) = \ln(80) = 4.382
$$

**Step 5: Discharge per unit length:**

$$
q = \frac{1206.37}{2 \times 4.382} = \frac{1206.37}{8.764} = 137.65 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}
$$

**Step 6: Total discharge from the gallery (length 50 m):**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = q \cdot L = 137.65 \times 50 = 6882.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q_{\text{total}} \approx 6883 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} \approx 6.88 \text{ ML/day}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION PITFALLS** — *Where Students Commonly Lose Marks*:
>
> 1. **Confined vs. Unconfined Mix-up (–2 to –3 marks)**: Students often apply the confined equation (\(T = Kb\)) to an unconfined aquifer problem or use \(h^2\) forms in a confined case. **Always check the aquifer type first.**
> 2. **Sign Convention Errors (–1 mark)**: Forgetting the negative sign in Darcy's Law or reversing the head difference. Use \((H_1 - H_2)\) consistently with \(H_1 > H_2\).
> 3. **Unit Inconsistency (–1 to –2 marks)**: Mixing m/day with cm/s or km². **Standardize** all units at the start. KTU examiners deduct heavily for missing unit conversions.
> 4. **Forgetting to Square in Unconfined Case (–2 marks)**: The \(h^2 - h_0^2\) form is non-negotiable. Writing \(h - h_0\) in a parabolic profile problem is a direct loss.
> 5. **Omitting the "per unit length" or "per unit width" specification (–1 mark)**: Always state whether your discharge is \(Q\) (total), \(q\) (per unit width), or per unit length of gallery.
> 6. **Not Stating Dupuit's Assumptions (–1 mark)**: For derivations in Part B, explicitly mention that flow is horizontal, the hydraulic gradient equals the water table slope, and the velocity is uniform over depth.
> 7. **No Final Answer Box (–0.5 to –1 mark)**: Always box the final numerical answer with proper units.

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **ESSENTIAL TAKEAWAYS — MEMORIZE BEFORE EXAM**

- ✅ **Darcy's Law**: \(Q = -KA(dh/dL)\); valid for **laminar flow** in saturated porous media.
- ✅ **Three Hydraulic Properties**: \(K\) (m/s), \(T = Kb\) (m²/s), \(S = S_s b\) (dimensionless).
- ✅ **Steady 1D Assumption**: \(\partial h/\partial t = 0\); Laplace equation reduces to 1D ODE.
- ✅ **Confined vs. Unconfined**:
  - Confined → linear in \(h\), use \(T\)
  - Unconfined → linear in \(h^2\), use \(K\)
- ✅ **Recharge Effect**: Transforms the head profile from **linear → parabolic** (unconfined: \(h^2\) parabolic; confined: \(h\) parabolic).
- ✅ **Drain Discharge with Recharge**: Each of two symmetric drains collects \(WL/2\) per unit width.
- ✅ **Infiltration Gallery**: Radial-1D flow; discharge per unit length \(q = \pi K(H^2 - h_d^2) / [2 \ln(R/r_d)]\).
- ✅ **Water Table Divide**: Located at the **midpoint** \(x = L/2\) for symmetric recharge between two drains.
- ✅ **Dupuit's Assumptions**: Horizontal flow, uniform velocity with depth, hydraulic gradient ≈ water table slope.
- ✅ **Validity of Darcy's Law**: Reynolds number Re < 1–10 (laminar regime only).
- ✅ **Typical Units to Use**: \(K\) in m/day, \(T\) in m²/day, \(Q\) in m³/day, \(W\) in m/day or mm/day.
- ✅ **Real-World Applications**: Tile drainage, dewatering, water supply galleries, landfill leachate control, salinity management.

> - **Topic:** Evaluation of Aquifer Parameters by Theis Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Theis Method (1935)** is a graphical curve-matching procedure used to determine the **hydraulic properties of a confined aquifer** — specifically the **Transmissivity ($T$)** and the **Storage Coefficient / Storativity ($S$)** — by analyzing the time-drawdown data recorded during a pumping test, based on the analogy between groundwater flow and heat conduction (Theis, 1935).

The method assumes an **ideal confined aquifer** that is:
- Homogeneous, isotropic, and of infinite areal extent
- Fully penetrating (the well screen covers the entire aquifer thickness)
- Leaky-free (no vertical leakage from adjacent strata)
- Discharge is at a constant rate ($Q$ = constant)
- The well diameter is infinitesimally small
- Water is released instantaneously from storage as the head declines (elastic storage)

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The Pebble-in-Pond Concept

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine dropping a pebble in a still pond. The water surface near the pebble dips down (drawdown), and that depression spreads outward as time passes. The shape of the depression depends on how easily water can flow horizontally (Transmissivity, $T$) and how much water the pond "releases" from below to refill the dip (Storativity, $S$).

In a confined aquifer, when a well is pumped:
- Water is drawn from storage by the **decompression of water** and the **compaction of the aquifer skeleton**.
- A **cone of depression** forms around the well and expands outward with time.
- The Theis method mathematically replicates this expanding cone using the **Theis Equation**, which links drawdown $s$ at distance $r$ and time $t$ to $T$ and $S$.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> - **Transmissivity ($T$):** Expressed in **m²/day** (or **m²/s**). Indicates the ease with which water is transmitted through the aquifer.
> - **Storativity ($S$):** A **dimensionless** quantity. For confined aquifers, typical range is **$5 \times 10^{-5}$ to $5 \times 10^{-3}$**.
> - **Euler's Constant ($\gamma \approx 0.5772$):** Appears in the Theis well-function expansion.
> - **Standard Pumping Rate ($Q$):** Measured in **m³/day** or **litres per minute (LPM)**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Why Theis Works

Theis borrowed the heat-conduction equation from **Carlslaw & Jaeger (1959)** and applied it to radial groundwater flow. The reasoning chain is:

1. **Darcy's Law** governs radial flow towards a well:
   $$Q = 2\pi r T \frac{\partial s}{\partial r}$$
2. **Continuity Equation** (mass balance) for an annular ring of aquifer:
   $$\frac{\partial s}{\partial t} = \frac{T}{S} \left( \frac{\partial^2 s}{\partial r^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial s}{\partial r} \right)$$
3. **Boundary Conditions:**
   - $s = 0$ when $t = 0$ (initial head undisturbed)
   - $s \to 0$ as $r \to \infty$ (no effect at infinity)
   - $Q$ is constant for $t > 0$
4. Solving this PDE yields the **Theis Equation** (a closed-form solution in terms of an exponential integral).

### 2.2 The Cooper-Jacob Link (Why It Works at Large $t$)

When $u < 0.01$ (i.e., for large $t$ or small $r$), the series form of $W(u)$ simplifies, and the drawdown plot becomes a straight line on a semi-log plot. This is the **Cooper-Jacob approximation**, derived directly from Theis. The Theis curve is therefore the **parent** of all later methods.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Symbol | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $s$ | Drawdown at observation well | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u)$ | **m** |
| $Q$ | Constant pumping rate | Given (field input) | **m³/day** |
| $T$ | Transmissivity | $T = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi s} W(u)$ | **m²/day** |
| $S$ | Storativity (Storage Coeff.) | $S = \dfrac{4 T t}{r^2} \cdot u$ | **dimensionless** |
| $u$ | Dimensionless argument | $u = \dfrac{r^2 S}{4 T t}$ | **dimensionless** |
| $W(u)$ | Theis Well Function | $\displaystyle W(u) = \int_u^\infty \dfrac{e^{-y}}{y}\, dy$ | **dimensionless** |
| $W(u)$ series | Exponential Integral series | $W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln(u) + u - \dfrac{u^2}{4} + \dfrac{u^3}{18} - \cdots$ | **dimensionless** |
| $r$ | Radial distance (well to obs.) | Field input | **m** |
| $t$ | Time since pumping began | Field input | **day** or **s** |
| Match-point $1/u$ | Used in curve matching | Read from type curve | dimensionless |
| Match-point $W(u)$ | Used in curve matching | Read from type curve | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** The Theis method is the **foundation** for all modern pumping-test interpretations. It is used to design well fields, predict regional drawdowns, plan dewatering systems for excavations, and assess the sustainable yield of aquifers for municipal and agricultural supply.

### 2.4 Assumptions — When Theis Will Fail

> [!WARNING]
> The Theis solution is **invalid** for:
> - Unconfined aquifers (unless using **Neuman's** method for delayed yield)
> - Leaky confined aquifers (use **Hantush-Jacob**)
> - Aquifers with boundaries (recharge or barrier) — use **image-well theory**
> - Partial penetration wells (use **Hantush's** partial-penetration correction)
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Theis Well Function Expansion

Starting from the integral form of the exponential integral (Theis Well Function):

$$W(u) = \int_u^\infty \frac{e^{-y}}{y}\, dy$$

**Step 1 — Substitution for the upper limit:**
Introduce a parameter by writing the exponential series. Substitute $e^{-y}$ by its Maclaurin series:

$$e^{-y} = 1 - y + \frac{y^2}{2!} - \frac{y^3}{3!} + \frac{y^4}{4!} - \cdots$$

**Step 2 — Divide term-by-term by $y$:**

$$\frac{e^{-y}}{y} = \frac{1}{y} - 1 + \frac{y}{2!} - \frac{y^2}{3!} + \frac{y^3}{4!} - \cdots$$

**Step 3 — Integrate term-by-term from $u$ to $\infty$:**

$$W(u) = \int_u^\infty \frac{1}{y}\, dy - \int_u^\infty 1\, dy + \int_u^\infty \frac{y}{2!}\, dy - \int_u^\infty \frac{y^2}{3!}\, dy + \cdots$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate each integral:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W(u) &= \Big[ \ln y \Big]_u^\infty \;-\; \Big[ y \Big]_u^\infty \;+\; \Big[ \dfrac{y^2}{2 \cdot 2!} \Big]_u^\infty \;-\; \Big[ \dfrac{y^3}{3 \cdot 3!} \Big]_u^\infty \;+\; \cdots \\
&= \lim_{y \to \infty}(\ln y) - \ln u \;-\; \lim_{y \to \infty}(y) + u \;+\; \lim_{y \to \infty}\!\left(\dfrac{y^2}{4}\right) - \dfrac{u^2}{4} \;+\; \cdots
\end{aligned}
$$

> The limits at $\infty$ are divergent, so Theis **regularised** them by subtracting the divergent series and absorbing the constant into **Euler's constant** $\gamma \approx 0.5772$.

**Step 5 — Final regularised series:**

$$\boxed{\,W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln(u) + u - \frac{u^2}{4} + \frac{u^3}{18} - \frac{u^4}{96} + \cdots\,}$$

For small $u$ (large $t$), the higher-order terms vanish and we recover **Cooper-Jacob**:
$$W(u) \approx -0.5772 - \ln(u) = 2.303 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.25 T t}{r^2 S}\right)$$

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Transmissivity and Storativity from a Match Point

When a match is found between the field data curve $(s$ vs $t)$ and the Theis type curve $(W(u)$ vs $1/u)$, an **arbitrary match point** is selected. Let the matched coordinates be:

- From type curve: $W(u)_\text{m}$ and $(1/u)_\text{m}$
- From field curve: $s_\text{m}$ and $t_\text{m}$

**Step 1 — Solve Theis Equation for $T$:**

$$T = \frac{Q \cdot W(u)_\text{m}}{4\pi \cdot s_\text{m}}$$

**Step 2 — Express $u$ at the match point** using $1/u$:

$$u_\text{m} = \frac{1}{(1/u)_\text{m}}$$

**Step 3 — Solve the $u$-definition for $S$:**

$$S = \frac{4 T t_\text{m} \cdot u_\text{m}}{r^2}$$

Combining Steps 1–3 yields a **direct $S$ formula** that does not require explicit $T$:

$$S = \frac{4 T t_\text{m}}{r^2} \cdot \frac{1}{(1/u)_\text{m}} = \frac{Q \, t_\text{m}}{4\pi r^2} \cdot \frac{W(u)_\text{m}}{s_\text{m}} \cdot \frac{1}{(1/u)_\text{m}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In practice, engineers always use the **two separate formulas** for $T$ and $S$ to avoid compounding arithmetic errors.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Board-Standard Style)

> **Problem:** A confined aquifer is pumped at $Q = 2500 \,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$. At an observation well located $r = 100 \,\text{m}$ from the pumping well, the following data were obtained:

| $t$ (min) | $s$ (m) |
| :---: | :---: |
| 1 | 0.10 |
| 5 | 0.45 |
| 10 | 0.70 |
| 30 | 1.20 |
| 60 | 1.65 |
| 120 | 2.10 |
| 300 | 2.55 |
| 600 | 2.85 |

**Solution outline (full KTU-type curve procedure):**

**Step 1 — Plot field data:**
Draw $s$ (m) vs $t$ (min) on **transparent log-log paper** (cycle size $6.28 \,\text{cm} \times 3 \,\text{cycles/decade}$). 

**Step 2 — Overlay the Theis Type Curve:**
The Theis Type Curve is a pre-drawn plot of $W(u)$ vs $1/u$ on log-log paper, with the characteristic **S-shape** where it flattens for large $1/u$ (small $u$).

**Step 3 — Match the curves:**
Keeping axes parallel, slide the field-data curve over the type curve until the two coincide as closely as possible. The match is good if all field points lie on the type curve.

**Step 4 — Read the match point** (chosen conveniently near the centre of the overlapping region):

$$
W(u)_\text{m} = 1.0, \quad \frac{1}{u_\text{m}} = 10, \quad s_\text{m} = 1.0 \,\text{m}, \quad t_\text{m} = 10 \,\text{min} = 0.00694 \,\text{day}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $T$:**

$$T = \frac{Q \cdot W(u)_\text{m}}{4\pi \cdot s_\text{m}} = \frac{2500 \times 1.0}{4\pi \times 1.0}$$

$$T = \frac{2500}{12.566} = 198.94 \,\text{m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Step 6 — Compute $S$:**

$$u_\text{m} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1$$

$$S = \frac{4 T t_\text{m} u_\text{m}}{r^2} = \frac{4 \times 198.94 \times 0.00694 \times 0.1}{100^2}$$

$$S = \frac{0.552}{10000} = 5.52 \times 10^{-5}$$

> **Final Answer:** $T \approx 199 \,\text{m}^2/\text{day}$, $S \approx 5.5 \times 10^{-5}$ (typical of a confined aquifer).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Theis Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Pumping Test Data Available Q, r, t, s] --> B[Step A Plot field data s vs t on transparent log log paper]
    B --> C[Step B Obtain Theis Type Curve W u vs 1 by u]
    C --> D[Step C Superimpose field curve on type curve keeping axes parallel]
    D --> E[Step D Slide until best visual match is obtained]
    E --> F[Step E Select an arbitrary match point]
    F --> G1[Read W u at match from type curve]
    F --> G2[Read 1 by u at match from type curve]
    F --> G3[Read s at match from field curve]
    F --> G4[Read t at match from field curve]
    G1 --> H[Step F Compute Transmissivity T]
    G2 --> H
    G3 --> H
    G4 --> I[Step G Compute Storativity S]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Step H Tabulate final T and S values with units]
    J --> K[Step I Report with applicable assumptions stated]
    K --> L[End: Aquifer parameters evaluated]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagram Instead of Plot?**
> The Theis method relies on a hand-drawn log-log curve match. Because a Mermaid diagram cannot faithfully reproduce a logarithmic axis, this **block-level functional flow** is provided instead to capture the **logical sequence** of operations that a student must perform on paper during the examination.

### 4.2 Schematic of the Field Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Confined Aquifer Cross Section] --> A1[Top Aquiclude Impermeable Cap]
    A --> A2[Aquifer Thickness b with Transmissivity T]
    A --> A3[Bottom Aquiclude Impermeable Base]
    A2 --> B[Pumping Well Q constant]
    B --> C[Observation Well r away]
    B --> D[Initial Piezometric Surface]
    B --> E[Drawdown Cone of Depression]
    C --> F[s measured versus t at distance r]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Field Data s versus t]
    G --> H[Theis Type Curve W u versus 1 by u]
    H --> I[Match Point Selected]
    I --> J[Compute T and S]
```

### 4.3 The S-Shape of the Theis Type Curve — Why It Matters

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Small 1 by u Large t Small u] --> B[W u very large Drawdown rising]
    B --> C[Intermediate 1 by u Mid range]
    C --> D[Asymptotic flattening near 1 by u equals 10]
    D --> E[Large 1 by u Small t W u approaches 0]
    E --> F[Curve resembles mirror S shape on log log plot]
    F --> G[Field data curve plotted at same log scale is matched here]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1.** *State the assumptions of the Theis method for evaluation of aquifer parameters.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The aquifer is **confined, homogeneous, isotropic, and of infinite lateral extent** (1 mark).
2. The pumping well is **fully penetrating** and of **infinitesimally small diameter** (1 mark).
3. Pumping rate $Q$ is **constant**, and water is released **instantaneously from elastic storage** as the head declines (1 mark).

---

**Q2.** *What is the Theis well function $W(u)$? Write its series expansion.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$W(u)$ is the **exponential integral** representing drawdown response (definition – 1 mark). Its series expansion is:

$$W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln u + u - \frac{u^2}{2 \cdot 2!} + \frac{u^3}{3 \cdot 3!} - \frac{u^4}{4 \cdot 4!} + \cdots \quad (1 \text{ mark})$$

The value $-0.5772$ is **Euler's constant** $\gamma$ (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Style Model Paper]**

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Derive the Theis equation for drawdown in a confined aquifer pumped at a constant rate $Q$. Clearly list the assumptions and define each term. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A pumping test in a confined aquifer yields the following match-point values when superimposed on the Theis type curve: $W(u)_\text{m} = 2.0$, $1/u_\text{m} = 100$, $s_\text{m} = 0.50 \,\text{m}$, $t_\text{m} = 60 \,\text{min}$, with $r = 200 \,\text{m}$ and $Q = 3000 \,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$. Determine the transmissivity and storativity. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
1. Darcy's law for radial flow gives $Q = 2\pi r T \dfrac{\partial s}{\partial r}$ (1 mark).
2. Continuity yields $\dfrac{\partial s}{\partial t} = \dfrac{T}{S}\!\left(\dfrac{\partial^2 s}{\partial r^2} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial s}{\partial r}\right)$ (1 mark).
3. Boundary conditions: $s(r,0)=0$, $s(\infty, t)=0$, $\lim_{r\to 0}\!\left(r\dfrac{\partial s}{\partial r}\right) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T}$ (1 mark).
4. By analogy with the heat-conduction solution of Carlslaw and Jaeger, the drawdown is $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u)$ with $W(u) = \int_u^\infty \dfrac{e^{-y}}{y}\,dy$ and $u = \dfrac{r^2 S}{4Tt}$ (2 marks).
5. Assumptions (1 mark): confined, homogeneous, isotropic, infinite extent, full penetration, constant $Q$, instantaneous release.
6. Definition of terms (1 mark).

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

Convert $t_\text{m}$ to days: $t_\text{m} = 60 \,\text{min} = 1/24 \,\text{day} = 0.0417 \,\text{day}$ (1 mark).

**Transmissivity** (from $T = \dfrac{Q W(u)_\text{m}}{4\pi s_\text{m}}$):

$$T = \frac{3000 \times 2.0}{4\pi \times 0.50} = \frac{6000}{6.2832} = 954.93 \,\text{m}^2/\text{day} \quad (3 \text{ marks})$$

**Storativity** (from $S = \dfrac{4 T t_\text{m} u_\text{m}}{r^2}$ with $u_\text{m} = 1/100 = 0.01$):

$$S = \frac{4 \times 954.93 \times 0.0417 \times 0.01}{200^2} = \frac{1.5926}{40000}$$

$$S = 3.98 \times 10^{-5} \quad (3 \text{ marks})$$

> **Final:** $T \approx 955 \,\text{m}^2/\text{day}$, $S \approx 4.0 \times 10^{-5}$ — characteristic of a confined aquifer.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**(a)** Explain the **type-curve matching procedure** of the Theis method with the help of a stepwise procedure. What are the advantages and limitations of this method? **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A confined aquifer of thickness 30 m is pumped at $Q = 2000 \,\text{m}^3/\text{day}$. An observation well at $r = 50 \,\text{m}$ shows a steady-state drawdown of 2.5 m. Using the Theis method (assume $W(u) = 1$ as a first approximation when $u$ is small), estimate the **hydraulic conductivity $K$** and the storativity $S$. **\[7 marks\]**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Stepwise procedure (4 marks):**
1. Plot $s$ vs $t$ on log-log tracing paper.
2. Overlay on the master Theis type curve of $W(u)$ vs $1/u$ (log-log).
3. Keep axes parallel; slide for best fit.
4. Mark an arbitrary match point and read the four coordinates.
5. Compute $T$ and $S$ using the match-point values.

**Advantages** (1.5 marks): applicable at **all** times (no restriction to late time), graphical, valid for short pumping tests.

**Limitations** (1.5 marks): requires accurate plot, no partial-penetration correction, not valid for unconfined/leaky aquifers.

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

First-approximation approach: $W(u) \approx 1.0$, so:

$$T = \frac{Q \cdot W(u)}{4\pi s} = \frac{2000 \times 1.0}{4\pi \times 2.5} = \frac{2000}{31.416} = 63.66 \,\text{m}^2/\text{day} \quad (2 \text{ marks})$$

**Hydraulic conductivity** (1 mark):

$$K = \frac{T}{b} = \frac{63.66}{30} = 2.12 \,\text{m}/\text{day}$$

For storativity, use the standard reference relationship: for a confined aquifer, when $W(u) = 1$, $u \approx 0.5$. Substituting:

$$S = \frac{4 T t u}{r^2} = \frac{4 \times 63.66 \times t \times 0.5}{50^2} = 0.0509 \, t$$

> Without an explicit time value (the problem says "steady-state"), interpret $t$ as the **time of observation** at drawdown 2.5 m. A common assumption in such simplified problems is $u = 0.5$, leading to $S \approx 5 \times 10^{-2}$ (4 marks) for a generic late-time $t$ of about 1 day.
> If the problem statement explicitly gives a time (e.g., $t = 12$ hours = 0.5 day), then $S = 0.0255$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $t$:** Convert minutes → days **before** computing $S$. Mixing units is the #1 cause of lost marks.
> 2. **Sign error in $W(u)$ series:** Remember that the constant is **$-0.5772$**, not $+0.5772$.
> 3. **Misreading match point:** $1/u$ is plotted on the **x-axis** of the type curve; $W(u)$ on the y-axis. Do not swap.
> 4. **Forgetting $\pi$ in $4\pi$:** A classic 1-mark error in the $T$ formula.
> 5. **No statement of assumptions:** Always list them — KTU awards 1 mark exclusively for assumptions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU Exam:**

- [x] Theis method evaluates **Transmissivity $T$** and **Storativity $S$** of a **confined** aquifer.
- [x] The governing equation is $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u)$ with $u = \dfrac{r^2 S}{4 T t}$.
- [x] $W(u)$ is the **exponential integral**; series begins with $-0.5772 - \ln u + \cdots$
- [x] **Procedure:** log-log plot of $s$ vs $t$ matched to log-log master curve of $W(u)$ vs $1/u$.
- [x] At the match point: $T = \dfrac{Q W(u)_\text{m}}{4\pi s_\text{m}}$ and $S = \dfrac{4 T t_\text{m} u_\text{m}}{r^2}$.
- [x] **Assumptions:** confined, homogeneous, isotropic, infinite, full penetration, constant $Q$, elastic storage, infinitesimally thin well.
- [x] **Cooper-Jacob** is the **late-time limit** of Theis (when $u < 0.01$).
- [x] Typical $S$ for confined aquifers lies in the range $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- [x] Typical $T$ for confined aquifers is $10$ to $10^4 \,\text{m}^2/\text{day}$.
- [x] For **unconfined** aquifers, use **Neuman's** method; for **leaky confined**, use **Hantush-Jacob**.
- [x] **Always** state the assumptions and **convert units** before substituting.
- [x] Euler's constant $\gamma = 0.5772$ must be remembered for the series expansion.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Jacob's and Chow's Method (Problems from Evaluation of Aquifer Parameters)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Jacob's and Chow's Method (Problems from Evaluation of Aquifer Parameters)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

**Jacob's Method** (also called the *Cooper-Jacob straight-line method* or *Theis-Jacob approximation*) is a simplified analytical procedure derived from the Theis equation for evaluating aquifer parameters — **Transmissivity (T)** and **Storativity (S)** — from a single-well pumping test. It applies when the dimensionless time factor $u = \dfrac{r^2 S}{4 T t}$ is sufficiently small (typically $u \leq 0.01$ or $u \leq 0.05$), which corresponds to large values of pumping time $t$ or small radial distances $r$ from the well.

**Chow's Method** (formally, the *Chow Time-Drawdown Graphical Method*, 1952) is another modification of the Theis equation. Instead of assuming $u$ to be small, Chow introduced a dimensionless function $F(u) = W(u) \cdot e^{u}$, and his method is valid for **all values of $u$**, including the early-time data where the Jacob approximation fails.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** Module 1 of PECET751 explicitly lists *"Evaluation of aquifer parameters by Jacob's and Chow's method"* as a core topic. Both methods are numerical/graphical analytical techniques used to compute **T** and **S** from time-drawdown field data obtained during constant-rate pumping tests in a **confined aquifer**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **circular swimming pool** with a giant floating sponge at the bottom. The moment you start pumping water out of the pool (analogous to pumping from a well), water begins rushing radially inward from all sides to fill the void. Now imagine plotting, second by second, **how far the water level drops** at a fixed observation point (say, 50 m from the pump). That drop-vs-time graph is what hydrologists call the **time-drawdown curve**.

**Jacob's insight** was a masterstroke of simplification: he observed that once enough time has passed, the early chaotic transient behaviour settles into a beautifully simple, almost **straight-line pattern** when plotted on a **semi-log paper** (time on log-scale, drawdown on linear scale). The slope and intercept of that straight line can be measured with a ruler, and from them — using only two simple equations — the entire hidden personality of the aquifer (its $T$ and $S$) can be revealed. It is like a doctor diagnosing a patient by simply reading the *final stable portion* of an EKG strip.

**Chow's brilliance** was different: he asked, *"What if I don't want to wait for stability? What if early-time data is all I have?"* He engineered a clever matching scheme using a special function $F(u)$ that can be read directly off the field curve using a **spreadsheet or hand ruler**, making the method universally applicable across all time ranges.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Field Units Used in KTU Examinations**
> - Transmissivity, $T$: **m²/day** (or **m²/s** in SI)
> - Storativity, $S$: **dimensionless** (typically $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-1}$)
> - Pumping rate, $Q$: **m³/day** (or **m³/s**)
> - Drawdown, $s$: **metres (m)**
> - Time, $t$: **minutes** (typically) — but must be converted to **seconds or days** to match $T$'s units
> - Radial distance, $r$: **metres (m)**
> - The exponential constant: $e \approx 2.71828$
> - Euler's constant: $\gamma \approx 0.5772$ (used inside the well function)

---

### 1.4 Pre-Requisite: The Theis Equation (Foundation)

Both Jacob's and Chow's methods are derived from the classical Theis equation (1935), valid for a fully confined, homogeneous, isotropic, infinite aquifer of uniform thickness:

$$
s = \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} \, W(u)
$$

$$
W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln(u) - \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-u)^n}{n \cdot n!}
$$

$$
u = \frac{r^{2} S}{4 T t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> $W(u)$ is the **dimensionless well function** (also called the *Theis function* $W(u)$ or $\text{Ei}(-u)$ with sign convention). It is the corner-stone of transient groundwater hydraulics. Without $W(u)$, neither Jacob nor Chow methods exist.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Cooper-Jacob Straight-Line Method (Theoretical Breakdown)

**Step 1 — Simplification Trigger:**
The Theis well function $W(u)$ is an infinite series:

$$
W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln(u) + u - \frac{u^{2}}{2 \cdot 2!} + \frac{u^{3}}{3 \cdot 3!} - \cdots
$$

When $u < 0.01$ (i.e., late time or close observation well), all terms beyond $-\ln(u)$ become negligibly small. Hence:

$$
W(u) \approx -0.5772 - \ln(u)
$$

**Step 2 — Substitution of $u$:**

$$
W(u) \approx -0.5772 - \ln\left( \frac{r^{2} S}{4 T t} \right)
$$

Using the identity $-\ln(x) = \ln(1/x)$:

$$
W(u) = \ln\left( \frac{4 T t}{r^{2} S} \right) - 0.5772 = \ln\left( \frac{2.25 \, T t}{r^{2} S} \right)
$$

because $e^{0.5772} = 1.78107 \Rightarrow 4/e^{0.5772} = 4/1.78107 \approx 2.246 \approx 2.25$.

**Step 3 — Final Jacob's Drawdown Equation:**

$$
s = \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} \ln\left( \frac{2.25 \, T t}{r^{2} S} \right)
$$

Converting to common logarithm ($\ln x = 2.303 \log x$):

$$
\boxed{s = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log\left( \frac{2.25 \, T t}{r^{2} S} \right)}
$$

**Step 4 — Geometric Interpretation (Why a Straight Line?):**
Expanding the logarithm:

$$
s = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log\left( \frac{2.25 \, T}{r^{2} S} \right) + \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log(t)
$$

This is the equation of a **straight line** of the form $s = C + m \log(t)$:

- **Slope** (per log cycle of time): $m = \dfrac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T}$
- **y-intercept (at $\log t = 0$, i.e., $t = 1$):** $C = \dfrac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log\left( \dfrac{2.25 \, T}{r^{2} S} \right)$

**Step 5 — Special Condition $s = 0$ (Jacob's Time Intercept):**
Setting $s = 0$:

$$
0 = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log\left( \frac{2.25 \, T t_{0}}{r^{2} S} \right)
$$

The argument of the log must equal **1** (since $\log(1) = 0$):

$$
\frac{2.25 \, T t_{0}}{r^{2} S} = 1
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{0} = \frac{r^{2} S}{2.25 \, T} = \frac{0.4444 \, r^{2} S}{T}}
$$

This $t_0$ is the **time intercept** — the value of $t$ where the extrapolated straight line crosses the **zero-drawdown axis** (extension of the line to $s = 0$).

---

### 2.2 Chow's Method (Theoretical Breakdown)

**Step 1 — Definition of Chow's Function $F(u)$:**

Chow (1952) defined a new function:

$$
F(u) = W(u) \cdot e^{u}
$$

**Step 2 — Substituting $W(u)$ and $u$:**
From Theis: $W(u) = \dfrac{4 \pi T s}{Q}$ and $e^{u} = e^{r^{2} S / (4 T t)}$

$$
F(u) = \frac{4 \pi T s}{Q} \cdot e^{u}
$$

**Step 3 — Key Property of $F(u)$:**
Chow showed that $F(u)$ is **independent of $r$ and $T$ in its pure mathematical form**, but for a specific field curve of $s$ vs $t$, a chosen point gives:

$$
\boxed{F(u) = \frac{s \cdot 4 \pi T}{Q} \cdot e^{u}}
$$

Equivalently, by taking the **logarithm** of the Theis equation:

$$
\log W(u) = \log\left( \frac{4 \pi T s}{Q} \right)
$$

By the logarithmic relationship between $W(u)$ and $u$ on the Theis type curve, Chow's "match-point" procedure allows $F(u)$ and $u$ to be read off a plot of the field data $s$ vs $t$ on log-log paper.

**Step 4 — The Field Procedure:**
1. Plot $s$ (Y-axis) vs $t$ (X-axis) on **log-log paper** using field data.
2. Overlay the field curve on top of the Theis type curve ($W(u)$ vs $u$).
3. After matching, select an arbitrary point $(W(u), u)$ on the type curve.
4. Read the corresponding $(s, t)$ values on the field curve at the **same point in the match**.
5. Compute $T$ and $S$.

**Step 5 — Master Equations for $T$ and $S$:**

$$
\boxed{T = \frac{Q}{4 \pi s} \cdot W(u) = \frac{Q}{4 \pi s} \cdot F(u) \cdot e^{-u}}
$$

$$
\boxed{S = \frac{4 T u t}{r^{2}}}
$$

For practical problem-solving in KTU exams, a **numerical method** is most common:

- From the matched point, $F(u) = \dfrac{4 \pi T s}{Q} \cdot e^{u}$ is computed iteratively (since $u$ depends on $T$ and $S$).
- Alternatively, use the **Chow iteration approach**: assume $e^{u} = 1$ (i.e., $u$ small), get first estimate, then refine.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Jacob's Method | Chow's Method |
|---|---|---|
| Drawdown equation | $s = \dfrac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log\left( \dfrac{2.25 T t}{r^{2} S} \right)$ | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4 \pi T} W(u)$ with $F(u) = W(u) e^{u}$ |
| Transmissivity (T) | $T = \dfrac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi \Delta s}$ | $T = \dfrac{Q \cdot F(u)}{4 \pi s}$ |
| Storativity (S) | $S = \dfrac{2.25 \, T \, t_{0}}{r^{2}}$ | $S = \dfrac{4 T u t}{r^{2}}$ |
| Validity condition | $u \leq 0.01$ (late time) | All values of $u$ (early + late time) |
| Plot type | Semi-log ($s$ vs $\log t$) | Log-log ($s$ vs $t$) overlay on type curve |
| Slope of line | $m = \dfrac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T}$ | Not a straight line (matched curve) |
| Time intercept | $t_{0} = \dfrac{r^{2} S}{2.25 T}$ | Match point $(s,t)$ gives $u$ directly |
| Special constant | $2.25 = 4 / e^{0.5772}$ | $F(u) = W(u) e^{u}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world utility:** $T$ controls how fast water moves through the aquifer (used in well design, dewatering, contaminant transport). $S$ controls how much water is released from storage per unit area per unit drop in head (critical for predicting seasonal groundwater fluctuations and seawater intrusion in coastal Kerala aquifers).

---

### 2.4 Why These Methods Matter in Engineering Practice

- **Tubewell design** in Kerala's laterite & alluvial aquifers (e.g., Thrissur, Palakkad districts)
- **Land subsidence** studies (Kochi metro region)
- **Salinity intrusion** monitoring in coastal aquifers
- **Dewatering systems** for metro construction (Kochi, Trivandrum)
- **Contaminant plume tracking** for industrial sites (Eloor, Ernakulam)

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Jacob's Straight-Line Equation

Starting from the Theis equation:

$$
s = \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} W(u)
$$

with $W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln(u) - \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{(-u)^n}{n \cdot n!}$ and $u = \dfrac{r^{2} S}{4 T t}$.

**Step A — Truncate the series for $u \ll 1$:**

$$
W(u) \approx -0.5772 - \ln(u) + u - \frac{u^{2}}{4} + \cdots
$$

For $u \leq 0.01$, the remainder $|u - \frac{u^{2}}{4} + \cdots| < 0.01$. Thus:

$$
W(u) \approx -0.5772 - \ln(u)
$$

**Step B — Substitute $u$:**

$$
W(u) \approx -0.5772 - \ln\left( \frac{r^{2} S}{4 T t} \right)
$$

**Step C — Apply logarithm rules:**

$$
W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln\left( \frac{r^{2} S}{4 T} \right) + \ln(t)
$$

$$
W(u) = \ln(t) + \ln\left( \frac{4 T}{r^{2} S} \right) - 0.5772
$$

Using the identity $\ln(a) - 0.5772 = \ln(a) - \ln(e^{0.5772}) = \ln\left( \dfrac{a}{e^{0.5772}} \right)$:

$$
W(u) = \ln(t) + \ln\left( \frac{4 T}{e^{0.5772} \, r^{2} S} \right)
$$

**Step D — Combine logarithms:**

$$
W(u) = \ln\left( \frac{4 T t}{e^{0.5772} \, r^{2} S} \right) = \ln\left( \frac{2.25 \, T t}{r^{2} S} \right)
$$

where $\dfrac{4}{e^{0.5772}} = \dfrac{4}{1.78107} = 2.2461 \approx 2.25$.

**Step E — Insert into Theis drawdown equation:**

$$
s = \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} \ln\left( \frac{2.25 \, T t}{r^{2} S} \right)
$$

**Step F — Convert to log₁₀:**

$$
\boxed{s = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log\left( \frac{2.25 \, T t}{r^{2} S} \right)}
$$

**Step G — Derive the slope-based formula for $T$:**
The slope per log cycle:

$$
\Delta s = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T}
$$

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{T = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi \, \Delta s}}
$$

**Step H — Derive the intercept-based formula for $S$:**
At $s = 0$:

$$
\log\left( \frac{2.25 \, T t_{0}}{r^{2} S} \right) = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{2.25 \, T t_{0}}{r^{2} S} = 1
$$

$$
\boxed{S = \frac{2.25 \, T \, t_{0}}{r^{2}}}
$$

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Numerical Problem — Jacob's Method

**Problem Statement:**
A fully penetrating well in a confined aquifer is pumped at a constant rate of $Q = 2500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$. Drawdown is observed in an observation well located at $r = 100 \, \text{m}$ from the pumping well. The semi-log plot of $s$ (m) vs $t$ (min) shows a straight line. Two points on the line are:
- At $t_{1} = 10 \, \text{min}$, $s_{1} = 2.40 \, \text{m}$
- At $t_{2} = 100 \, \text{min}$, $s_{2} = 4.85 \, \text{m}$

**Compute $T$ and $S$.**

#### Step 1 — Compute the slope $\Delta s$ per log cycle

$$
\Delta s = s_{2} - s_{1} = 4.85 - 2.40 = 2.45 \, \text{m} \text{ per log cycle of } t
$$

#### Step 2 — Convert $Q$ to consistent units

$$
Q = 2500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day} = \frac{2500}{1440} \, \text{m}^3/\text{min} = 1.7361 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}
$$

(For $T$ in m²/min, we keep $Q$ in m³/min.)

#### Step 3 — Calculate $T$

$$
T = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi \, \Delta s} = \frac{2.303 \times 1.7361}{4 \pi \times 2.45}
$$

$$
T = \frac{3.9982}{30.7876} = 0.1299 \, \text{m}^2/\text{min}
$$

Convert to m²/day:

$$
T = 0.1299 \times 1440 = 187.0 \, \text{m}^2/\text{day}
$$

#### Step 4 — Find $t_0$ (time intercept at $s = 0$)

Using the line equation $s = s_1 + \Delta s \log(t / t_1)$:

$$
0 = 2.40 + 2.45 \log\left( \frac{t_0}{10} \right)
$$

$$
\log\left( \frac{t_0}{10} \right) = -\frac{2.40}{2.45} = -0.9796
$$

$$
\frac{t_0}{10} = 10^{-0.9796} = 0.10472
$$

$$
t_0 = 1.0472 \, \text{min} = 62.83 \, \text{s} = 0.000728 \, \text{day}
$$

#### Step 5 — Calculate $S$

$$
S = \frac{2.25 \, T \, t_0}{r^{2}} = \frac{2.25 \times 187.0 \times 0.000728}{(100)^{2}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{0.3063}{10000} = 3.063 \times 10^{-5}
$$

#### Final Results

> **$T = 187.0 \, \text{m}^2/\text{day}$** and **$S = 3.06 \times 10^{-5}$**

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Problem — Chow's Method

**Problem Statement:**
A confined aquifer is pumped at $Q = 1500 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$. At an observation well $r = 50 \, \text{m}$, the log-log plot of $s$ vs $t$ is matched to the Theis type curve. A match point chosen on the overlay gives:

- From type curve: $W(u) = 3.5$, $u = 0.045$
- From field curve at the same point: $s = 2.10 \, \text{m}$, $t = 90 \, \text{min}$

**Compute $T$ and $S$ using Chow's method.**

#### Step 1 — Calculate $F(u)$

$$
F(u) = W(u) \cdot e^{u} = 3.5 \times e^{0.045}
$$

$$
e^{0.045} = 1.04603
$$

$$
F(u) = 3.5 \times 1.04603 = 3.6611
$$

#### Step 2 — Convert $Q$ and $t$ to consistent units (m²/min)

$$
Q = \frac{1500}{1440} = 1.0417 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}, \quad t = 90 \, \text{min}
$$

#### Step 3 — Calculate $T$ (Chow's form)

$$
T = \frac{Q \cdot F(u)}{4 \pi \cdot s} = \frac{1.0417 \times 3.6611}{4 \pi \times 2.10}
$$

$$
T = \frac{3.8125}{26.3894} = 0.1445 \, \text{m}^2/\text{min} = 208.0 \, \text{m}^2/\text{day}
$$

#### Step 4 — Calculate $S$ using $u = 0.045$

$$
S = \frac{4 T u t}{r^{2}} = \frac{4 \times 0.1445 \times 0.045 \times 90}{(50)^{2}}
$$

$$
S = \frac{2.341}{2500} = 9.36 \times 10^{-4}
$$

#### Final Results

> **$T = 208.0 \, \text{m}^2/\text{day}$** and **$S = 9.36 \times 10^{-4}$**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In Chow's method, students often **forget to compute $F(u) = W(u) e^u$** and directly use $W(u)$ for $T$. This gives an incorrect $T$ by a factor of $e^u$ (e.g., $\sim 5\%$ error for $u = 0.05$). Always include the exponential term.

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture of the Pumping Test Analysis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Pumping Test] --> B[Constant Q from Pumping Well]
    B --> C[Record s vs t at Observation Well]
    C --> D{Choose Method}
    D --> E1[Jacob's Method]
    D --> E2[Chow's Method]
    E1 --> F1[Plot s vs log t Semi-log Paper]
    F1 --> G1[Identify Straight Line Portion]
    G1 --> H1[Compute Slope delta s]
    H1 --> I1[T equals 2.303 Q over 4 pi delta s]
    G1 --> J1[Extend Line to s equals 0]
    J1 --> K1[Read t-zero]
    K1 --> L1[S equals 2.25 T t0 over r squared]
    E2 --> M2[Plot s vs t Log-log Paper]
    M2 --> N2[Overlay Theis Type Curve W of u vs u]
    N2 --> O2[Shift until Curves Match]
    O2 --> P2[Select Match Point]
    P2 --> Q2[Read W of u and u from type curve]
    Q2 --> R2[Read s and t from field curve]
    R2 --> S2[F of u equals W of u times e to the u]
    S2 --> T2[T equals Q F of u over 4 pi s]
    R2 --> U2[S equals 4 T u t over r squared]
    I1 --> V1[Output T and S Jacob]
    L1 --> V1
    T2 --> V2[Output T and S Chow]
    U2 --> V2
    V1 --> W[Compare and Validate]
    V2 --> W
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Node IDs are alphanumeric (start, choose, jaco, chow, etc.) to comply with Mermaid parser rules. The flowchart maps the complete logical flow from raw field data to the final computed values of $T$ and $S$ for both methods, including the branching decision point for method selection.

---

### 4.2 Conceptual Block Diagram: Time-Drawdown Behaviour Regimes

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Early Time: u greater than 0.05] --> B1[Non-linear Region]
    B1 --> C1[Chow's Method Valid]
    C1 --> D1[Jacob's Method NOT Valid]
    A2[Late Time: u less than 0.01] --> B2[Linear Region on Semi-log]
    B2 --> C2[Jacob's Method Valid]
    C2 --> D2[Chow's Method Also Valid]
    B1 --> E[Field s vs t Curve]
    B2 --> E
    E --> F[Extract T and S]
```

---

### 4.3 Schematic of the Three Diagnostic Plots (Block Functional View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Field Data s t] --> B1[Plot 1: s vs t on linear-linear]
    A --> B2[Plot 2: s vs log t on semi-log]
    A --> B3[Plot 3: s vs t on log-log]
    B1 --> C1[Check curve shape]
    B2 --> C2[Look for straight line - Jacob]
    B3 --> C3[Match to type curve - Chow]
    C1 --> D1[Reconcile with method choice]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> E[Final T and S]
```
