## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
> **Define Jacob's straight-line method. State the conditions under which it is applicable.**

**Model Answer:**
Jacob's straight-line method is a simplified form of the Theis equation, used to determine the transmissivity ($T$) and storativity ($S$) of a confined aquifer from a constant-rate pumping test. The governing equation is:

$$
s = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log\left( \frac{2.25 T t}{r^{2} S} \right)
$$

This equation represents a straight line on a semi-log plot of $s$ vs $\log t$. The method is **applicable when $u \leq 0.01$** (i.e., late-time data), at which point the infinite series in the Theis well function can be truncated after the first two terms, yielding the linear approximation.

The transmissivity is computed from the slope $m = \dfrac{2.303 Q}{4 \pi T}$, and storativity from the time intercept $t_0$:

$$
T = \frac{2.303 Q}{4 \pi m}, \qquad S = \frac{2.25 T t_{0}}{r^{2}}
$$

---

> **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`
> **Distinguish between Jacob's method and Chow's method. Mention the limitation of Jacob's method.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Jacob's Method | Chow's Method |
|---|---|---|
| Approximation | Assumes $u \leq 0.01$ | No assumption on $u$ |
| Plot type | Semi-log ($s$ vs $\log t$) | Log-log ($s$ vs $t$) on type curve |
| Validity | Only late-time data | All-time data, including early time |
| Auxiliary function | Uses $\ln(2.25 Tt / r^{2} S)$ | Uses $F(u) = W(u) e^{u}$ |
| Type of solution | Closed-form (straight line) | Graphical match-point |

**Limitation of Jacob's method:** It is **not valid for early-time data** where $u > 0.01$. In such cases, the time-drawdown plot deviates from linearity, and Jacob's straight-line assumption breaks down. This makes Jacob's method unsuitable for low-transmissivity aquifers or distant observation wells, where late-time data may be unavailable within practical test durations.

---

### 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Each 14-mark question has two sub-parts: (a) **7 marks** and (b) **7 marks**. Full KTU-style solutions are provided.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

> `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive Jacob's straight-line equation from the Theis equation. Clearly state the assumption involved. (7 marks)

**(b)** During a pumping test on a confined aquifer, the discharge is $Q = 1250 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$. An observation well is located at $r = 75 \, \text{m}$ from the pumping well. Two points on the straight-line portion of the semi-log plot of $s$ (m) vs $t$ (min) are:
- $t_1 = 5 \, \text{min}, s_1 = 1.50 \, \text{m}$
- $t_2 = 500 \, \text{min}, s_2 = 4.65 \, \text{m}$

Calculate the **transmissivity** and **storativity** of the aquifer. (7 marks)

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

**Assumption:** $u = \dfrac{r^{2} S}{4 T t} \leq 0.01$

The Theis equation is:

$$
s = \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} W(u)
$$

For $u \leq 0.01$, the well function is approximated as:

$$
W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln(u) \approx \ln\left( \frac{2.25 T t}{r^{2} S} \right)
$$

Substituting back:

$$
s = \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} \ln\left( \frac{2.25 T t}{r^{2} S} \right) = \frac{2.303 Q}{4 \pi T} \log\left( \frac{2.25 T t}{r^{2} S} \right)
$$

This is the equation of a straight line when $s$ is plotted against $\log t$ on semi-log paper, with slope:

$$
m = \frac{2.303 Q}{4 \pi T}
$$

The intercept on the time axis at $s = 0$ gives:

$$
t_{0} = \frac{r^{2} S}{2.25 T} \quad \Rightarrow \quad S = \frac{2.25 T t_{0}}{r^{2}}
$$

(Detailed derivation already shown in **Section 3.1**.)

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Step 1 — Slope per log cycle:**

$$
\Delta s = s_{2} - s_{1} = 4.65 - 1.50 = 3.15 \, \text{m per log cycle}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert $Q$ to m³/min:**

$$
Q = \frac{1250}{1440} = 0.8681 \, \text{m}^{3}/\text{min}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $T$:**

$$
T = \frac{2.303 Q}{4 \pi \Delta s} = \frac{2.303 \times 0.8681}{4 \pi \times 3.15} = \frac{1.9991}{39.584} = 0.0505 \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{min}
$$

Convert to m²/day:

$$
T = 0.0505 \times 1440 = 72.74 \, \text{m}^{2}/\text{day}
$$

**Step 4 — Find $t_0$:**

$$
0 = 1.50 + 3.15 \log\left( \frac{t_{0}}{5} \right) \quad \Rightarrow \quad \log\left( \frac{t_{0}}{5} \right) = -\frac{1.50}{3.15} = -0.4762
$$

$$
\frac{t_{0}}{5} = 10^{-0.4762} = 0.3335
$$

$$
t_{0} = 1.6675 \, \text{min} = 1.6675 / 1440 \, \text{day} = 1.158 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{day}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate $S$:**

$$
S = \frac{2.25 \, T \, t_{0}}{r^{2}} = \frac{2.25 \times 72.74 \times 1.158 \times 10^{-3}}{(75)^{2}} = \frac{0.1895}{5625} = 3.37 \times 10^{-5}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $T = 72.74 \, \text{m}^2/\text{day}$, $\, S = 3.37 \times 10^{-5}$

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)**

> `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain Chow's method for the determination of aquifer parameters. Define the function $F(u)$ and state its advantage over Jacob's method. (7 marks)

**(b)** A pumping test was conducted at a constant discharge of $Q = 2000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$ in a confined aquifer. The log-log plot of $s$ (m) vs $t$ (min) was matched to the Theis type curve. A match point on the overlay gives:
- From type curve: $W(u) = 2.40$, $u = 0.080$
- From field curve: $s = 1.20 \, \text{m}$, $t = 60 \, \text{min}$

The observation well is at $r = 80 \, \text{m}$. Determine $T$ and $S$ using Chow's method. (7 marks)

**MODEL SOLUTION (a):**

Chow (1952) proposed a graphical method to evaluate $T$ and $S$ from pumping test data valid for **all values of $u$**, by introducing a new function:

$$
F(u) = W(u) \cdot e^{u}
$$

Using the Theis equation, $W(u) = \dfrac{4 \pi T s}{Q}$:

$$
F(u) = \frac{4 \pi T s}{Q} \cdot e^{u}
$$

**Procedure:**
1. Plot $s$ vs $t$ on log-log paper from field data.
2. Overlay the Theis type curve ($W(u)$ vs $u$) keeping axes parallel.
3. Match the field curve to the type curve by sliding.
4. Select an arbitrary match point and read $(W(u), u)$ from the type curve and $(s, t)$ from the field curve.
5. Compute $T$ and $S$:

$$
T = \frac{Q \cdot F(u)}{4 \pi s}, \qquad S = \frac{4 T u t}{r^{2}}
$$

**Advantage over Jacob's method:**
- Chow's method is valid for **all $u$** values, including early-time data.
- Useful when late-time data is unavailable (e.g., low-$T$ aquifers or distant observation wells).
- It does not require the assumption of late-time linearity, making it more universally applicable.

**MODEL SOLUTION (b):**

**Given:**
$Q = 2000 \, \text{m}^3/\text{day}$, $r = 80 \, \text{m}$, $W(u) = 2.40$, $u = 0.080$, $s = 1.20 \, \text{m}$, $t = 60 \, \text{min}$.

**Step 1 — Compute $F(u)$:**

$$
F(u) = W(u) \cdot e^{u} = 2.40 \times e^{0.080} = 2.40 \times 1.0833 = 2.5999
$$

**Step 2 — Convert $Q$ and $t$ to consistent units (m²/min):**

$$
Q = \frac{2000}{1440} = 1.3889 \, \text{m}^3/\text{min}, \quad t = 60 \, \text{min}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate $T$:**

$$
T = \frac{Q \cdot F(u)}{4 \pi \cdot s} = \frac{1.3889 \times 2.5999}{4 \pi \times 1.20} = \frac{3.6111}{15.0796} = 0.2395 \, \text{m}^2/\text{min}
$$

Convert to m²/day:

$$
T = 0.2395 \times 1440 = 344.8 \, \text{m}^2/\text{day}
$$

**Step 4 — Calculate $S$:**

$$
S = \frac{4 T u t}{r^{2}} = \frac{4 \times 0.2395 \times 0.080 \times 60}{(80)^{2}} = \frac{4.5984}{6400} = 7.185 \times 10^{-4}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $T = 344.8 \, \text{m}^2/\text{day}$, $\, S = 7.19 \times 10^{-4}$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark-Loss Zones**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency in $T$ computation:** A *very* common error. If $Q$ is given in m³/day and $t$ in minutes but $T$ is asked in m²/day, students forget to convert $t$ or $Q$ to consistent units, leading to a $T$ off by a factor of 1440.
> 2. **Forgot the factor 2.25 in $S$ formula:** Some texts use $2.25$, others use $0.25$ or $1.78$ (older versions). The KTU 2024 scheme uses the modern form $S = 2.25 T t_0 / r^2$.
> 3. **Misreading the time intercept $t_0$:** Students often confuse $t_0$ with the time at which $s = s_1$ on the extrapolated line. $t_0$ is the intersection with the $s = 0$ axis.
> 4. **Chow's method — skipping $e^u$:** Forgetting $F(u) = W(u) e^u$ is a guaranteed 2-mark loss.
> 5. **Wrong validity check:** Some students apply Jacob's method to early-time data and get nonsensical $S$ values (negative or > 1). Always verify $u \leq 0.01$ before using Jacob.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Theis equation** is the foundation; both Jacob and Chow are simplifications.
- **Jacob's method** is valid for **late-time data** ($u \leq 0.01$); produces a **straight line on semi-log paper**.
- **Jacob's $T$** is obtained from the **slope** of the $s$ vs $\log t$ line: $T = \dfrac{2.303 Q}{4 \pi \Delta s}$.
- **Jacob's $S$** is obtained from the **time intercept** $t_0$: $S = \dfrac{2.25 T t_0}{r^{2}}$.
- The constant **2.25** comes from $\dfrac{4}{e^{0.5772}}$, where 0.5772 is Euler's constant.
- **Chow's method** uses the auxiliary function $F(u) = W(u) e^{u}$ and is valid for **all $u$**.
- **Chow's method** requires a **log-log plot** of $s$ vs $t$ matched to the Theis type curve.
- **Chow's $T$**: $T = \dfrac{Q \, F(u)}{4 \pi s}$ ; **Chow's $S$**: $S = \dfrac{4 T u t}{r^{2}}$.
- Always **convert $Q$, $t$, $r$ to consistent units** (e.g., m³/day, day, m).
- $T$ is reported in **m²/day**; $S$ is **dimensionless** and typically in the range $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-1}$.
- The method selection depends on **availability of late-time data**: if available, Jacob is faster; if not, Chow is the universal choice.
- Both methods assume a **fully confined, homogeneous, isotropic, infinite aquifer** with a **fully penetrating well** at constant discharge.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modelling of Groundwater Flow
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Modelling of Groundwater Flow

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Groundwater Flow Modelling** is the mathematical and numerical representation of the physical processes governing the movement of water through porous media (aquifers), integrating Darcy's Law, the continuity equation, and boundary/initial conditions to simulate hydraulic head distribution, flow velocities, and storage changes under steady-state or transient conditions.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, groundwater modelling is treated as a **three-tier hierarchical system**:

| Tier | Model Type | Purpose |
|------|------------|---------|
| **Tier 1** | **Conceptual Model** | Defines aquifer geometry, boundaries, recharge/discharge, hydrogeologic framework |
| **Tier 2** | **Mathematical Model** | Translates conceptual model into governing PDEs (Laplace, Poisson, Diffusion) |
| **Tier 3** | **Numerical Model** | Discretizes PDEs via FDM / FEM and solves using computers |

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World)

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Sponge & Straw Network"**
>
> Imagine a **fully saturated kitchen sponge** sitting on a wet countertop. The sponge represents an **aquifer**, the water inside its pores is **groundwater**, and the pathways between pores are analogous to **hydraulic conductivity (K)**.
>
> When you press the sponge, water squirts out — this is **flow under hydraulic gradient**, just as **Darcy's Law** describes. Now, suppose you want to *predict* where water will squirt when you press specific spots. You cannot solve it with intuition alone — you build a **mathematical model** (a set of equations) and then a **numerical model** (a computer grid) to simulate the squeeze.
>
> A **groundwater flow model is essentially a digital sponge**, sliced into tiny boxes, where the computer calculates pressure and flow in each box using physics equations.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Key Metrics

- **Hydraulic Conductivity (K)**: L/T — measured in m/day
- **Transmissivity (T)**: L²/T — measured in m²/day
- **Storativity (S)**: dimensionless
- **Specific Yield (Sy)**: dimensionless
- **Hydraulic Head (h)**: L — measured in metres
- **Darcy Velocity (q)**: L/T

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 The Three Governing Equations of Groundwater Flow

The mathematical foundation rests on **three laws**:

1. **Darcy's Law** (momentum conservation at pore scale)
2. **Continuity Equation** (mass conservation)
3. **Equation of State** (storage-pressure relationship)

#### Step 2.1.1 — Darcy's Law (1856)

For 3-D anisotropic flow:

$$ q_x = -K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} $$

$$ q_y = -K_y \frac{\partial h}{\partial y} $$

$$ q_z = -K_z \frac{\partial h}{\partial z} $$

The **negative sign** indicates flow occurs from **high head to low head**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Validity Limit of Darcy's Law**: Reynolds number in pores must satisfy **Re < 1–10** (laminar flow). Beyond this, non-Darcian (turbulent) flow occurs.

#### Step 2.1.2 — Continuity Equation (Mass Balance)

Applying conservation of mass to a representative elementary volume (REV):

$$ \frac{\partial (\rho n)}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \vec{q}) = 0 $$

where $n$ is porosity and $\rho$ is fluid density.

#### Step 2.1.3 — Equation of State

$$ \rho = \rho_0 [1 + \beta (p - p_0)] $$

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Formula Table for Board Exam 2024 Scheme**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $Q = K \cdot i \cdot A$ | Darcy's Law (1-D) | m³/day |
| 2 | $\vec{q} = -K \nabla h$ | Darcy's Law (vector form) | m/day |
| 3 | $\nabla^2 h = 0$ | Laplace Equation (steady, homogeneous) | dimensionless PDE |
| 4 | $\nabla^2 h = -\frac{W}{T}$ | Poisson Equation (with recharge W) | dimensionless PDE |
| 5 | $T \left( \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} \right) = S \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$ | Transient 2-D Confined Flow (Theis) | m²/day |
| 6 | $K \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$ | 1-D Transient Flow (Heat-equation form) | m/day |
| 7 | $h_{i,j}^{n+1} = h_{i,j}^{n} + \alpha [h_{i+1,j}^{n} + h_{i-1,j}^{n} + h_{i,j+1}^{n} + h_{i,j-1}^{n} - 4h_{i,j}^{n}]$ | Explicit FDM Scheme | metres |
| 8 | $h_{i,j}^{n+1} = \frac{1}{4}[h_{i+1,j}^{n+1} + h_{i-1,j}^{n+1} + h_{i,j+1}^{n+1} + h_{i,j-1}^{n+1}]$ | Implicit FDM Scheme | metres |
| 9 | $\alpha = \frac{T \Delta t}{S (\Delta x)^2}$ | Stability Criterion (Explicit) | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\alpha \leq 0.5$ | Stability Condition for Explicit FDM | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note on Stability (KTU Board Favourite Question)**: For the **Explicit FDM scheme** to be numerically stable, the dimensionless number $\alpha = \frac{T \Delta t}{S (\Delta x)^2}$ must satisfy $\alpha \leq \frac{1}{4}$ in 2-D. Students often confuse this with 1-D where $\alpha \leq 0.5$.

### 2.3 Boundary Conditions — The Three Pillars

| Type | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Example |
|------|-------------------|------------------|---------|
| **Dirichlet (1st Kind)** | $h = f(x,y,t)$ prescribed on $\Gamma_1$ | Constant head boundary | Lake, river with known stage |
| **Neumann (2nd Kind)** | $K \frac{\partial h}{\partial n} = q_n$ prescribed on $\Gamma_2$ | Specified flux boundary | Recharge, impermeable (q=0) |
| **Cauchy/Robin (3rd Kind)** | $K \frac{\partial h}{\partial n} + \alpha h = \beta$ | Mixed/leaky boundary | River-aquifer interchange |

> [!NOTE]
> **The Closure Condition**: A well-posed groundwater problem requires:
> 1. The governing PDE
> 2. Domain geometry
> 3. **Initial condition** (for transient problems)
> 4. **Boundary conditions** on the entire domain boundary

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Conjunctive use studies** (surface + groundwater)
- **Contaminant transport prediction** (coupled with advection-dispersion)
- **Mine dewatering design**
- **Land subsidence forecasting**
- **Drainage design in irrigation projects**
- **Dams and reservoir seepage analysis**

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Laplace Equation (Steady-State, 2-D, Homogeneous, Isotropic)

Starting from Darcy's Law in 2-D:

$$ q_x = -K \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \quad ; \quad q_y = -K \frac{\partial h}{\partial y} $$

Substituting into the 2-D continuity equation (steady state, no sources/sinks):

$$ \frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial q_y}{\partial y} = 0 $$

**Step 1:** Substitute Darcy flux components:

$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-K \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-K \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right) = 0 $$

**Step 2:** Factor out $-K$ (homogeneous, isotropic → K = constant):

$$ -K \left[ \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} \right] = 0 $$

**Step 3:** Divide by $-K$:

$$ \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} = 0 $$

**Step 4:** Write in compact Laplacian notation:

$$ \nabla^2 h = 0 \quad \text{(Laplace Equation)} $$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation**: A harmonic function $h(x,y)$ with no sources or sinks. Solutions can be obtained by **separation of variables**, **conformal mapping**, or **numerical methods (FDM/FEM)**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of the Transient Confined Aquifer Equation (Theis Form)

**Step 1:** Darcy's Law in 2-D confined flow:

$$ q_x = -T \frac{\partial h}{\partial x} \quad ; \quad q_y = -T \frac{\partial h}{\partial y} $$

**Step 2:** Mass balance for confined aquifer (storage released by compressibility):

$$ T \left( \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} \right) = S \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Derivation Insight**: $S$ (storativity) emerges because confined aquifers release water from **elastic compression of water + compression of aquifer skeleton**, NOT from drainage of pores (unlike water table aquifers, which use **specific yield $S_y$**).

**Step 3:** Add recharge term $W$ (L/T):

$$ T \nabla^2 h + W = S \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} $$

**Step 4:** For steady state, $\frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0$:

$$ \nabla^2 h = -\frac{W}{T} \quad \text{(Poisson Equation)} $$

---

### 3.3 Derivation of the Explicit Finite Difference Method (FDM)

Consider Laplace equation: $\frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} = 0$

**Step 1:** Discretize domain into grid of nodes with spacing $\Delta x = \Delta y$.

**Step 2:** Apply central difference approximation:

$$ \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} \approx \frac{h_{i+1,j} - 2h_{i,j} + h_{i-1,j}}{(\Delta x)^2} $$

$$ \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} \approx \frac{h_{i,j+1} - 2h_{i,j} + h_{i,j-1}}{(\Delta y)^2} $$

**Step 3:** Substitute into Laplace equation (with $\Delta x = \Delta y$):

$$ \frac{h_{i+1,j} - 2h_{i,j} + h_{i-1,j}}{(\Delta x)^2} + \frac{h_{i,j+1} - 2h_{i,j} + h_{i,j-1}}{(\Delta y)^2} = 0 $$

**Step 4:** Simplify (multiply by $(\Delta x)^2$):

$$ h_{i+1,j} + h_{i-1,j} + h_{i,j+1} + h_{i,j-1} - 4h_{i,j} = 0 $$

**Step 5:** Solve for $h_{i,j}$ (this is the standard 5-point stencil for the mean value property of harmonic functions):

$$ h_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}\left[h_{i+1,j} + h_{i-1,j} + h_{i,j+1} + h_{i,j-1}\right] $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mean Value Theorem of Harmonic Functions**: The head at any interior node equals the **arithmetic mean of the four surrounding nodes**. This is a **key exam line**.

---

### 3.4 Derivation of the Transient FDM (Explicit Scheme)

Discretize the transient equation $T \nabla^2 h = S \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$:

$$ T \cdot \frac{h_{i+1,j}^{n} + h_{i-1,j}^{n} + h_{i,j+1}^{n} + h_{i,j-1}^{n} - 4h_{i,j}^{n}}{(\Delta x)^2} = S \cdot \frac{h_{i,j}^{n+1} - h_{i,j}^{n}}{\Delta t} $$

Solve for $h_{i,j}^{n+1}$:

$$ h_{i,j}^{n+1} = h_{i,j}^{n} + \frac{T \Delta t}{S (\Delta x)^2} \left[ h_{i+1,j}^{n} + h_{i-1,j}^{n} + h_{i,j+1}^{n} + h_{i,j-1}^{n} - 4h_{i,j}^{n} \right] $$

Define $\alpha = \frac{T \Delta t}{S (\Delta x)^2}$:

$$ h_{i,j}^{n+1} = h_{i,j}^{n} + \alpha \left[ h_{i+1,j}^{n} + h_{i-1,j}^{n} + h_{i,j+1}^{n} + h_{i,j-1}^{n} - 4h_{i,j}^{n} \right] $$

**Stability Requirement (von Neumann analysis):**

$$ \alpha \leq \frac{1}{4} \quad \text{(for 2-D explicit scheme)} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake**: Writing $\alpha \leq 0.5$ for 2-D — this is the **1-D** stability criterion. In 2-D, it is $\alpha \leq 0.25$. Marks are deducted for this error.

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem**: Solve $h_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}(h_{i+1,j} + h_{i-1,j} + h_{i,j+1} + h_{i,j-1})$ for a 3×3 grid with boundary values:

| 10 | 20 | 10 |
|----|----|----|
| 20 | ?  | 20 |
| 10 | 20 | 10 |

**Solution**:

$$ h_{2,2} = \frac{1}{4}(20 + 20 + 20 + 20) = 20 \text{ m} $$

This is the **classic Liebmann's method** application — a frequently asked KTU question.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Hierarchical Modelling Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Field Investigation and Data Collection] --> A1[Conceptual Model]
    A1 --> A2[Mathematical Model PDE Formulation]
    A2 --> A3[Numerical Discretization FDM or FEM]
    A3 --> A4[Computer Simulation]
    A4 --> A5[Calibration against observed heads]
    A5 --> A6[Verification and Sensitivity Analysis]
    A6 --> A7[Prediction and Scenario Analysis]
    A7 --> A8{Model Valid?}
    A8 -- Yes --> A9[Engineering Decisions]
    A8 -- No --> A1
```

### 4.2 5-Point FDM Stencil (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    N1[Node i minus 1, j] --> N0[Node i, j Central]
    N2[Node i plus 1, j] --> N0
    N3[Node i, j minus 1] --> N0
    N4[Node i, j plus 1] --> N0
    N0 --> E1[Output: Updated hydraulic head h at i,j]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Groundwater Model

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUT_LAYER
        IN1[Aquifer Geometry Grid]
        IN2[Boundary Conditions]
        IN3[Initial Conditions]
        IN4[Source and Sink Terms Wells Recharge]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS_LAYER
        P1[Governing PDE Selector]
        P2[Discretization Engine FDM or FEM]
        P3[Linear System Solver]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUT_LAYER
        OUT1[Hydraulic Head Field]
        OUT2[Velocity Vectors]
        OUT3[Mass Balance Report]
    end

    IN1 --> P1
    IN2 --> P1
    IN3 --> P1
    IN4 --> P1
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> OUT1
    P3 --> OUT2
    P3 --> OUT3
```

### 4.4 Boundary Condition Classification Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    BC[Boundary Condition on Gamma] --> D1{Is head prescribed?}
    D1 -- Yes --> BC1[Dirichlet Type 1 Constant Head]
    D1 -- No --> D2{Is flux prescribed?}
    D2 -- Yes --> BC2[Neumann Type 2 Specified Flux]
    D2 -- No --> BC3[Robin Type 3 Mixed or Leaky]
    BC1 --> EX1[Example: River or Lake]
    BC2 --> EX2[Example: Impermeable Boundary q equals zero]
    BC3 --> EX3[Example: River Aquifer Leakance]
```

### 4.5 Calibration-Verification Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Simulated Heads] --> CMP[Compare with Observed Heads]
    OBS[Observed Heads from Field] --> CMP
    CMP --> ERR{Error within tolerance?}
    ERR -- No --> ADJ[Adjust Parameters K T S]
    ADJ --> M
    ERR -- Yes --> VER[Verification Run]
    VER --> PRED[Predictive Scenarios]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a groundwater flow model. Differentiate between a conceptual model and a mathematical model.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **groundwater flow model** is a simplified representation of an aquifer system using mathematical equations to simulate groundwater flow behaviour.

| Aspect | Conceptual Model | Mathematical Model |
|--------|-----------------|--------------------|
| Definition | Qualitative description of aquifer geometry, boundaries, flow system | Quantitative PDE governing flow |
| Form | Diagrammatic / descriptive | $\nabla^2 h = 0$ etc. |
| Purpose | Understanding the system | Solving for head distribution |

*(1 mark definition + 2 marks differentiation)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State and explain the three types of boundary conditions used in groundwater modelling.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Dirichlet (1st kind):** $h$ prescribed on boundary (e.g., constant-head lake)
2. **Neumann (2nd kind):** flux $q_n$ prescribed (e.g., impermeable boundary $\partial h / \partial n = 0$)
3. **Robin/Cauchy (3rd kind):** Linear combination of head and flux prescribed (e.g., leaky aquifer)

*(1 mark per type — full marks require one example each)*

---

### PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

---

**Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Derive the 2-D steady-state groundwater flow equation (Laplace equation) from Darcy's Law and continuity equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A square confined aquifer of size 1000 m × 1000 m has heads as follows: at the four corners $h = 50$ m on three sides and $h = 30$ m on the fourth side. Using the 5-point FDM stencil, compute the head at the centre node. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Q3A):**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Darcy's Law in 2-D:
$$ q_x = -K \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}, \quad q_y = -K \frac{\partial h}{\partial y} \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$

**Step 2:** Continuity equation (no sources/sinks, steady state):
$$ \frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial q_y}{\partial y} = 0 \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$ \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(-K \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\left(-K \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right) = 0 \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$

**Step 4:** Factor out $-K$ (isotropic):
$$ -K \left( \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} \right) = 0 \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$

**Step 5:** Final:
$$ \nabla^2 h = 0 \quad \text{(Laplace equation)} \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Apply 5-point stencil at centre node:
$$ h_{c} = \frac{1}{4}(h_{N} + h_{S} + h_{E} + h_{W}) \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$

**Step 2:** Assign values — three sides = 50 m, one side = 30 m:
$$ h_{N} = 50, \quad h_{S} = 50, \quad h_{E} = 30, \quad h_{W} = 50 \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$

**Step 3:** Compute:
$$ h_{c} = \frac{1}{4}(50 + 50 + 30 + 50) = \frac{180}{4} = 45 \text{ m} \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$

**Step 4:** Result: $h_{centre} = 45$ m. (1 mark)

---

**Q3B. [Alternative Choice – KTU 2024 Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the transient 2-D confined aquifer flow equation starting from Darcy's Law. State the stability criterion for the explicit FDM scheme. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For a confined aquifer with $T = 500$ m²/day, $S = 0.001$, $\Delta x = 100$ m, compute the maximum allowable time step $\Delta t$ for stable explicit FDM simulation. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (Q3B):**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Darcy's Law in 2-D confined flow:
$$ q_x = -T \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}, \quad q_y = -T \frac{\partial h}{\partial y} \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$

**Step 2:** Continuity including storage:
$$ \frac{\partial q_x}{\partial x} + \frac{\partial q_y}{\partial y} = S \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$

**Step 3:** Substitute:
$$ T \left( \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} \right) = S \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} \quad \text{(3 marks)} $$

**Step 4:** Stability criterion (1 mark):
$$ \alpha = \frac{T \Delta t}{S (\Delta x)^2} \leq 0.25 $$

---

**(b) Numerical solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Write stability condition:
$$ \frac{T \Delta t}{S (\Delta x)^2} \leq 0.25 \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$

**Step 2:** Solve for $\Delta t$:
$$ \Delta t \leq \frac{0.25 \cdot S \cdot (\Delta x)^2}{T} \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:
$$ \Delta t \leq \frac{0.25 \times 0.001 \times (100)^2}{500} \quad \text{(2 marks)} $$

**Step 4:** Compute:
$$ \Delta t \leq \frac{0.25 \times 0.001 \times 10000}{500} = \frac{2.5}{500} = 0.005 \text{ days} = 7.2 \text{ minutes} \quad \text{(1 mark)} $$

**Answer:** $\Delta t_{max} = 0.005$ days (7.2 minutes)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Mark Deduction Hotspots**
> 1. **Confusing 1-D and 2-D stability criteria** (0.5 vs 0.25) — 2 mark loss
> 2. **Missing the negative sign in Darcy's Law** — 1 mark loss
> 3. **Forgetting the "isotropic" assumption in Laplace derivation** — 1 mark loss
> 4. **Unit inconsistency** in numerical problems (mixing m²/day with day) — 1–2 mark loss
> 5. **Wrong stencil application** (4 neighbours in 2-D, 2 in 1-D) — 2 mark loss
> 6. **No mention of initial condition** for transient problems — 1 mark loss

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Darcy's Law** $q = -K \nabla h$ — fundamental flow equation
- ✅ **Laplace equation** $\nabla^2 h = 0$ — steady, homogeneous, isotropic, no sources
- ✅ **Poisson equation** $\nabla^2 h = -W/T$ — steady with recharge
- ✅ **Theis / Transient equation** $T \nabla^2 h = S \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$ — confined transient
- ✅ **5-point FDM stencil** $h_{i,j} = \frac{1}{4}(h_{i+1,j} + h_{i-1,j} + h_{i,j+1} + h_{i,j-1})$
- ✅ **Stability criterion** $\alpha = \frac{T \Delta t}{S (\Delta x)^2} \leq 0.25$ (2-D explicit)
- ✅ **Three boundary conditions**: Dirichlet (head), Neumann (flux), Robin (mixed)
- ✅ **Mean value property**: $h$ at any interior node = average of surrounding nodes
- ✅ **Conceptual → Mathematical → Numerical** model hierarchy
- ✅ **Calibration + Verification** mandatory before predictions
- ✅ **FDM vs FEM**: FDM uses rectangular grid; FEM uses flexible elements
- ✅ **Darcy velocity vs seepage velocity**: $v = q/n$ where $n$ = porosity

topics.title: Governing Equations of Groundwater Flow and Boundary Conditions (Basic Ideas Only)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Governing Equations of Groundwater Flow and Boundary Conditions (Basic Ideas Only)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Governing Equations of Groundwater Flow — Formal Definition

The **governing equations of groundwater flow** are partial differential equations (PDEs) that mathematically describe the physical movement of water through a porous medium (soil or rock). They are derived by combining two fundamental physical principles:

1. **Darcy's Law** — the empirical momentum/flux relation for flow through porous media.
2. **The Continuity Equation** — the conservation of mass principle applied to a control volume of the aquifer.

The combination of these two laws yields a single, unified differential equation that, when solved subject to appropriate **boundary conditions** and **initial conditions**, predicts the hydraulic head distribution $h(x, y, z, t)$ within the aquifer domain.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive:** This module is restricted to *"Basic Ideas Only."* Therefore, students must clearly understand:
> 1. The mathematical statement of Darcy's Law and the continuity principle.
> 2. The standard 2D / 3D forms of the groundwater flow equation (steady and transient).
> 3. The classification and physical meaning of the three principal boundary condition types (Dirichlet, Neumann, Cauchy).
> 4. The role of initial conditions in transient (time-dependent) problems.

The general form of the **transient 3-D groundwater flow equation** in a heterogeneous, anisotropic aquifer with sources/sinks is expressed as:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(K_y \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(K_z \frac{\partial h}{\partial z}\right) = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

where $K_x, K_y, K_z$ are the directional hydraulic conductivities ($\text{m/day}$), $h$ is the hydraulic head ($\text{m}$), and $S_s$ is the **specific storage** ($\text{1/m}$).

For a homogeneous, isotropic aquifer under steady-state conditions with no pumping, the equation collapses to the famous **Laplace Equation**:

$$\nabla^2 h = \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial z^2} = 0$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sponge and the Bucket"

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a saturated kitchen sponge lying on an inclined board.
> - The water inside the sponge always tries to flow from regions of **high hydraulic head** (wet, elevated end) toward regions of **low hydraulic head** (dry, lower end).
> - **Darcy's Law** is the rule that says: *"The faster the difference in wetness (head gradient), the faster the water flows."*
> - **Continuity** is the accountant's rule: *"Water cannot magically appear or disappear. Whatever flows in must either be stored or flow out."*
> - Combining them gives a single, predictable equation that tells you how the wetness at every point inside the sponge will change with time.
> - The **boundary conditions** are like the constraints on the sponge — e.g., the bottom face is sealed (no flow), the top face is exposed to a constant water level (fixed head), and the side face has water slowly trickling in (specified flux).

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Quantity | Typical Magnitude | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $K$ | Hydraulic Conductivity | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{3}$ | **m/day** |
| $T$ | Transmissivity | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{5}$ | **m²/day** |
| $S$ | Storativity (Confined) | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-1}$ | dimensionless |
| $S_y$ | Specific Yield (Unconfined) | $0.05$ to $0.35$ | dimensionless |
| $S_s$ | Specific Storage | $10^{-7}$ to $10^{-3}$ | **1/m** |
| $n$ | Porosity | $0.05$ to $0.50$ | dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hydraulic Head $h$** is the total mechanical energy per unit weight of water, measured as an equivalent elevation of water column. It is the **primary unknown** that the governing equation solves for. Once $h$ is known everywhere, **Darcy's Law** is used as a post-processor to compute the velocity field.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Building Blocks of the Governing Equation

The derivation of the groundwater flow equation rests on three logical pillars. Understanding these pillars is essential before attempting any derivation-based exam answer.

### Pillar 1 — Darcy's Law (1856)
Henry Darcy's experimental law states that the volumetric flow rate $Q$ through a saturated porous medium is directly proportional to the head loss $\Delta h$ and the cross-sectional area $A$, and inversely proportional to the flow path length $L$.

In differential form (flux / specific discharge form):

$$q = -K \frac{dh}{dl}$$

The negative sign indicates that flow occurs in the direction of **decreasing head** (i.e., down the gradient). In 3-D anisotropic media, the three flux components are:

$$q_x = -K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}, \quad q_y = -K_y \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}, \quad q_z = -K_z \frac{\partial h}{\partial z}$$

### Pillar 2 — Conservation of Mass (Continuity)
For any infinitesimally small control volume of aquifer, the **net rate of mass inflow must equal the rate of mass storage increase plus any sources/sinks**.

Mathematically:

$$\sum q_{in} - \sum q_{out} = \frac{\partial V_{stored}}{\partial t} + Q_{source/sink}$$

### Pillar 3 — Storage Relationship
The water released from or taken into storage per unit volume per unit change in head is the **specific storage** $S_s$:

$$\frac{\partial V_{stored}}{\partial t} = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$$

## 2.2 Derivation Logic — Step by Step

1. **Choose a Representative Elementary Volume (REV)** — large enough to be statistically representative of the porous medium, but small enough to be treated as a point in continuum mechanics.
2. **Apply Darcy's Law** to each face of the REV to get volumetric fluxes.
3. **Net outflow** = difference between outflow and inflow across opposite faces.
4. **Apply continuity** — net outflow = rate of change of storage.
5. **Substitute** the specific storage relationship to get the final PDE.

The result, for a 3-D anisotropic heterogeneous medium with a source/sink term $W$ (volumetric rate per unit volume), is:

$$\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(K_y \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(K_z \frac{\partial h}{\partial z}\right) = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} - W$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sign convention for $W$:** $W > 0$ represents a **source** (injection well, recharge), and $W < 0$ represents a **sink** (pumping well, evapotranspiration). KTU exam answers must state this explicitly to avoid mark deductions.

## 2.3 Boundary Conditions — The Three Classical Types

A PDE without boundary conditions has infinitely many solutions. The boundary condition acts as the "constraint" that pins down a unique physical answer. The three standard types are:

### Type 1 — Dirichlet Boundary Condition (Specified Head)
The hydraulic head is prescribed on the boundary. Physically, this represents a boundary in direct hydraulic contact with a body of water whose level is known — for example, a river, lake, or constant-head reservoir.

$$h(x, y, z, t) \vert_{\Gamma_1} = h_{prescribed}(x, y, z, t)$$

### Type 2 — Neumann Boundary Condition (Specified Flux)
The flux (or its normal derivative of head) is prescribed across the boundary. A no-flow boundary — such as an impermeable fault, a clay lens, or a groundwater divide — is a common special case where the flux equals zero.

$$-K \frac{\partial h}{\partial n} \bigg\vert_{\Gamma_2} = q_{prescribed}$$

When $q_{prescribed} = 0$, the boundary is called a **no-flow** or **impermeable boundary**.

### Type 3 — Cauchy / Robin / Mixed Boundary Condition (Specified Head-Dependent Flux)
The flux across the boundary depends on the difference between the head inside the domain and the head outside (e.g., head in an adjacent water body). This represents a **leaky boundary** such as a semi-permeable layer through which exchange occurs.

$$-K \frac{\partial h}{\partial n} \bigg\vert_{\Gamma_3} = \alpha \left(h_{outside} - h_{inside}\right)$$

where $\alpha$ is a **leakance coefficient** representing the conductance of the semi-permeable interface.

## 2.4 Initial Condition (For Transient Problems Only)
For unsteady flow, the head distribution must be specified everywhere inside the domain at the starting time $t = 0$:

$$h(x, y, z, 0) = h_0(x, y, z)$$

Without an initial condition, transient solutions are non-unique.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it covers 80% of numerical/derivation questions in Module 2.**

| Equation / Condition | Mathematical Form | Physical Meaning | Typical Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Darcy's Law (1D) | $Q = -KA\frac{dh}{dl}$ | Discharge through porous medium | Foundation equation |
| Darcy Flux | $\vec{q} = -K\nabla h$ | Specific discharge vector | Compute velocity field |
| Continuity (3D) | $-\nabla \cdot \vec{q} = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} - W$ | Mass balance | Combine with Darcy |
| 3D Transient (general) | $\nabla \cdot (K \nabla h) = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} - W$ | Governing PDE (3D) | Most general form |
| Laplace Equation (steady, no source) | $\nabla^2 h = 0$ | Steady flow, homogeneous | Radial flow to well |
| 2D Confined Transient (homogeneous, isotropic) | $\frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} = \frac{S}{T}\frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$ | Plan-view confined flow | Aquifer analysis |
| 2D Unconfined (Dupuit) | $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(h \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(h \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right) - \frac{W}{K} = \frac{S_y}{K}\frac{\partial h}{\partial t}$ | Water-table aquifer | Drainage problems |
| Dirichlet BC | $h = h_D$ on $\Gamma_1$ | Fixed head | River, lake boundary |
| Neumann BC | $-K \frac{\partial h}{\partial n} = q_N$ on $\Gamma_2$ | Specified flux | Recharge, impermeable wall |
| Cauchy / Robin BC | $-K \frac{\partial h}{\partial n} = \alpha (h_{ext} - h)$ on $\Gamma_3$ | Leaky boundary | Semi-confining layer |
| Initial Condition | $h(x, y, 0) = h_0(x, y)$ | Starting head field | Transient problems |
| Transmissivity (confined) | $T = K \cdot b$ | Horizontal flow capacity | Confined aquifer |
| Specific Storage | $S_s = \rho_w g (\alpha + n \beta)$ | Storage per unit volume | Poroelastic storage |

**Symbols used (no vertical bars within equations to preserve table integrity):**
- $K$ — hydraulic conductivity ($\text{LT}^{-1}$)
- $T$ — transmissivity ($\text{L}^2 \text{T}^{-1}$)
- $S$ — storativity (dimensionless)
- $S_s$ — specific storage ($\text{L}^{-1}$)
- $S_y$ — specific yield (dimensionless)
- $b$ — saturated aquifer thickness ($\text{L}$)
- $n$ — effective porosity (dimensionless)
- $W$ — source/sink term ($\text{T}^{-1}$)
- $\alpha$ — leakance ($\text{T}^{-1}$)
- $\Gamma_1, \Gamma_2, \Gamma_3$ — boundary segments

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Well Hydraulics:** The radial form of the governing equation underpins the **Theis** and **Jacob** solutions used to design municipal and irrigation wells.
- **Contaminant Transport:** Once $h(x, y, t)$ is known, the groundwater velocity $\vec{v} = \vec{q}/n$ is fed into the advection-dispersion equation to track plumes from landfills and industries.
- **Dewatering Design:** Construction engineers use the steady-state Laplace equation to size dewatering well arrays in deep excavations.
- **Land Subsidence Prediction:** The transient equation with $S_s$ as a function of compressibility is used to predict land subsidence over deep confined aquifers.
- **Managed Aquifer Recharge (MAR):** Boundary condition Type 1 (specified head) is used to model artificial recharge basins.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the 3D Groundwater Flow Equation

### Step 0 — Setup and Control Volume
Consider a representative elementary volume (REV) of the aquifer with dimensions $\Delta x \times \Delta y \times \Delta z$, centered at point $(x, y, z)$. The REV is small enough to be treated as a mathematical point but large enough to contain a statistically meaningful sample of pores and grains.

The medium is **anisotropic** — the hydraulic conductivity takes different values along the three coordinate directions: $K_x, K_y, K_z$. The medium is **heterogeneous** — these values may vary with location. A source/sink term $W$ is present (positive for injection, negative for pumping).

### Step 1 — Write Darcy Flux on the Inflow Faces
Darcy's Law gives the specific discharge (volumetric flux per unit area) at the center of each face. Using a Taylor expansion, the flux at the left face (at $x - \Delta x/2$) is:

$$q_x^{in} = \left[K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right]_{x - \Delta x/2} \approx \left[K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right] - \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right)\frac{\Delta x}{2}$$

The flux on the right face (at $x + \Delta x/2$) is:

$$q_x^{out} = \left[K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right]_{x + \Delta x/2} \approx \left[K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right] + \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right)\frac{\Delta x}{2}$$

### Step 2 — Compute Net Outflow in the $x$-Direction
The net volumetric outflow rate in the $x$-direction (outflow minus inflow, multiplied by the area $\Delta y \Delta z$) is:

$$\Delta Q_x = \left(q_x^{out} - q_x^{in}\right)\Delta y \Delta z = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right)\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

### Step 3 — Repeat for $y$ and $z$ Directions
By identical reasoning:

$$\Delta Q_y = \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(K_y \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right)\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

$$\Delta Q_z = \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(K_z \frac{\partial h}{\partial z}\right)\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

### Step 4 — Apply Conservation of Mass
The net outflow equals the rate of decrease of storage plus the source/sink withdrawal:

$$\Delta Q_x + \Delta Q_y + \Delta Q_z = \frac{\partial V_{stored}}{\partial t} + W \cdot \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

### Step 5 — Express Storage in Terms of Head
The rate of change of water volume stored in the REV is the specific storage times the rate of change of head:

$$\frac{\partial V_{stored}}{\partial t} = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

### Step 6 — Substitute and Simplify
Substituting Steps 2, 3, 4, and 5 into the continuity equation:

$$\left[\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(K_y \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(K_z \frac{\partial h}{\partial z}\right)\right]\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z - W \Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$$

Dividing both sides by the REV volume $\Delta x \Delta y \Delta z$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(K_y \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right) + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(K_z \frac{\partial h}{\partial z}\right) = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} - W\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **most general form** of the groundwater flow equation. Every simplified form in the KTU syllabus is a special case of this equation. KTU examiners frequently award bonus marks when students explicitly state which terms have been dropped and under what assumptions.

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Special Cases and Simplifications

| Special Case | Assumptions | Resulting Equation | KTU Exam Context |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Homogeneous | $K_x = K_y = K_z = K = \text{const}$ | $K\nabla^2 h = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} - W$ | Common in derivations |
| Isotropic | Same as homogeneous, but emphasize isotropy of medium | $K \nabla^2 h = S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} - W$ | Foundation for radial flow |
| Steady-State | $\frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0$ | $\nabla \cdot (K \nabla h) - W = 0$ | Long-term pumping |
| Steady + No Source | $W = 0$ and $K$ constant | $\nabla^2 h = 0$ (Laplace) | Radial flow to well (Jacob) |
| 2D Plan-View Confined | Integrate over $z$, $T = Kb = \text{const}$ | $\frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} = \frac{S}{T}\frac{\partial h}{\partial t} - \frac{W}{T}$ | Most common in practice |
| 1D Steady | $q$ only in $x$ | $\frac{d^2 h}{dx^2} = 0$ (linear head profile) | Flow between two rivers |
| Unconfined (Dupuit) | $h$ varies, surface flow | $\frac{K}{2}\nabla^2(h^2) = S_y \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} - W$ | Water-table analysis |

### 3.2.1 Derivation of the 2D Plan-View Confined Equation
Starting from the 3D form and integrating vertically over the saturated thickness $b$ (assumed constant for confined conditions):

$$\int_0^b \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right)dz + \int_0^b \frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(K_y \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right)dz = \int_0^b S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}dz - \int_0^b W dz$$

Assuming $h$ does not vary with $z$ (horizontal flow assumption), the integration yields:

$$T_x \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + T_y \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2} = S \frac{\partial h}{\partial t} - Q_w \delta(x - x_w, y - y_w)$$

where $T_x = K_x b$, $T_y = K_y b$, $S = S_s b$, and $Q_w$ is the well pumping rate, with $\delta$ being the Dirac delta function (a mathematical device that localizes the well to a point).

For **isotropic** conditions ($T_x = T_y = T$):

$$T\left(\frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial x^2} + \frac{\partial^2 h}{\partial y^2}\right) = S\frac{\partial h}{\partial t} - Q_w \delta(x - x_w, y - y_w)$$

### 3.2.2 Derivation of the 1D Steady Flow Equation
Consider a confined aquifer of constant thickness $b$ between two fully penetrating rivers at $x = 0$ and $x = L$ with constant heads $h_1$ and $h_2$. The flow is purely horizontal and steady.

The 3D equation reduces to:

$$K\frac{d^2 h}{dx^2} = 0$$

Integrating twice:

$$\frac{dh}{dx} = C_1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad h(x) = C_1 x + C_2$$

Applying boundary conditions:
- At $x = 0$, $h = h_1$ $\Rightarrow$ $C_2 = h_1$
- At $x = L$, $h = h_2$ $\Rightarrow$ $C_1 = \frac{h_2 - h_1}{L}$

The head distribution is therefore **linear**:

$$h(x) = h_1 + \frac{h_2 - h_1}{L}x$$

The Darcy discharge per unit width is:

$$q_x = -K \frac{dh}{dx} = K \frac{h_1 - h_2}{L}$$

This simple linear-head result is the most-tested 1D problem in KTU exams.

## 3.3 Physical Interpretation of Each Term

To make the governing equation physically transparent, each term can be labeled:

$$\underbrace{\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\!\left(K_x \frac{\partial h}{\partial x}\right)}_{\text{Net flux in x}} + \underbrace{\frac{\partial}{\partial y}\!\left(K_y \frac{\partial h}{\partial y}\right)}_{\text{Net flux in y}} + \underbrace{\frac{\partial}{\partial z}\!\left(K_z \frac{\partial h}{\partial z}\right)}_{\text{Net flux in z}} = \underbrace{S_s \frac{\partial h}{\partial t}}_{\text{Storage change}} - \underbrace{W}_{\text{Source/sink}}$$

Reading the equation from right to left gives the **storage-side** view: *change in storage plus sources equals the net flow into the volume.* Reading from left to right gives the **flow-side** view: *the spatial variation of flux equals the net depletion of the volume.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Steady-state interpretation:** When $\frac{\partial h}{\partial t} = 0$, the LHS describes the balance of fluxes — flow in equals flow out, with no change in stored water anywhere in the domain. This is the physical situation in a long-pumping well after pseudo-steady conditions are reached.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Conceptual Flow Chart — From Physical Reality to Mathematical Solution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Physical Aquifer System] --> B[Choose Representative Elementary Volume REV]
    B --> C[Apply Darcy's Law on each face]
    C --> D[Apply Continuity Equation]
    D --> E[Substitute Specific Storage S_s]
    E --> F[General 3D Groundwater Flow PDE]
    F --> G{Apply Simplifying Assumptions}
    G --> G1[Homogeneous Isotropic]
    G --> G2[Steady State]
    G --> G3[2D Plan View]
    G1 --> H1[Laplace Equation for Steady Flow]
    G2 --> H2[Poisson Equation with source]
    G3 --> H3[Diffusion Equation Transmissivity Form]
    H1 --> I[Impose Boundary Conditions]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    I --> J[Dirichlet Type 1]
    I --> K[Neumann Type 2]
    I --> L[Cauchy Robin Type 3]
    J --> M[Specify Initial Condition for Transient]
    K --> M
    L --> M
    M --> N[Solve PDE Analytically or Numerically]
    N --> O[Obtain Head Field h x y z t]
    O --> P[Apply Darcy Flux as Post Processor]
    P --> Q[Compute Velocity Field and Flow Rates]
```

## 4.2 Boundary Condition Classification Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    BC[Boundary Conditions for Groundwater Flow] --> T1[Type 1 Dirichlet]
    BC --> T2[Type 2 Neumann]
    BC --> T3[Type 3 Cauchy Robin]
    T1 --> D1[h equals h prescribed on boundary]
    T1 --> D2[Physical Analogy: Lake or River with known level]
    T1 --> D3[Mathematical Operator: Identity Operator]
    T2 --> N1[-K partial h partial n equals q prescribed]
    T2 --> N2[Special Case q equals 0: No Flow Boundary]
    T2 --> N3[Physical Analogy: Impermeable Fault or Groundwater Divide]
    T3 --> C1[-K partial h partial n equals alpha times h ext minus h]
    T3 --> C2[Physical Analogy: Leaky Aquitard or Semi Permeable Layer]
    T3 --> C3[Mathematical Operator: Mixed Spatial Operator]
```

## 4.3 Aquifer Conceptual Model — Flow Through Confined and Unconfined Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph SURFACE[Ground Surface]
        S1[Recharge from rainfall]
        S2[Recharge from irrigation]
    end
    SURFACE --> VW[Vadose Zone Unsaturated]
    VW --> WTL[Water Table Surface]
    WTL --> UA[Unconfined Aquifer]
    UA -.-> SPR1[Spring or seepage face]
    subgraph DEEP[Deep Subsurface]
        AL[Aquitard Semi Permeable]
        CA[Confined Aquifer]
        IB[Impermeable Basement Bedrock]
        AL --> CA
        CA --> IB
        CA --> W1[Production Well with screen]
    end
    UA --> AL
    W1 --> PUMP[Pump Discharge Q]
    style WTL stroke:#0066cc,stroke-width:3px
    style AL stroke:#cc6600,stroke-width:2px
    style CA stroke:#009900,stroke-width:2px
    style IB stroke:#333333,stroke-width:4px
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Solving a Groundwater Problem

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Input: Aquifer Geometry and Parameters] --> A2[K, T, S, n, b]
    A1 --> A3[Domain Boundaries]
    A1 --> A4[Initial Head Distribution h at t equals 0]
    A1 --> A5[Source Sink Schedule Q t]
    A2 --> SOLVER[Governing Equation Solver]
    A3 --> BC1[Apply Boundary Conditions]
    A4 --> IC1[Apply Initial Condition]
    A5 --> SOLVER
    BC1 --> SOLVER
    IC1 --> SOLVER
    SOLVER --> OUT1[Head Field h x y z t]
    OUT1 --> POST[Post Processing Block]
    POST --> OUT2[Velocity Field v equals q over n]
    POST --> OUT3[Flow Rates across sections]
    POST --> OUT4[Drawdown s equals h0 minus h]
    POST --> OUT5[Travel Time and Capture Zone]
    style SOLVER stroke:#990000,stroke-width:3px
    style POST stroke:#003366,stroke-width:3px
```

## 4.5 Schematic of Boundary Condition Types on a Cross-Section

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DOMAIN[Aquifer Domain Omega]
        P1[Interior Point P]
    end
    DOMAIN --- BD1[Boundary Segment Gamma 1: Dirichlet]
    DOMAIN --- BD2[Boundary Segment Gamma 2: Neumann]
    DOMAIN --- BD3[Boundary Segment Gamma 3: Cauchy]
    BD1 --- LAKE[Constant Head Lake h equals 50 m]
    BD2 --- WALL[Impermeable Wall q equals 0]
    BD3 --- LEAKY[Leaky Aquitard with alpha]
    style BD1 fill:#cce6ff
    style BD2 fill:#ffcccc
    style BD3 fill:#ccffcc
    style LAKE fill:#99ccff
    style WALL fill:#cc9999
    style LEAKY fill:#99cc99
```
