# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define Storativity (Storage Coefficient, $S$). What are its typical values for confined and unconfined aquifers? `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Storativity ($S$)** is defined as the volume of water released from storage per unit area of the aquifer per unit decline in the hydraulic head.
> Mathematically: $S = b\, S_s$, where $S_s$ = specific storage and $b$ = aquifer thickness.
>
> - **Confined aquifer:** $S$ ranges from **$1 \times 10^{-5}$ to $5 \times 10^{-3}$** (typically $\sim 10^{-4}$).
> - **Unconfined aquifer:** $S$ (= specific yield) ranges from **0.05 to 0.30** (typically 0.10–0.25).
>
> The huge difference is because confined aquifers store water through **elastic compression** of water and aquifer matrix, whereas unconfined aquifers store water by **gravity drainage**.

---

### Q2. State the assumptions of the Theis equation for unsteady radial flow towards a well. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> The assumptions of the Theis equation (1935) are:
> 1. Aquifer is **confined, homogeneous, isotropic, and of infinite areal extent**.
> 2. Well is **fully penetrating** with **infinitesimal diameter**.
> 3. Pumping rate $Q$ is **constant**.
> 4. Flow is **horizontal / radial** (Dupuit assumptions).
> 5. Water is released from storage **instantaneously** with decline in head.
> 6. Drawdown $s$ is **negligible** compared to initial aquifer thickness.
> 7. **Darcy's law** is valid throughout.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — `OR` Choice I** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the governing differential equation for unsteady radial flow in a confined aquifer. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A confined aquifer has $T = 200$ m²/day and $S = 2 \times 10^{-4}$. A well pumped at $Q = 1000$ m³/day. Compute the drawdown at a distance of $r = 80$ m after $t = 5$ days using (i) the Theis equation, and (ii) the Cooper-Jacob approximation. Given $W(0.04) = 2.681$ and $W(0.004) = 4.948$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Step 1 — Apply Darcy's law in radial form:**

$$Q = 2\pi r b K \frac{\partial h}{\partial r} = 2\pi r T \frac{\partial h}{\partial r}$$

In terms of drawdown $s = h_0 - h$:

$$Q = -2\pi r T \frac{\partial s}{\partial r}$$

**Step 2 — Continuity for an annular ring of inner radius $r$, outer radius $r+dr$, height $b$:**

$$\left[Q_r - Q_{r+dr}\right] dt = (2\pi r\, dr \cdot b) S_s\, ds$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate Darcy's expression:**

$$\frac{\partial Q}{\partial r} = -2\pi T\left[\frac{\partial s}{\partial r} + r\frac{\partial^2 s}{\partial r^2}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Substitute into continuity equation, divide by $2\pi r\, dr\, dt$:**

$$T\left[\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial s}{\partial r} + \frac{\partial^2 s}{\partial r^2}\right] = b S_s \frac{\partial s}{\partial t}$$

Using $S = b S_s$:

$$\boxed{\frac{\partial^2 s}{\partial r^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial s}{\partial r} = \frac{S}{T}\frac{\partial s}{\partial t}}$$

This is the **radial diffusion equation** governing unsteady confined-aquifer flow.

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1 — Compute $u$ at $t = 5$ days, $r = 80$ m:**

$$u = \frac{r^2 S}{4Tt} = \frac{(80)^2 \cdot 2\times 10^{-4}}{4 \cdot 200 \cdot 5} = \frac{1.28}{4000} = 3.2 \times 10^{-4} = 0.00032$$

Since the standard table gives $W(0.004) = 4.948$ (which is the closest tabulated value above 0.00032), we use $W(0.00032) \approx 6.45$ by interpolation/extrapolation using the late-time approximation $W(u) \approx -\ln u - 0.5772$:

$$W(0.00032) = -\ln(0.00032) - 0.5772 = 8.047 - 0.5772 = 7.47$$

**Step 2 — Theis drawdown:**

$$s = \frac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u) = \frac{1000}{4\pi (200)} \cdot 7.47 = \frac{7470}{2513.3} = 2.97 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Cooper-Jacob check (since $u = 0.00032 < 0.01$ ✓):**

$$s = \frac{0.183 \cdot 1000}{200} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.25 \cdot 200 \cdot 5}{(80)^2 \cdot 2\times 10^{-4}}\right)$$

$$= 0.915 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2250}{1.28}\right) = 0.915 \cdot \log_{10}(1757.8)$$

$$= 0.915 \cdot 3.245 = 2.97 \text{ m}$$

**Both methods give $s \approx 2.97$ m** — perfect agreement because $u$ is well below 0.01.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — `OR` Choice II** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** What is image well theory? With neat sketches, explain how it is used to compute drawdown near (i) a recharge boundary and (ii) an impermeable boundary. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A well is located 200 m from a straight river. The aquifer has $T = 500$ m²/day and $S = 5 \times 10^{-4}$. The well pumps at $Q = 2000$ m³/day. Compute the drawdown at a point P located 100 m from the well on the side **away** from the river, after 2 days of pumping. Use $W(0.05) = 2.467$. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Image well theory** replaces an aquifer boundary by an imaginary well (image well) placed symmetrically on the opposite side. The boundary is the **locus of zero drawdown** (recharge) or **zero flow** (impermeable).

**Case (i) — Recharge Boundary (River):**
A **recharge image well** of equal discharge $Q$ is placed at the mirror-image position. The image well **injects** water (negative pumping). The drawdown at any point P is:

$$s_P = s_{real}(r) - s_{image}(\rho) = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_r) - W(u_\rho)\bigr]$$

**Case (ii) — Impermeable Boundary (Fault):**
An **image well of equal magnitude** acts as a *discharge* well. The boundary becomes a line of no flow by symmetry. The drawdown is:

$$s_P = s_{real}(r) + s_{image}(\rho) = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_r) + W(u_\rho)\bigr]$$

*See Block Diagram in Section 4.2 for the conceptual flow.*

---

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Geometry:**
- Real well: 200 m from river.
- Point P: 100 m from real well, on the side away from river.
- Therefore, point P is **300 m from the river**.
- Image well (recharge type) is on the *opposite* side of the river from real well → distance from P to image well:

$$\rho = 100 + 2(200) = 500 \text{ m}$$

*Wait — correct by geometry:*

Real well at $x = 0$, river at $x = 200$, P at $x = -100$ (opposite side of real well from river). Image well at $x = +400$ (mirror of real across river).

$$r = 100 \text{ m (real → P)}, \quad \rho = 400 - (-100) = 500 \text{ m (image → P)}$$

**Step 1 — Compute $u_r$ and $u_\rho$ at $t = 2$ days:**

$$u_r = \frac{(100)^2 \cdot 5\times 10^{-4}}{4 \cdot 500 \cdot 2} = \frac{5}{4000} = 0.00125$$

$$u_\rho = \frac{(500)^2 \cdot 5\times 10^{-4}}{4 \cdot 500 \cdot 2} = \frac{125}{4000} = 0.03125$$

**Step 2 — Well functions (use $W(u) \approx -\ln u - 0.5772$ since both $u$ values are small):**

$$W(0.00125) = -\ln(0.00125) - 0.5772 = 6.685 - 0.5772 = 6.108$$

$$W(0.03125) = -\ln(0.03125) - 0.5772 = 3.466 - 0.5772 = 2.889$$

**Step 3 — Recharge-boundary drawdown at P:**

$$s_P = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_r) - W(u_\rho)\bigr] = \frac{2000}{4\pi(500)}\cdot(6.108 - 2.889)$$

$$= \frac{2000}{6283.2}\cdot 3.219 = 0.3183 \cdot 3.219 = 1.025 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answer:** $s_P \approx 1.02$ m (much less than a well of same $Q$ in an infinite aquifer, due to river recharge).

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly lose marks in Unsteady Radial Flow problems:**
>
> 1. **Mixing units:** Always use $Q$ in m³/day, $T$ in m²/day, $r$ in m, $t$ in days. *Do not* mix cgs/SI with field units.
> 2. **Forgetting the 0.183 vs 0.366 factor:** Use $0.183$ for time-drawdown (1 log cycle) and $0.366$ for distance-drawdown. Confusing them is the #1 mistake.
> 3. **Applying Cooper-Jacob when $u > 0.01$:** Check the $u$ value **first**; if too large, the straight-line formula gives a wrong answer.
> 4. **Image-well sign error:** Recharge boundary → **subtract**; impermeable boundary → **add**. Students get this backwards frequently.
> 5. **Theis recovery formula:** Use $\log(t/t')$, **not** $\log(t')$. Many students invert the ratio.
> 6. **Skipping state of units in $T = 0.183 Q / \Delta s$:** Always write the units explicitly in the final answer.
> 7. **Boundary-condition statements in derivations:** Examiners want all three (initial, inner, outer) — missing one costs 2 marks.

---

## 5.X Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-minute revision essentials for KTU exam:**
>
> - ✅ **Theis equation** = $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u)$, with $u = \dfrac{r^2 S}{4Tt}$.
> - ✅ **Cooper-Jacob (time-drawdown)** = $s = \dfrac{0.183\, Q}{T} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2.25\, T t}{r^2 S}\right)$; valid for $u \le 0.01$.
> - ✅ **Cooper-Jacob (distance-drawdown)** = $s = \dfrac{0.366\, Q}{T} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2.25\, T t}{r^2}{r_1}\right)$ — note the 0.366 (not 0.183) for distance-drawdown per log cycle.
> - ✅ **Slope of $s$ vs $\log t$** = $\Delta s$ per log cycle → $T = 0.183\, Q / \Delta s$.
> - ✅ **Intercept $t_0$** (where $s = 0$) → $S = 2.25\, T t_0 / r^2$.
> - ✅ **Theis recovery** = $s' = 0.183\,(Q/T) \log_{10}(t/t')$.
> - ✅ **Image well — river** (recharge) → subtract image drawdown.
> - ✅ **Image well — fault** (impermeable) → add image drawdown.
> - ✅ **PDE to remember** = $\dfrac{\partial^2 s}{\partial r^2} + \dfrac{1}{r}\dfrac{\partial s}{\partial r} = \dfrac{S}{T}\dfrac{\partial s}{\partial t}$.
> - ✅ **Storativity of confined aquifer** ≪ unconfined aquifer (10⁻⁴ vs 0.1).
> - ✅ **Transmissivity units** in KTU: m²/day.
> - ✅ Always check **validity of $u \le 0.01$** before applying Cooper-Jacob.
> - ✅ Recovery test **eliminates well loss** — use it for reliable $T$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Well Hydraulics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Well Hydraulics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Well Hydraulics** is the branch of groundwater engineering that mathematically describes the flow of water toward a well during pumping, and the corresponding drawdown (lowering of the water table or piezometric surface) in the surrounding aquifer. It combines principles of fluid mechanics, hydrogeology, and mathematics to predict well yield, radius of influence, and aquifer parameters (Transmissivity **T** and Storativity **S**).

**Key Terms (KTU Syllabus Vocabulary):**
- **Drawdown (s):** The vertical distance the water level (or piezometric head) drops from its static level at a point due to pumping. Unit: **meters (m)** or **m**.
- **Cone of Depression (C.O.D):** The 3-D funnel-shaped depression formed in the water table/piezometric surface around a pumping well.
- **Radius of Influence (R):** The radial distance from the well center beyond which drawdown is practically zero. Typical values: **100 m to 1000 m**.
- **Steady-State Flow:** A condition where drawdown at any point does **not change with time** (∂s/∂t = 0).
- **Unsteady-State (Transient) Flow:** A condition where drawdown changes with time (∂s/∂t ≠ 0).

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Honey Dipper"

Imagine you insert a straw (the well) into a jar of thick honey (the aquifer). When you suck, the honey right at the straw moves up first. As you continue sucking, honey from farther away starts flowing toward the straw, and the surface of the honey near the straw dips down, forming a small valley. 

- The **deeper you suck**, the wider the valley becomes (larger cone of depression).
- The **farther** the honey is from the straw, the **less** it dips (drawdown reduces with radial distance).
- If honey keeps getting supplied from below or from the sides (recharge), the surface stabilizes — this is the **steady state**.
- If the jar is sealed, the level keeps dropping forever — this is the **unsteady state**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The fundamental governing equation for all well hydraulics is the combination of Darcy's Law and the Continuity Equation**, applied in radial cylindrical coordinates because flow into a well is naturally **radial** (towards a point).

---

### 1.3 Classification of Aquifers Encountered in Well Problems

| Aquifer Type | Upper Boundary | Lower Boundary | Flow Assumption |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Confined** | Impermeable (aquiclude) | Impermeable (aquiclude) | Radial through saturated thickness **b** |
| **Unconfined** | Water Table (free surface) | Impermeable (aquiclude) | Radial through variable thickness h |
| **Leaky** | Aquitard + recharge | Impermeable | Radial + vertical leakage |
| **Perched** | Local aquiclude | Impermeable | Localized, often ignored in exam |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic Flow for Radial Flow to a Well

**Why is the flow Radial?**
When a vertical well is pumped, water must travel from all directions (360°) toward the well axis. By symmetry, the equipotential surfaces form concentric cylinders around the well. Hence, the flow net consists of concentric **radial flow lines** and **cylindrical equipotentials**.

**The "Why" Behind the Equations:**

1. **Darcy's Law in Radial Form:** Original Darcy is linear (q = Ki). For radial flow through a cylindrical surface of radius r and height b (confined), discharge Q passes through area A = 2πrb. Thus:
   
   $$q = K \cdot \frac{ds}{dr} \implies Q = (2 \pi r b) \cdot K \cdot \frac{ds}{dr}$$

   *Why area = 2πrb?* Because the flow front is the curved surface of a cylinder, not a flat plane.

2. **Steady State Assumption (∂s/∂t = 0):** This means the cone of depression has stabilized. Aquifer properties (T, b) remain constant. Recharge equals pumping rate.

3. **Boundary Conditions:**
   - At r = r_w (well face): drawdown = s_w
   - At r = R (radius of influence): drawdown = 0

4. **Integration:** The differential equation is separable. Integrating from r_w to R gives the classical **Dupuit/Thiem Equation**.

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Confined Aquifer)

| Formula Name | Expression | Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dupuit-Thiem Equation (Steady, Confined)** | $s = \dfrac{Q}{2 \pi T} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r}\right) = \dfrac{2.303 \, Q}{2 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r}\right)$ | Q = discharge, T = transmissivity, R = radius of influence, r = radial distance | s in m, Q in m³/s, T in m²/s |
| **Transmissivity** | $T = K \cdot b$ | K = hydraulic conductivity, b = aquifer thickness | m²/s |
| **Two-Well Method (Confined)** | $T = \dfrac{2.303 \, Q}{2 \pi (s_1 - s_2)} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | s₁, s₂ at r₁, r₂ | m²/s |
| **Specific Capacity** | $S_c = \dfrac{Q}{s_w}$ | Q = pumping rate, s_w = drawdown at well | m²/s or L/s/m |
| **Well Efficiency** | $\eta_w = \dfrac{s_{ideal}}{s_{actual}} \times 100$ | s_ideal = theoretical, s_actual = observed | Percent (%) |
| **Radius of Influence (Sichardt)** | $R = 3000 \, s_w \sqrt{K}$ | s_w in m, K in m/s | m |
| **Drawdown Component** | $s_{total} = s_{formation} + s_{well-loss}$ | Formation + well losses | m |
| **Jacob's Equation (s_w)** | $s_w = \dfrac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2.25 \, T t}{r_w^2 S}\right)$ | t = time, S = storativity | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTB Memory Trick:** For confined steady state, always use the natural log form. For unconfined steady state, the equation is **NON-LINEAR** because saturated thickness changes with drawdown.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Unconfined Aquifer)

| Formula Name | Expression | Key Insight |
|---|---|---|
| **Dupuit Equation (Unconfined)** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi K (H^2 - h^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)} = \dfrac{1.366 \, K (H^2 - h^2)}{\log_{10}(R/r_w)}$ | H = static, h = dynamic water table |
| **Drawdown in terms of h** | $s = H - h$ | Since drawdown reduces the saturated thickness |
| **Transmissivity (Unconfined)** | $T = K \cdot h_{avg}$ | h_avg = (H + h) / 2 |
| **Maximum Yield** | $Q_{max} = \dfrac{\pi K H^2}{2 \ln(R/r_w)}$ | Occurs when h = H/2 (50% drawdown) |

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet — Unsteady Flow (Theis & Jacob)

| Formula Name | Expression | Used For |
|---|---|---|
| **Theis Equation (1935)** | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4 \pi T} \cdot W(u)$ | General transient, all u values |
| **Well Function W(u)** | $W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln(u) + u - \dfrac{u^2}{2 \cdot 2!} + \dfrac{u^3}{3 \cdot 3!} - \dots$ | Computed from series |
| **u Parameter** | $u = \dfrac{r^2 S}{4 T t}$ | Dimensionless time parameter |
| **Jacob's Approximation** | $s = \dfrac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{2.25 \, T t}{r^2 S}\right)$ | Valid when u < 0.01 |
| **Cooper-Jacob Method** | $\Delta s \,(\text{per log cycle}) = \dfrac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T}$ | Straight-line method on s vs log(t) |
| **Recovery Equation** | $s' = \dfrac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{t'}\right)$ | s' = residual drawdown, t' = recovery time |
| **Image Well Theory** | Mirror well across boundary with ±Q sign | Recharge (–) or barrier (+) boundary |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Design of Production Wells:** Determines pump depth, screen length, and required yield.
- **Aquifer Testing (Pump Tests):** Estimates T and S, vital for water supply projects.
- **Contaminant Plume Tracking:** Predicts how pollutants move toward extraction wells.
- **Land Subsidence Studies:** Quantifies drawdown to predict consolidation.
- **Dewatering for Construction:** Designing deep well dewatering systems for excavations.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Dupuit-Thiem Equation (Steady Radial Flow in Confined Aquifer)

**Starting Point — Darcy's Law in Radial Coordinates:**

For a fully penetrating well pumping at steady rate Q from a confined aquifer of thickness b, water flows radially inward through a cylindrical surface of radius r and area A = 2πrb.

By Darcy's Law (radial form):

$$Q = K \cdot i \cdot A = K \cdot \frac{ds}{dr} \cdot (2 \pi r b)$$

*Where i = ds/dr is the hydraulic gradient along the radial direction. The negative sign convention is absorbed by the direction of r.*

**Rearrange to separate variables:**

$$Q \cdot \frac{dr}{r} = 2 \pi T \, ds$$

*Here we substitute T = Kb (transmissivity of the confined aquifer), and note that ds is the differential drawdown increment.*

**Integrate between the two known boundaries:**

- Lower limit: at r = r_w, drawdown = s_w (at well face)
- Upper limit: at r = R, drawdown = 0 (at radius of influence)

$$\int_{r_w}^{R} Q \, \frac{dr}{r} = \int_{s_w}^{0} 2 \pi T \, ds$$

**Evaluate both integrals:**

The left side gives Q · ln(R/r_w) and the right side gives 2πT · (0 − s_w) = −2πT s_w.

$$Q \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_w}\right) = -2 \pi T \, s_w$$

**Solve for s_w (drawdown at the well):**

$$s_w = \frac{Q}{2 \pi T} \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_w}\right)$$

**Convert to common log (log₁₀) for engineering convenience using ln(x) = 2.303 log₁₀(x):**

$$s_w = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{2 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R}{r_w}\right)$$

**General form at any radius r (where s is the drawdown):**

$$\boxed{s = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{2 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R}{r}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** This equation is **linear in s vs log(r)**. This is the principle used in the **two-observation well method** to find T from field data.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Dupuit Equation for Unconfined Aquifer (Steady State)

For an unconfined aquifer, the saturated thickness is **NOT constant** — it changes with drawdown. At radius r, the saturated thickness is h (measured from the impermeable base to the water table).

**Apply Darcy's Law in radial form using h as the variable thickness:**

$$Q = 2 \pi r h K \frac{ds}{dr}$$

Here, ds/dr = dh/dr because drawdown s = H − h, so ds = −dh, and along the direction of increasing r (moving outward), h increases, so we write:

$$Q = -2 \pi r K h \frac{dh}{dr}$$

**Separate variables and integrate:**

$$Q \, \frac{dr}{r} = -2 \pi K h \, dh$$

Integrate from r_w to R and corresponding h to H:

$$\int_{r_w}^{R} Q \, \frac{dr}{r} = -2 \pi K \int_{h}^{H} h \, dh$$

**Evaluate:**

$$Q \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{r_w}\right) = -2 \pi K \left[\frac{H^2 - h^2}{2}\right] = -\pi K (H^2 - h^2)$$

**Solve for Q:**

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi K (H^2 - h^2)}{\ln(R/r_w)} = \frac{1.366 \, K (H^2 - h^2)}{\log_{10}(R/r_w)}}$$

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Theis Equation (Unsteady Radial Flow)

**Step 1 — Governing PDE:**

The 3-D groundwater flow equation in radial coordinates (with full vertical integration over aquifer thickness) reduces to the radial diffusivity equation:

$$\frac{\partial^2 s}{\partial r^2} + \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial s}{\partial r} = \frac{S}{T} \frac{\partial s}{\partial t}$$

**Step 2 — Assumptions:**
- Infinite, homogeneous, isotropic, confined aquifer.
- Well is a perfect line source (penetrates full thickness).
- Water released instantaneously from storage (specific storage S_s).
- Discharge Q is constant.

**Step 3 — Boundary & Initial Conditions:**

- s(r, 0) = 0 for all r > 0 (initially no drawdown)
- s(∞, t) = 0 (infinite aquifer)
- $\lim_{r \to 0} \left( r \frac{\partial s}{\partial r} \right) = \frac{Q}{2 \pi T}$ (well flux condition)

**Step 4 — Solution using Laplace and Hankel Transforms:**

Theis (1935) applied the method of images and used a sink term Q at r = 0. The solution is expressed in terms of the exponential integral (well function):

$$s(r, t) = \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} W(u)$$

Where the dimensionless variables are:

$$u = \frac{r^2 S}{4 T t}, \quad W(u) = \int_{u}^{\infty} \frac{e^{-y}}{y} dy$$

**Step 5 — Series Expansion of W(u):**

Expanding e^(−y) as a power series and integrating term by term:

$$W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln(u) + u - \frac{u^2}{2 \cdot 2!} + \frac{u^3}{3 \cdot 3!} - \frac{u^4}{4 \cdot 4!} + \dots$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **−0.5772 is the Euler-Mascheroni Constant** — a fixed mathematical constant, not an empirical coefficient.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Jacob's Approximation (from Theis)

For small values of u (u < 0.01), all higher-order terms in the series become negligible. The Theis equation reduces to:

$$s \approx \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} \left[ -0.5772 - \ln(u) \right]$$

Substitute u = r²S / (4Tt) and use ln(x) = 2.303 log₁₀(x):

$$s = \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{4 T t}{r^2 S}\right) - 0.5772 \right]$$

Combine the constants (since e^0.5772 = 1.781, the term 4 × 1.781 = 2.25):

$$s = \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} \ln\!\left(\frac{2.25 \, T t}{r^2 S}\right) = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.25 \, T t}{r^2 S}\right)$$

**Significance:** This gives a **straight line** when s is plotted against log₁₀(t). The slope of this line = 2.303Q/(4πT), enabling field determination of T. The intercept gives S.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5: Image Well Theory (Boundary Effects)

**Concept:** To model a real boundary, an "image" well is placed as a mirror image on the opposite side. The principle of superposition applies.

| Boundary Type | Image Well | Effect |
|---|---|---|
| **Recharge Boundary** (river) | Image well with **–Q** (injection) | Reduces drawdown, streamlines real well |
| **Barrier Boundary** (impermeable fault) | Image well with **+Q** (pumping) | Increases drawdown, streamlines repel |

**Mathematical Statement (Recharge Boundary at distance L):**

$$s_{total} = \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} \left[ W\!\left(\frac{r_1^2 S}{4 T t}\right) - W\!\left(\frac{r_2^2 S}{4 T t}\right) \right]$$

Where:
- r₁ = distance from **real well** to observation point
- r₂ = distance from **image well** to observation point

The negative sign for the image (recharge) cancels the drawdown contribution; positive sign (barrier) doubles it.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define the terms: (i) Drawdown, (ii) Cone of Depression, and (iii) Radius of Influence.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Drawdown (s):** The vertical drop in the water level (in a water table aquifer) or the piezometric surface (in a confined aquifer) at a point due to pumping, measured from the original static level. Unit: metres.
- **Cone of Depression:** The funnel-shaped depression in the water table or piezometric surface that forms around a pumping well. The vertex is at the well, and the surface slopes radially outward.
- **Radius of Influence (R):** The radial distance from the centre of the well beyond which the drawdown is effectively zero. Mathematically, at r = R, s = 0.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2023] State the assumptions of the Dupuit-Thiem equation for steady radial flow in a confined aquifer.**

**Model Answer:**
1. The aquifer is **homogeneous, isotropic**, and has a constant thickness **b**.
2. The aquifer is **confined** between two impermeable layers.
3. Flow is **steady** (∂s/∂t = 0) and **radial** (toward the well).
4. The well **fully penetrates** the aquifer thickness.
5. The well is pumped at a **constant discharge Q**.
6. The aquifer is **infinite** in horizontal extent (or has a clearly defined radius of influence R).
7. Darcy's Law is valid (laminar flow).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]**

**Question A:**
**(a)** Derive the Dupuit equation for steady radial flow in a **confined aquifer** and explain its significance. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** A fully penetrating well in a confined aquifer pumps at **1500 L/min**. The drawdown at the well face is **3.0 m**, and at a distance of **60 m** the drawdown is **1.2 m**. If the radius of influence is **300 m** and the aquifer thickness is **20 m**, calculate the **transmissivity** and **hydraulic conductivity**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation:**
Refer to **Section 3.1** of these notes. The complete derivation is given step-by-step from Darcy's law in radial form, separation of variables, integration with boundary conditions r = r_w (s = s_w) and r = R (s = 0), to the final boxed equation.

Final expression:

$$s = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{2 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R}{r}\right)$$

**Significance:**
- Predicts drawdown at any radial distance r from a pumping well.
- Used in the **two-observation-well method** to compute T from field data.
- Forms the basis for steady-state aquifer characterization.
- Combined with unconfined and leaky cases, it covers most practical situations.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Q = 1500 L/min = 1500 / 60000 m³/s = **0.025 m³/s**
- s_w = 3.0 m at r_w (well face, r_w ≈ 0.1 m typical — but we use the two-observation well method)
- s₁ = 1.2 m at r₁ = 60 m
- R = 300 m, b = 20 m

**Method — Two-Observation Well Form:**

We use the equation with two known drawdowns (s₁ at r₁, and s_w at r_w, with r_w assumed small; alternatively, use the r_w value directly):

$$T = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{2 \pi (s_w - s_1)} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_w}\right)$$

However, since r_w is not given, we use the **Thiem equation at both points and subtract**:

$$s_w - s_1 = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{2 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R}{r_w}\right) - \frac{2.303 \, Q}{2 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{R}{r_1}\right)$$

$$s_w - s_1 = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{2 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_w}\right)$$

**Apply R = 300 m as a substitute, and use the s-w value at the well face directly (assume r_w ≈ 0.1 m for this problem — typical assumption for screen radius).**

**Step 1 — Compute T:**

$$T = \frac{2.303 \times 0.025}{2 \pi \times (3.0 - 1.2)} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{60}{0.1}\right)$$

$$T = \frac{0.0576}{2 \pi \times 1.8} \log_{10}(600)$$

$$T = \frac{0.0576}{11.31} \times 2.778$$

$$T = 0.00509 \times 2.778 = 0.01414 \text{ m}^2/\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Compute K:**

$$K = \frac{T}{b} = \frac{0.01414}{20} = 7.07 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$

**Final Answer:**
- **T = 0.01414 m²/s = 1218 m²/day**
- **K = 7.07 × 10⁻⁴ m/s**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion:** Q must be in m³/s. Many students forget to convert L/min and lose 1 mark immediately.
> 2. **Logarithm Base:** Always use log₁₀ (common log), not ln. The factor 2.303 only works for base 10.
> 3. **Formula Choice:** Use the **two-observation well** form when only one drawdown is given, not the r_w form, unless r_w is explicitly given.

---

### 5.3 Part B — Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Question Bank 2024]**

**Question B:**
**(a)** Explain the **Theis equation** for unsteady radial flow. State its assumptions and discuss **Jacob's approximation**. **(7 Marks)**
**(b)** In a pumping test, the drawdown at an observation well located **50 m** from the pumping well is measured as follows: at **t = 10 min, s = 0.45 m** and at **t = 100 min, s = 1.05 m**. The discharge rate is **0.05 m³/min**. Determine the **transmissivity** and **storativity** of the aquifer using the **Cooper-Jacob straight-line method**. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theis Equation & Jacob's Approximation:**
The Theis equation for unsteady radial flow in a confined aquifer is:

$$s = \frac{Q}{4 \pi T} W(u), \quad \text{where } u = \frac{r^2 S}{4 T t}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Aquifer is confined, homogeneous, isotropic, infinite in extent, of constant thickness.
2. The well is fully penetrating, with infinitesimal diameter.
3. Water is released instantaneously from storage with constant storativity S.
4. Discharge Q is constant in time.

**Jacob's Approximation:**
For small u (u < 0.01, typically after a long pumping time), the higher-order terms in the W(u) series are negligible. The equation simplifies to:

$$s = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.25 \, T t}{r^2 S}\right)$$

This gives a **straight line** when s is plotted against log₁₀(t). The slope of this line is Δs per log cycle = 2.303Q/(4πT). Extrapolation to s = 0 gives t₀, from which S can be calculated.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- r = 50 m
- t₁ = 10 min, s₁ = 0.45 m
- t₂ = 100 min, s₂ = 1.05 m
- Q = 0.05 m³/min

**Step 1 — Slope of s vs log(t):**

$$\Delta s = s_2 - s_1 = 1.05 - 0.45 = 0.60 \text{ m (per one log cycle of t)}$$

**Step 2 — Compute T:**

$$T = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi \Delta s} = \frac{2.303 \times 0.05}{4 \pi \times 0.60}$$

$$T = \frac{0.1152}{7.540} = 0.01528 \text{ m}^2/\text{min} = 22.0 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}$$

**Step 3 — Compute t₀ (intercept at s = 0):**

The straight line equation passing through (t₁, s₁) with slope Δs per log cycle:

$$s_1 = \frac{2.303 \, Q}{4 \pi T} \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2.25 \, T t_1}{r^2 S}\right) = \text{slope} \times \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{t_1}{t_0}\right)$$

Therefore:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{t_1}{t_0}\right) = \frac{s_1}{\Delta s} = \frac{0.45}{0.60} = 0.75$$

$$\frac{t_1}{t_0} = 10^{0.75} = 5.623$$

$$t_0 = \frac{t_1}{5.623} = \frac{10}{5.623} = 1.778 \text{ min}$$

**Step 4 — Compute S:**

Using the relation at s = 0 (Jacob's formula):

$$t_0 = \frac{r^2 S}{2.25 \, T}$$

$$S = \frac{2.25 \, T \cdot t_0}{r^2} = \frac{2.25 \times 0.01528 \times 1.778}{50^2}$$

$$S = \frac{0.06115}{2500} = 2.446 \times 10^{-5}$$

**Final Answer:**
- **T = 0.0153 m²/min = 22.0 m²/day**
- **S = 2.45 × 10⁻⁵** (dimensionless, typical for confined aquifer range 10⁻⁵ to 10⁻³)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Time unit consistency:** t in minutes and T in m²/min must be consistent. If you convert t to seconds, convert T to m²/s.
> 2. **S in confined aquifers** is **small** (10⁻⁵ to 10⁻³). If your S comes out > 0.1, you likely have a calculation error.
> 3. **t₀ < t₁ always.** If t₀ > t₁, your sign or slope is wrong.
> 4. **Use the Jacob method ONLY when u < 0.01.** Verify: u = r²S/(4Tt). For early times this is violated.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Well Hydraulics (Module 3)**

- **Dupuit-Thiem (Confined):** $s = (2.303Q / 2\pi T) \log_{10}(R/r)$ — linear in log r.
- **Dupuit (Unconfined):** $Q = 1.366 K (H^2 - h^2) / \log_{10}(R/r_w)$ — **non-linear**, thickness varies.
- **Maximum yield in unconfined** occurs at **h = H/2** (50% drawdown rule).
- **Transmissivity T = Kb** (confined) or **T = K × h_avg** (unconfined).
- **Specific Capacity S_c = Q/s_w** — indicator of well productivity.
- **Well Efficiency η = s_ideal / s_actual × 100%** — typical good well > 70%.
- **Sichardt's formula:** $R = 3000 \, s_w \sqrt{K}$ (for unconfined aquifers).
- **Theis Equation:** $s = (Q/4\pi T) W(u)$ where $u = r^2 S / (4Tt)$.
- **Jacob's straight-line:** Plot s vs log t, slope = 2.303Q/(4πT), intercept t₀ gives S.
- **Jacob is valid only when u < 0.01** (late time approximation).
- **Cooper-Jacob Two-Well:** $T = (2.303Q / 2\pi \Delta s) \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$.
- **Image Well — Recharge boundary → Image has –Q (injector).**
- **Image Well — Barrier boundary → Image has +Q (pump).**
- **Recovery Equation:** $s' = (2.303Q/4\pi T) \log_{10}(t/t')$ — useful because it eliminates well losses.
- **Confined S range:** $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-3}$.
- **Unconfined S range:** 0.05 to 0.30 (Specific Yield Y).
- **KTB Memory Mnemonic — "TRACR"**: **T**ransmissivity, **R**adius of influence, **A**quifer type, **C**onfinement, **R**ecovery test.
- **Always carry units in your derivation steps** — KTU examiners award method marks for dimensional consistency.
- **Watch for partial penetration** — gives extra drawdown of $s_p = (Q^2 / 4\pi^2 b^2) \cdot (1/t - 1/r)$ form (Hantush's partial penetration equation).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Well Flow Near Aquifer Boundaries
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Well Flow Near Aquifer Boundaries

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Aquifer Boundary?

In real-world groundwater systems, an aquifer is **never infinite**. It terminates at physical hydrogeological boundaries that fundamentally alter the flow field generated by a pumping well. According to the KTU 2024 Ground Water Engineering syllabus, aquifer boundaries are classified into two principal types:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aquifer Boundary**: A physical or hydraulic discontinuity that limits the lateral extent of the aquifer and imposes a specific head or flux condition on the groundwater flow equation.

### Type 1 — Recharge Boundary (Constant-Head Boundary)
A boundary along which the **hydraulic head remains constant** in time, irrespective of pumping. Classic examples include:
- Perennial rivers, lakes, and reservoirs hydraulically connected to the aquifer
- Highly permeable formations in contact with a free water surface

> [!NOTE]
> Mathematically, the condition is **s = 0** at the boundary (drawdown is zero).

### Type 2 — Barrier Boundary (Impermeable / No-Flow Boundary)
A boundary across which **no groundwater flow can occur** (q = 0). Common examples include:
- Geological faults with gouge material
- Tight sheet piles or underground cut-off walls
- Sharp contact between the aquifer and an aquitard

> [!NOTE]
> Mathematically, the condition is **∂h/∂n = 0** at the boundary (no flow perpendicular to it).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mirror in a Room"

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition: Think of a wall and a light bulb.**
> Imagine you are standing in a dark room with a bright bulb. The bulb illuminates only one side of the wall. Now place a **mirror exactly on the wall**. The mirror creates a *virtual* second bulb behind the wall, and an observer inside the room cannot tell whether there is one real bulb and a mirror, or two real bulbs. The light intensity (analogous to drawdown) at any point inside the room is the **sum of the contribution from the real bulb and the contribution from the mirror-image bulb**.
>
> - If the wall is a **perfect mirror** (recharge boundary — head cannot change), the mirror image is a **virtual recharge well** (opposite sign).
> - If the wall is a **black absorber** that simply blocks light (barrier boundary — no flow), the mirror image is a **virtual pumping well** (same sign).

This is the celebrated **Method of Images**, attributed to Lord Kelvin (Thomson, 1848) and first applied to groundwater by Ferris (1959).

## 1.3 Standard Notation Used

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Q | Pumping rate of real well | m³/day |
| T | Transmissivity | m²/day |
| S | Storativity | dimensionless |
| t | Pumping time | day |
| b | Perpendicular distance from well to boundary | m |
| r₁ | Distance from real well to observation point | m |
| r₂ | Distance from image well to observation point | m |
| R | Radius of influence (steady-state) | m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Method of Images — The Core Idea

The Method of Images is a **mathematical trick** based on the principle of superposition. The governing groundwater flow equation (Theis / Laplace form) is **linear**, so we can add solutions. To satisfy the boundary condition, we invent a fictitious "image well" placed at the mirror-image position of the real well across the boundary.

**Operational Logic in Five Steps:**

1. **Locate the real well** at perpendicular distance *b* from the boundary.
2. **Reflect the well** across the boundary to get the image well location.
3. **Assign a sign to the image well** depending on the boundary type (see table below).
4. **Calculate drawdown** at any point P using superposition: *s*<sub>total</sub> = *s*<sub>real</sub> ± *s*<sub>image</sub>.
5. **Verify** that the boundary condition (s = 0 for recharge, ∂s/∂n = 0 for barrier) is satisfied at the boundary line.

## 2.2 Sign Convention for the Image Well

| Boundary Type | Real Well Discharge | Image Well Discharge | Superposition Formula |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Recharge (Constant Head)** | +Q (pumping) | −Q (recharging) | s = s_real − s_image |
| **Barrier (Impermeable)** | +Q (pumping) | +Q (pumping) | s = s_real + s_image |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **physical meaning** is striking: a recharge boundary *suppresses* drawdown (water flows in to replenish), while a barrier boundary *amplifies* drawdown (no inflow, so the well "starves" the cone of depression).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Complete Summary

| # | Situation | Governing Equation | Validity |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Theis (single well, infinite aquifer) | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u), \quad u = \dfrac{r^2 S}{4Tt}$ | Transient |
| 2 | Jacob (u < 0.01) | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2.25 Tt}{r^2 S}\right)$ | Late transient |
| 3 | Steady-state single well | $s = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r}\right)$ | Steady state |
| 4 | Recharge boundary, transient | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} \left[ W(u_1) - W(u_2) \right]$ | Transient |
| 5 | Recharge boundary, Jacob | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_2^{\,2}}{r_1^{\,2}}\right) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | Late time |
| 6 | Recharge boundary, steady | $s = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | Steady state |
| 7 | Barrier boundary, transient | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} \left[ W(u_1) + W(u_2) \right]$ | Transient |
| 8 | Barrier boundary, steady | $s = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\left(\dfrac{R^2}{r_1\, r_2}\right)$ | Steady state |
| 9 | u₁ (real), u₂ (image) | $u_1 = \dfrac{r_1^{\,2} S}{4Tt}, \quad u_2 = \dfrac{r_2^{\,2} S}{4Tt}$ | Auxiliary |
| 10 | Corner with two recharge boundaries (90°) | 3 image wells, all with −Q | Multi-image |
| 11 | Corner with two barrier boundaries (90°) | 3 image wells, all with +Q | Multi-image |
| 12 | General angle θ corner | $\left(\dfrac{360°}{\theta} - 1\right)$ image wells | Multi-image |

> [!NOTE]
> The notation $r_2$ in the recharge/boundary formulas above is **NOT** the conventional "radius of influence" R; it is the **distance from the image well to the observation point**. Do not confuse the two.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Municipal water supply design**: A well field located near a perennial river benefits from the induced infiltration from the river — a recharge boundary effect. Engineers size the well capacity using the *reduced* drawdown formula.
- **Construction dewatering**: Dewatering wells placed near a buried sheet-pile wall must account for *doubled* drawdown (barrier effect) to avoid over-pumping and settlement of nearby structures.
- **Contaminant remediation**: Understanding boundary effects is crucial for designing pump-and-treat systems; a barrier boundary can trap contaminants in the cone of depression, requiring additional extraction wells.
- **Seawater intrusion studies**: The coastline acts as a constant-head boundary; the method of images helps predict inland migration of the saline-fresh water interface.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Drawdown Equation for a Well Near a Recharge Boundary

**Setup**: A fully penetrating well of discharge Q pumps from a confined aquifer of infinite extent, situated at perpendicular distance *b* from a straight recharge boundary. An observation point P is at distances *r₁* from the real well and *r₂* from the image well.

### Step 1 — Theis equation for the real well alone
The classical Theis solution for a single well in an infinite confined aquifer is

$$
s_{\text{real}} = \frac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u_1)
$$

where the dimensionless time parameter is

$$
u_1 = \frac{r_1^{\,2} S}{4 T t}
$$

### Step 2 — Theis equation for the image well alone
The image well is located at the mirror position. Its discharge is **−Q** (it recharges at rate Q). By substitution,

$$
s_{\text{image}} = \frac{(-Q)}{4\pi T} W(u_2) = -\frac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u_2)
$$

with

$$
u_2 = \frac{r_2^{\,2} S}{4 T t}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply superposition (linearity of governing equation)
The total drawdown at P is the algebraic sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{\text{total}} &= s_{\text{real}} + s_{\text{image}} \\
&= \frac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u_1) - \frac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u_2) \\
&= \frac{Q}{4\pi T} \left[ W(u_1) - W(u_2) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify the boundary condition
On the recharge boundary, by symmetry of the mirror image, $r_1 = r_2$ and $u_1 = u_2$, so

$$
s_{\text{boundary}} = \frac{Q}{4\pi T} [W(u) - W(u)] = 0 \quad \checkmark
$$

This confirms the constant-head condition is satisfied.

### Step 5 — Jacob's late-time approximation
For $u_1 < 0.01$ and $u_2 < 0.01$, the well function reduces to $W(u) \approx -0.5772 - \ln(u) = \ln(2.25Tt/(r^2 S))$. Substituting,

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{\text{total}} &= \frac{Q}{4\pi T} \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{2.25 Tt}{r_1^{\,2} S}\right) - \ln\!\left(\frac{2.25 Tt}{r_2^{\,2} S}\right) \right] \\
&= \frac{Q}{4\pi T} \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{2.25 Tt}{r_1^{\,2} S}\right) - \ln\!\left(\frac{2.25 Tt}{r_2^{\,2} S}\right) \right] \\
&= \frac{Q}{4\pi T} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2^{\,2}}{r_1^{\,2}}\right) \\
&= \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight**: Drawdown depends *only* on the ratio of distances — it is **time-independent** at late times. This is called the *steady-state* or *equilibrium* drawdown distribution for a recharge boundary.

## 3.2 Derivation of Drawdown Equation for a Well Near a Barrier Boundary

**Setup**: Same geometry, but the boundary is impermeable (∂s/∂n = 0).

### Step 1 — Image well has discharge +Q
To satisfy the no-flow condition, the image well must pump at the **same** rate as the real well. This is because the perpendicular components of drawdown gradient at the boundary line are equal and opposite, hence cancel.

### Step 2 — Superposition

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{\text{total}} &= s_{\text{real}} + s_{\text{image}} \\
&= \frac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u_1) + \frac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u_2) \\
&= \frac{Q}{4\pi T} \left[ W(u_1) + W(u_2) \right]
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Steady-state (Jacob) form
For late time, using $W(u) \approx \ln(2.25Tt/(r^2 S))$,

$$
\begin{aligned}
s_{\text{total}} &= \frac{Q}{4\pi T} \left[ \ln\!\left(\frac{2.25 Tt}{r_1^{\,2} S}\right) + \ln\!\left(\frac{2.25 Tt}{r_2^{\,2} S}\right) \right] \\
&= \frac{Q}{4\pi T} \ln\!\left(\frac{(2.25 Tt)^2}{r_1^{\,2} r_2^{\,2} S^2}\right) \\
&= \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\left(\frac{2.25 Tt}{S \sqrt{r_1 r_2}^{\,2}}\right)
\end{aligned}
$$

In the steady-state limit (introducing the radius of influence *R*),

$$
\boxed{\, s_{\text{barrier}} = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\left(\frac{R^2}{r_1 r_2}\right) \,}
$$

### Step 4 — Verify barrier boundary condition
On the boundary, $r_1 = r_2 = b$, so

$$
s_{\text{boundary}} = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\left(\frac{R^2}{b^2}\right) = 2 \cdot \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\left(\frac{R}{b}\right)
$$

This is exactly **twice** the single-well drawdown — confirming the amplification effect of a barrier.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example

**Problem (KTU Model)**: A well pumping at $Q = 1500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ is located at $b = 50 \text{ m}$ from a recharge boundary in a confined aquifer with $T = 500 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}$ and $S = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$. Compute the drawdown at a point 30 m from the real well, on the line connecting the well perpendicular to the boundary (between the well and the boundary), after $t = 20 \text{ days}$ of continuous pumping.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Distances**
- $r_1 = 30 \text{ m}$ (real well to observation point)
- $r_2 = 2b - r_1 = 100 - 30 = 70 \text{ m}$ (image well to observation point)

**Step 2 — Compute $u_1$ and $u_2$**

$$
\begin{aligned}
u_1 &= \frac{r_1^{\,2} S}{4 T t} = \frac{30^2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 500 \times 20} = \frac{0.135}{40000} = 3.375 \times 10^{-6} \\
u_2 &= \frac{r_2^{\,2} S}{4 T t} = \frac{70^2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 500 \times 20} = \frac{0.735}{40000} = 1.838 \times 10^{-5}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify Jacob's approximation is valid**
Both $u_1$ and $u_2$ are well below 0.01, so we can safely use:

$$
W(u) \approx -0.5772 - \ln(u)
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the well functions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W(u_1) &= -0.5772 - \ln(3.375 \times 10^{-6}) = -0.5772 + 12.598 = 12.021 \\
W(u_2) &= -0.5772 - \ln(1.838 \times 10^{-5}) = -0.5772 + 10.905 = 10.328
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the drawdown (recharge boundary formula)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
s &= \frac{Q}{4\pi T} \left[ W(u_1) - W(u_2) \right] \\
&= \frac{1500}{4\pi \times 500} \left[ 12.021 - 10.328 \right] \\
&= \frac{1500}{6283.19} \times 1.693 \\
&= 0.2387 \times 1.693 \\
&\approx 0.404 \text{ m}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification with Jacob's explicit form**:

$$
s = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) = \frac{1500}{2\pi \times 500} \ln\!\left(\frac{70}{30}\right) = 0.4775 \times 0.8473 \approx 0.405 \text{ m}
$$

The two methods agree to within rounding error. ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Method of Images Decision Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RealWell[Real Well pumping Q at distance b from boundary] --> RealDraw[Real Well Drawdown s1 at Point P]
    ImageWell[Image Well at mirror position across boundary] --> ImageEffect[Image Well Effect s2 at Point P]
    BoundaryType[Step 1 Identify Boundary Type] --> BType{Recharge or Barrier}
    BType -->|Recharge Constant Head| RechargeImage[Step 2a Assign Image Q Im equals minus Q]
    BType -->|Barrier Impermeable| BarrierImage[Step 2b Assign Image Q Im equals plus Q]
    RechargeImage --> ImageWell
    BarrierImage --> ImageWell
    RealDraw --> SuperRecharge[Step 3a Superposition Recharge s equals s1 minus s2]
    ImageEffect --> SuperRecharge
    RealDraw --> SuperBarrier[Step 3b Superposition Barrier s equals s1 plus s2]
    ImageEffect --> SuperBarrier
    SuperRecharge --> Check1[Boundary Check s equals 0 on boundary Line]
    SuperBarrier --> Check2[Boundary Check gradient ds by dn equals 0 on boundary Line]
    Check1 --> FinalDraw[Step 4 Final Drawdown s at Point P]
    Check2 --> FinalDraw
    CornerInput[Corner Geometry Two intersecting boundaries] --> AngleCheck{Interior angle theta}
    AngleCheck -->|theta equals 90 deg| ThreeImg[Three image wells required]
    AngleCheck -->|theta equals 45 deg| SevenImg[Seven image wells required]
    AngleCheck -->|General theta| GenImg[N equals 360 divided by theta minus 1 image wells]
    ThreeImg --> FinalDraw
    SevenImg --> FinalDraw
    GenImg --> FinalDraw
```

## 4.2 Schematic Geometry (Textual Description)

The classical layout for the **single-boundary problem** is illustrated conceptually below:

```
                 BARRIER BOUNDARY (Impermeable)
   =========================================================
                                                       |
                                                       | distance b
                                                       |
                                          IMAGE WELL   |
                                          (+Q)         |
                                                       |
   ..................................................................
                                                       |
                                          REAL WELL    |
                                          (+Q)         |
                                                       |
                                          OBSERVATION  |
                                          POINT P      | <-- r₁ from real well
                                                       | <-- r₂ from image well
                                                       |
```

For a **recharge boundary** (e.g., a river), the same geometry applies, but the **image well is an injection well (−Q)**, so that head remains constant along the boundary.

## 4.3 Architectural Explanation

The diagram is a **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** that maps the **decision logic** a hydrogeologist must follow when applying the method of images:

1. **Identification Block** (BoundaryType) classifies the hydrogeological setting.
2. **Image Generation Block** (RechargeImage / BarrierImage) creates the appropriate virtual well with the correct sign.
3. **Superposition Block** combines the two drawdown contributions.
4. **Validation Block** (Check1 / Check2) confirms the boundary condition.
5. **Corner Logic Branch** extends the single-boundary algorithm to multi-boundary (corner) cases using the $N = (360°/\theta) - 1$ rule.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The branch *Corner Geometry* is essential for **real aquifer wedges** such as alluvial fans, coastal corners, and bounded alluvial valleys where the aquifer is bordered on two or more sides.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define (a) recharge boundary and (b) barrier boundary in an aquifer. Give two real-world examples of each.**

**Model Answer:**

> **(a) Recharge Boundary (Constant-Head Boundary)**: A hydrogeological boundary across which the hydraulic head remains constant with time, regardless of pumping-induced stresses. It is mathematically described by the Dirichlet condition $h = \text{constant}$ (or $s = 0$ for drawdown).
>
> **Examples**: (i) A perennial river hydraulically connected to the aquifer; (ii) A large lake with stable water level.
>
> **(b) Barrier Boundary (Impermeable / No-Flow Boundary)**: A hydrogeological boundary across which no groundwater flow occurs in the direction perpendicular to the boundary. It is described by the Neumann condition $\partial h / \partial n = 0$.
>
> **Examples**: (i) A tight geological fault with impermeable gouge; (ii) A buried sheet-pile or diaphragm wall.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the principle of the method of images as applied to well hydraulics. What sign is assigned to the image well for (a) a recharge boundary and (b) a barrier boundary?**

**Model Answer:**

> **Principle**: The method of images is a mathematical technique that uses superposition of solutions to the linear groundwater flow equation. A fictitious "image well" is placed at the mirror-image position of the real well across the boundary, with a discharge chosen so that the specified boundary condition is automatically satisfied at every point on the boundary.
>
> **(a) Recharge boundary**: The image well is assigned a discharge **−Q** (i.e., it recharges the aquifer at the same rate the real well pumps). The combined drawdown at the boundary is zero.
>
> **(b) Barrier boundary**: The image well is assigned a discharge **+Q** (i.e., it pumps at the same rate as the real well). The drawdown gradients cancel perpendicular to the boundary, satisfying the no-flow condition.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> - **Sign error in image well**: Students frequently assign the wrong sign to the image well, producing drawdown that is *smaller* than the single-well case for barrier boundaries (physically wrong — barrier must *increase* drawdown).
> - **Confusing $r_2$ with R**: The variable $r_2$ in the image-well formula is the *distance from the image well to the observation point*, NOT the radius of influence R. Markers deduct 2 marks for this.
> - **Forgetting to square $r_1$, $r_2$ in u-formulas**: The expression for $u$ requires $r^2$, not $r$. A common slip costs 1 mark.
> - **Mixing up Theis and Jacob**: For large $t$ or small $r$, both should give nearly identical answers; mismatch indicates a calculation error.
> - **In corner problems, omitting alternate image wells**: For a 90° corner you need 3 image wells, not 1. Missing image wells = 3-mark penalty.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive, with clear steps, the equation for the drawdown at any point P in a confined aquifer when a well of discharge Q pumps near a recharge boundary. Use both the Theis (transient) and Jacob (steady-state) forms. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A well pumping at $Q = 1500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ is located at $b = 50 \text{ m}$ from a recharge boundary in a confined aquifer with $T = 500 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}$ and $S = 1.5 \times 10^{-4}$. Compute the drawdown at a point 30 m from the real well on the line joining the well perpendicular to the boundary (between the well and the boundary) after 20 days of pumping. Use the Theis-image method. **(7 Marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

#### (a) Derivation (7 Marks)

- **(1 Mark) Setup and governing equation**: State the Theis equation
  $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} W(u)$, $u = \dfrac{r^2 S}{4 T t}$.

- **(2 Marks) Image well placement**: Real well at distance $b$ from boundary. Image well at mirror position with discharge $-Q$ to satisfy $s = 0$ at the boundary.

- **(2 Marks) Superposition**:
  $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} \left[ W(u_1) - W(u_2) \right]$, where $u_1 = r_1^2 S / (4Tt)$ and $u_2 = r_2^2 S / (4Tt)$.

- **(2 Marks) Jacob's late-time form**: For $u < 0.01$, $W(u) \approx \ln(2.25Tt/(r^2 S))$. After substitution and simplification, $s = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln(r_2/r_1)$.

(Full derivation steps are presented in Section 3.1 of these notes — reproduce them in full in the answer book.)

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**(1 Mark)** Compute distances: $r_1 = 30 \text{ m}$, $r_2 = 2b - r_1 = 70 \text{ m}$.

**(1 Mark)** Compute $u_1$ and $u_2$:

$$
u_1 = \frac{30^2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 500 \times 20} = 3.375 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{70^2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 500 \times 20} = 1.838 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**(1 Mark)** Compute $W(u_1) = 12.021$ and $W(u_2) = 10.328$.

**(2 Marks)** Apply the recharge-boundary Theis-image equation:

$$
s = \frac{1500}{4\pi \times 500} \left[ 12.021 - 10.328 \right] = 0.2387 \times 1.693 \approx 0.404 \text{ m}
$$

**(1 Mark)** Verify with Jacob form: $s = \dfrac{1500}{2\pi \times 500} \ln(70/30) = 0.4775 \times 0.8473 \approx 0.405 \text{ m}$. Both methods agree. ✓

**(1 Mark)** State the final answer with units: **$s \approx 0.40 \text{ m}$**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with neat sketches and derive the equation of drawdown for a pumping well located near an impermeable (barrier) boundary. State the superposition rule and boundary condition used. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A well pumps at $Q = 2500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ from a confined aquifer of $T = 1000 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}$ and $S = 5 \times 10^{-4}$. The well is 80 m from a barrier boundary. Find the drawdown at a point located 40 m from the well, on the line connecting the well perpendicular to the boundary, on the same side as the well, after 50 days of pumping. **(7 Marks)**

**Complete Model Solution:**

#### (a) Derivation (7 Marks)

- **(1 Mark)** Sketch: Show real well + image well at mirror position, observation point P.

- **(1 Mark)** Boundary condition: $\partial h / \partial n = 0$ (no flow perpendicular to barrier).

- **(1 Mark)** Image well sign: $+Q$ (same as real well).

- **(2 Marks)** Theis-image equation:
  $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T} \left[ W(u_1) + W(u_2) \right]$.

- **(1 Mark)** Steady-state form:
  $s = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\left( \dfrac{R^2}{r_1 r_2} \right)$.

- **(1 Mark)** Verification: On the boundary, $r_1 = r_2 = b$, so $s$ at boundary = $2 \times$ single-well drawdown, confirming amplification.

#### (b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks)

**(1 Mark)** Distances: $r_1 = 40 \text{ m}$, $r_2 = 2b - r_1 = 160 - 40 = 120 \text{ m}$.

**(1 Mark)** Compute $u_1$ and $u_2$:

$$
u_1 = \frac{40^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 1000 \times 50} = \frac{0.8}{200000} = 4 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
u_2 = \frac{120^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 1000 \times 50} = \frac{7.2}{200000} = 3.6 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**(1 Mark)** Compute $W(u_1) = 11.852$ and $W(u_2) = 9.654$.

**(2 Marks)** Apply the barrier-boundary Theis-image equation:

$$
s = \frac{2500}{4\pi \times 1000} \left[ 11.852 + 9.654 \right] = 0.1989 \times 21.506 \approx 4.28 \text{ m}
$$

**(1 Mark)** Compare with a single well (no boundary) at 40 m after 50 days: $s_\infty = (2500 / 4\pi \times 1000) \times 11.852 \approx 2.36 \text{ m}$. The barrier has *doubled* the drawdown component, confirming the barrier effect.

**(1 Mark)** Final answer: **$s \approx 4.28 \text{ m}$**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Revision Bullets for the Night-Before Exam**

- **Recharge boundary** = constant head, s = 0 at boundary. Image well has discharge **−Q**.
- **Barrier boundary** = no flow, ∂s/∂n = 0 at boundary. Image well has discharge **+Q**.
- **Master equation (transient)**: $s = (Q / 4\pi T) [W(u_1) \mp W(u_2)]$ — **minus** for recharge, **plus** for barrier.
- **Master equation (steady / Jacob)**: Recharge: $s = (Q / 2\pi T) \ln(r_2/r_1)$; Barrier: $s = (Q / 2\pi T) \ln(R^2 / (r_1 r_2))$.
- **Jacob's approximation is valid when $u < 0.01$** — always verify this in numerical problems.
- **At the boundary itself**: $r_1 = r_2 = b$. Recharge → $s = 0$ ✓. Barrier → $s = 2 \times s_{\text{single well}}$.
- **Corner rule**: For an interior angle $\theta$, the number of image wells required is $N = 360°/\theta - 1$. (90° → 3, 60° → 5, 45° → 7.)
- **Sign of image in corner**: Mirror across each boundary; alternate signs if boundary types alternate, or carry the same sign for two similar boundaries.
- **Physical meaning**: Recharge boundary *reduces* drawdown (good for water supply); Barrier boundary *increases* drawdown (bad for dewatering).
- **Units consistency check**: Q in m³/day, T in m²/day, S dimensionless, t in days → s in metres.
- **Typical KTU pitfall**: Do NOT confuse the image-well distance $r_2$ with the radius of influence $R$. They are completely different quantities.
- **Time-independence of late-time recharge-boundary drawdown**: $s = (Q/2\pi T) \ln(r_2/r_1)$ has no $t$ term — it is essentially steady-state behaviour. This is a *favourite* KTU viva question.
- **Engineering analogy to remember**: The mirror is a *virtual well*; the wall is a *boundary*. The real well + virtual well = exact solution that satisfies the wall condition.
- **Ferris (1959)** is universally credited for introducing the method of images into groundwater hydrology; remember the name for viva.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Image Well System and Method of Images
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Image Well System and Method of Images

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition — Method of Images

In confined/unconfined well hydraulics, **aquifers in the field are never infinite**; they are bounded by physical or hydraulic boundaries such as rivers, lakes, faults, or impermeable rock walls. The **Method of Images** (also called the **Image Well Theory** or **Method of Virtual Wells**) is a mathematical boundary-value technique in which a **fictitious (virtual) "image" well** is mathematically placed on the *opposite side* of a straight-line boundary so that the real boundary condition (zero drawdown at a recharge boundary, or zero flow across an impervious boundary) is automatically satisfied.

The principle was first introduced by **Lord Kelvin** (Thomson, 1848) for electrostatics, later adapted by **Ferris (1959)** and **Hantush (1964)** for groundwater flow. The image well discharges (or recharges) at exactly the same rate $Q$ as the real well, and is located as a *mirror reflection* across the boundary line.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Optical Mirror

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: Looking at yourself in a flat mirror**

Imagine standing in front of a perfectly flat lake (the **recharge boundary**). When you (the **real pumping well**) drop a stone into the water, ripples spread in circles. But because the lake reflects waves perfectly, an observer cannot distinguish whether the ripples came from a *single* source or from **two identical sources**: you and a **mirror image of you** standing an equal distance *behind* the lake surface, dropping a stone *in sync* with you. The lake acts as a line of symmetry.

- If the boundary **recharges** the aquifer (river/lake) → image well **discharges** (with same $Q$).
- If the boundary is **impermeable** (no flow crosses) → image well **recharges** (with $-Q$, opposite sign).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The image well system is used to solve drawdown problems in aquifers bounded by *one* or *more* straight-line boundaries using the **Principle of Superposition** on the Theis solution $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}W(u)$.

## 1.3 Boundary Types in KTU Context

| Boundary Type | Physical Meaning | Image Well Character |
|---|---|---|
| **Recharge (Constant-Head) Boundary** | River, lake, or fully penetrating trench connected to the aquifer | **Discharging** (same $Q$ as real) |
| **Impervious (No-Flow) Boundary** | Fault, impervious rock contact, sheet pile | **Recharging** ($-Q$, opposite sign) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sign Convention Used Throughout These Notes**
> - **Pumping (Discharging) well:** $Q > 0$ → produces *drawdown* $s > 0$
> - **Recharging (Injection) well:** $Q < 0$ → produces *buildup* (negative drawdown)
> - **Theis image well** is placed at a distance $2a$ from the real well, where $a$ is the perpendicular distance of the real well from the boundary.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Logic — Why It Works

The Method of Images is grounded on three pillars:

1. **Linearity of the Governing Equation:** The groundwater flow equation (Theis / Jacob) is *linear* in head (or drawdown). Therefore, drawdowns from multiple wells can simply be **added algebraically** (Principle of Superposition).

2. **Symmetry Trick:** A straight-line boundary produces a problem with *mirror symmetry*. By placing a symmetric image well on the *other side*, the boundary condition is automatically satisfied everywhere on the line.

3. **Unique Solution Theorem:** For a linear PDE (Laplace / Theis) with given boundary conditions, the solution is **unique**. Hence the *real + image* system is mathematically identical to the *real well + real boundary* system.

## 2.2 The General Superposition Equation

For any number of wells (real + image), the drawdown at an observation point $P$ is:

$$
s_P = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i = \frac{1}{4\pi T}\sum_{i=1}^{N} Q_i \cdot W(u_i)
$$

where, for the $i$-th well at radial distance $r_i$ from $P$:

$$
u_i = \frac{r_i^2 \cdot S}{4\,T\,t}
$$

and $W(u) = -0.5772 - \ln u + u - \dfrac{u^2}{2\cdot 2!} + \dfrac{u^3}{3\cdot 3!} - \cdots$ is **Theis' well function**.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the **exam-critical formula table**. Memorize the *signs*, the *distance expressions*, and the *number of image wells* for each configuration.

| # | Configuration | No. of Image Wells | Drawdown Equation (Theis form) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Single well in infinite aquifer** | 0 | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}\,W(u_r)$ |
| 2 | **Recharge (river) boundary** — straight line | 1 (discharging) | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_r) - W(u_i)\bigr]$ |
| 3 | **Impervious (barrier) boundary** — straight line | 1 (recharging) | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_r) + W(u_i)\bigr]$ |
| 4 | **Finite-width recharge strip** (width $2a$) | 2 (1 disc. + 1 recharging image of image) | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_1) - W(u_2) + W(u_3) - W(u_4)\bigr]$ |
| 5 | **Two parallel impervious boundaries** (well at centre) | 2 (both recharging) | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_1) + W(u_2) + W(u_3) + W(u_4)\bigr]$ |
| 6 | **90° corner — two recharge boundaries** | 3 (all discharging) | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_1) - W(u_2) - W(u_3) + W(u_4)\bigr]$ |
| 7 | **90° corner — one recharge + one impervious** (L-shape) | 1 (one type only) | Depends on which arms are recharge/impervious |
| 8 | **45° corner — two impervious boundaries** | 3 image wells (special; satisfies Neumann BC) | $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_1) + W(u_2) + W(u_3) + W(u_4)\bigr]$ |

### Distance Notation (most common case)

For a real well $W$ at perpendicular distance $a$ from a straight boundary, and observation point $P$ located at distance $r$ from $W$ and $\rho$ from the image well $W'$:

$$
r \;=\; \text{distance from real well to } P
\qquad
\rho \;=\; \text{distance from image well to } P
$$

For the **recharge boundary**, the real and image distances are different; for a point on the boundary line itself, $r = \rho$ and hence $s = 0$ (confirms the BC is satisfied).

### Sign Convention Summary

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Recharge boundary} \Rightarrow s_{\text{image}} \text{ has } \text{minus sign (subtraction)}\;}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\text{Impervious boundary} \Rightarrow s_{\text{image}} \text{ has } \text{plus sign (addition)}\;}
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Designing pumping wells near rivers** (Kerala's Pamba, Bharathapuzha, Periyar) — predict drawdown to avoid river-water infiltration (induced recharge) and to size well yields.
- **Coastal aquifer management** — sea acts as a constant-head boundary; image wells estimate saltwater intrusion cone.
- **Trench/drain design** near construction sites.
- **Well-field layout** between two parallel impervious dykes.
- **Mine dewatering** near faults and shear zones.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use Image wells instead of numerical (MODFLOW) models?** The Method of Images gives a **closed-form analytical solution** in seconds — perfect for KTU board examinations where calculators and FDM/FEM software are not allowed. The image well approach is **a 2-mark to 14-mark topic every semester**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Drawdown Equation for a Single Recharge Boundary

### Step 1 — Set up the geometry

Let the **real pumping well** $W$ be located at a perpendicular distance $a$ from a **straight-line recharge boundary** (e.g., a river). Place a Cartesian frame so that the river coincides with the $y$-axis. The real well is at $(a, 0)$. The image well $W'$ (also discharging) is placed at $(-a, 0)$ — i.e., reflected across the river.

### Step 2 — Apply the Theis equation to each well

The drawdown at any field point $P(x, y)$ due to the **real well** alone (ignoring the boundary) is:

$$
s_r(x, y, t) = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\,W(u_r), \quad u_r = \frac{r^2 S}{4 T t}, \quad r^2 = (x-a)^2 + y^2
$$

The drawdown at $P$ due to the **image well** alone (treating it as a *free* well in an infinite aquifer) is:

$$
s_i(x, y, t) = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\,W(u_i), \quad u_i = \frac{\rho^2 S}{4 T t}, \quad \rho^2 = (x+a)^2 + y^2
$$

### Step 3 — Superpose (Principle of Superposition)

The **total drawdown** at $P$ is the algebraic sum:

$$
s(x, y, t) = s_r + s_i
$$

Since the image well is also a *discharging* well, the sign of the image contribution depends on the boundary condition. We now check the boundary condition to fix the sign.

### Step 4 — Verify the boundary condition on the river line $x = 0$

On the boundary $x = 0$:

$$
r^2 = (0-a)^2 + y^2 = a^2 + y^2
$$
$$
\rho^2 = (0+a)^2 + y^2 = a^2 + y^2
$$

Therefore $r = \rho$ and $u_r = u_i$. The Theis function $W(u)$ is *monotonic* in $u$, so:

$$
W(u_r) = W(u_i)
$$

For the **recharge boundary**, we require $s = 0$ on $x = 0$ for all $y$ and $t$. This is achieved by **subtracting** the image contribution:

$$
\boxed{\;s(x, y, t) = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\Bigl[W(u_r) - W(u_i)\Bigr]\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** At $x = 0$: $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_r) - W(u_r)\bigr] = 0$ ✓
> The drawdown on the river itself is zero, confirming the constant-head BC.

### Step 5 — Verify the flux (gradient) for the impervious boundary

If instead the boundary is **impervious (no-flow)**, the normal flux $\partial s / \partial n = 0$ on the boundary. Differentiating $s_r - s_i$ with respect to $x$ at $x=0$ gives a *non-zero* value (the gradients add), violating Neumann BC. We must therefore use **addition**:

$$
\boxed{\;s(x, y, t) = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\Bigl[W(u_r) + W(u_i)\Bigr] \quad \text{(Impervious boundary)}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification:** $\dfrac{\partial s}{\partial x}\bigg|_{x=0} = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[\partial_x W(u_r) + \partial_x W(u_i)\bigr] = 0$ by symmetry (odd function in $x$), because the chain rule gives $\partial W/\partial u \cdot \partial u/\partial x$ and the two terms are equal in magnitude but opposite in sign. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation: Two Intersecting Recharge Boundaries at 90° (Corner — Three Image Wells)

### Step 1 — Geometry

Two recharge boundaries meet at a 90° corner. Let the well be at distance $a$ from boundary 1 and $b$ from boundary 2. Place the corner at the origin with boundary 1 along the $x$-axis and boundary 2 along the $y$-axis. The real well is at $(a, b)$.

### Step 2 — Generate the image set

Reflect the real well across boundary 1 → image $I_1$ at $(a, -b)$ — **discharging**.
Reflect the real well across boundary 2 → image $I_2$ at $(-a, b)$ — **discharging**.
Reflect $I_1$ across boundary 2 (or $I_2$ across boundary 1) → image $I_3$ at $(-a, -b)$ — **recharging** (the double-reflection reverses the sign).

### Step 3 — Write the superposition equation

$$
\boxed{\;s = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\Bigl[W(u_{r}) - W(u_{I_1}) - W(u_{I_2}) + W(u_{I_3})\Bigr]\;}
$$

where the distances are computed from the four well positions to the observation point $P(x, y)$:

$$
r^2 = (x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2
$$
$$
\rho_1^2 = (x-a)^2 + (y+b)^2
$$
$$
\rho_2^2 = (x+a)^2 + (y-b)^2
$$
$$
\rho_3^2 = (x+a)^2 + (y+b)^2
$$

### Step 4 — Confirm sign rules for $n$ straight boundaries

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Golden Rule for $n$ intersecting straight boundaries forming a regular polygon** (after unfolding): the well is repeated at each symmetry position. The sign of each image contribution equals $(-1)^{m}$, where $m$ is the number of impervious boundaries the path from the *real well to the image* must cross. For *all-recharge* boundaries meeting at $90°$, you get 3 images with the alternating $1, -1, -1, +1$ pattern above.

## 3.3 Derivation: 45° Angle — Three Impervious Boundaries Meet (Special Case)

For a 45° wedge with **two impervious boundaries**, the **method of images yields three image wells** (not just the geometric reflection). This is a classic KTU favourite.

Let boundary 1 be the line $y = 0$ and boundary 2 be the line $y = x$ (45° line). Let the real well be at position $W = (a, b)$ with $0 < b < a$.

Using the multiple-reflection (unfolding) method, the image positions are:

- $I_1$ — reflection of $W$ in $y = 0$: image discharges → $+Q$
- $I_2$ — reflection of $W$ in $y = x$: image recharges → $-Q$ (one impervious crossing)
- $I_3$ — double reflection: image discharges → $+Q$

Total drawdown:

$$
\boxed{\;s = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\Bigl[W(u_r) + W(u_{I_1}) - W(u_{I_2}) + W(u_{I_3})\Bigr]\;}
$$

## 3.4 The Steady-State (Jacob / Steady Image Well) Specialisation

For long pumping times ($u \to 0$), $W(u) \approx -0.5772 - \ln u = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{2.25\,T\,t}{r^2 S}\right)$. The drawdown at a point at distance $r$ from the real well and $\rho$ from the image well becomes:

$$
s \;=\; \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{\rho^2}{r^2}\right) \quad \text{(steady-state, recharge boundary, single image)}
$$

Or equivalently using the common form:

$$
s \;=\; \frac{2.303\,Q}{2\pi T}\,\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\rho}{r}\right) \quad \text{(log form, single recharge boundary)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Units Reminder:** $Q$ in m³/day or L/s, $T$ in m²/day, $r$ and $\rho$ in metres, $t$ in days, $S$ dimensionless.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Method-of-Images Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Pumping Well Q at Distance a from Boundary] --> B{Identify Boundary Type}
    B --> C1[Recharge Boundary - River or Lake]
    B --> C2[Impervious Boundary - Fault or Dyke]
    C1 --> D1[Place Image Well at 2a with plus Q - Discharging]
    C2 --> D2[Place Image Well at 2a with minus Q - Recharging]
    D1 --> E1[Apply Superposition Sr minus Si]
    D2 --> E2[Apply Superposition Sr plus Si]
    E1 --> F1[Compute s for any field point P]
    E2 --> F1[Compute s for any field point P]
    F1 --> G[Validate BC on boundary line s equals 0 for recharge]
    F1 --> H[Validate BC ds/dn equals 0 for impervious]
```

## 4.2 Schematic: Single Recharge Boundary Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AQ[Aquifer Region - Recharge side]
        W[REAL WELL - Discharging Q - Located at distance a from boundary]
        P1[Observation Point P1 - r from real well]
    end
    BO[Recharge Boundary - River Line - Drawdown equals 0]
    subgraph IM[Mirror Region]
        WI[IMAGE WELL - Discharging plus Q - Mirror of W at distance a]
        P2[Mirror of P1 - rho from image well]
    end
    W -->|pumping| P1
    WI -->|mirrored pumping| P2
    BO -.symmetry axis.-> W
    BO -.symmetry axis.-> WI
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Single Impervious Boundary Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph AQ[Aquifer Region - Impervious side]
        WR[REAL WELL - Discharging Q]
        PO[Observation Point]
    end
    BO2[Impervious Boundary - Fault - Flow parallel to boundary]
    subgraph IM2[Mirror Region]
        WR2[IMAGE WELL - REcharging minus Q - Opposite sign to maintain zero normal flux]
    end
    WR -->|pumping| PO
    BO2 -.reflecting line.-> WR2
    WR2 -.opposite discharge.-> PO
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram: 90-Degree Recharge-Recharge Corner (3 Image Wells)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph CR[Corner Region - 90 degree]
        WR3[REAL WELL at a,b - plus Q - Discharging]
        I1A[Image I1 - reflection in X axis - Discharging plus Q]
        I2A[Image I2 - reflection in Y axis - Discharging plus Q]
        I3A[Image I3 - double reflection - Recharging minus Q]
        OBS3[Observation Point P]
    end
    WR3 -->|Sr| OBS3
    I1A -->|minus SI1| OBS3
    I2A -->|minus SI2| OBS3
    I3A -->|plus SI3| OBS3
```

## 4.5 Block Diagram: Decision Tree for Image Well Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Start - Identify Aquifer Boundary Geometry] --> Q1{How many straight boundaries}
    Q1 -->|Zero| Z[Single well in infinite aquifer - no image]
    Q1 -->|One| O{Type of single boundary}
    O -->|Recharge| OR[One discharging image well at 2a - Subtract]
    O -->|Impervious| OI[One recharging image well at 2a - Add]
    Q1 -->|Two parallel| TP{Width of strip}
    TP -->|Narrow strip recharge| NAR[Two image wells - 1 disc plus 1 rech - Mixed signs]
    TP -->|Narrow strip impervious| NAI[Two recharging image wells - Add]
    Q1 -->|Two intersecting| TI{Angle between boundaries}
    TI -->|90 degree both recharge| NIN[Three image wells alternating signs]
    TI -->|45 degree both impervious| FIF[Three image wells special case]
    TI -->|90 degree mixed - L| LM[One image well only]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid:** Each block above shows the **decision logic** a KTU examiner expects you to write down before starting any drawdown calculation. Drawing this decision tree on your answer sheet (1–2 minutes) earns you **partial marks** even if your final number is wrong.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]`
**Define the method of images as applied to well hydraulics. Under what conditions is a single image well sufficient?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The method of images is a boundary-value technique in which a **fictitious image well** is mathematically placed on the *opposite side* of a straight-line hydraulic boundary (recharge or impervious) such that the real boundary condition is automatically satisfied at every point on that boundary. The image well has the **same discharge $Q$** as the real well for a recharge boundary, but **$-Q$** (recharging) for an impervious boundary.

A single image well is sufficient when:
1. There is **only one straight boundary**, AND
2. The aquifer extends *infinitely* in all other directions, AND
3. The boundary is *perfectly straight* and *fully penetrating*.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam — May 2023]`
**Differentiate between a recharge boundary and an impervious boundary in terms of the image well character.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Recharge Boundary | Impervious Boundary |
|---|---|---|
| Example | River, lake | Fault, dyke |
| Drawdown at boundary | $s = 0$ (constant head) | $\partial s / \partial n = 0$ (no flow) |
| Image well | **Discharging** (same $Q$) | **Recharging** ($-Q$) |
| Sign in equation | **Minus** (subtraction) | **Plus** (addition) |
| Effect on real well drawdown | *Reduces* drawdown (recharge water) | *Increases* drawdown (no leakage) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]` (14 Marks)

A confined aquifer of transmissivity $T = 600 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}$ and storage coefficient $S = 2 \times 10^{-4}$ is bounded on the **west** by a fully penetrating **river** (recharge boundary). A well pumping at $Q = 2500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ is located **100 m east** of the river. Compute the drawdown at a point **P** that is:
