- (a) **80 m east** of the well and **120 m north** of the river, at time $t = 5$ days. (7 marks)
- (b) **20 m west** of the well (i.e., on the other side — *between the well and the river*), at $t = 5$ days. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Geometry and image well placement*
Real well $W$: take its position as the origin shifted so the river is the line $x = 0$. The well is at $(100, 0)$. The image well $W'$ (discharging, same $Q$) is at $(-100, 0)$ (mirror across the river).

Observation point $P$: 80 m east of well → $x = 180$. 120 m north of river → $y = 120$.

*Step 2 — Distances*

$$
r = \sqrt{(180-100)^2 + (120-0)^2} = \sqrt{80^2 + 120^2} = \sqrt{6400 + 14400} = \sqrt{20800} \approx 144.22 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\rho = \sqrt{(180-(-100))^2 + 120^2} = \sqrt{280^2 + 120^2} = \sqrt{78400 + 14400} = \sqrt{92800} \approx 304.63 \text{ m}
$$

*Step 3 — Well-function arguments $u$*

$$
u_r = \frac{r^2 S}{4 T t} = \frac{(144.22)^2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 600 \times 5} = \frac{20800 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{12000} = \frac{4.16}{12000} \approx 3.467 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
u_i = \frac{\rho^2 S}{4 T t} = \frac{92800 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{12000} = \frac{18.56}{12000} \approx 1.547 \times 10^{-3}
$$

*Step 4 — Theis well-function values* (from standard Theis tables or Jacob's log approximation, valid for small $u$):

$$
W(u) \approx -0.5772 - \ln(u) = \ln\!\left(\frac{2.25\,T\,t}{r^2 S}\right) \text{ for } u < 0.01
$$

$$
W(u_r) = \ln\!\left(\frac{2.25 \times 600 \times 5}{20800 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{6750}{4.16}\right) = \ln(1622.6) \approx 7.392
$$

$$
W(u_i) = \ln\!\left(\frac{6750}{18.56}\right) = \ln(363.7) \approx 5.897
$$

*Step 5 — Apply image well equation for recharge boundary*

$$
s = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_r) - W(u_i)\bigr] = \frac{2500}{4\pi \times 600}\,(7.392 - 5.897)
$$

$$
s = \frac{2500}{7539.82}\,(1.495) = 0.3316 \times 1.495 \approx 0.496 \text{ m}
$$

**$\boxed{s_P \approx 0.50 \text{ m}}$**

---

**Part (b) — 7 marks**

*Step 1 — Observation point 20 m west of well → $x = 80$, still $y = 0$*

*Step 2 — Distances*

$$
r = \sqrt{(80-100)^2 + 0^2} = 20 \text{ m}
$$

$$
\rho = \sqrt{(80-(-100))^2 + 0^2} = 180 \text{ m}
$$

*Step 3 — $u$ values*

$$
u_r = \frac{(20)^2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{12000} = \frac{0.08}{12000} = 6.667 \times 10^{-6}
$$

$$
u_i = \frac{(180)^2 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}{12000} = \frac{6.48}{12000} = 5.4 \times 10^{-4}
$$

Both are < 0.01, so use the log approximation.

*Step 4 — Well-function values*

$$
W(u_r) = \ln\!\left(\frac{2.25 \times 600 \times 5}{400 \times 2 \times 10^{-4}}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{6750}{0.08}\right) = \ln(84375) \approx 11.343
$$

$$
W(u_i) = \ln\!\left(\frac{6750}{6.48}\right) = \ln(1041.7) \approx 6.949
$$

*Step 5 — Apply recharge boundary formula*

$$
s = \frac{2500}{7539.82}\,(11.343 - 6.949) = 0.3316 \times 4.394 \approx 1.457 \text{ m}
$$

**$\boxed{s_P \approx 1.46 \text{ m}}$**

*Interpretation:* The point *closer to the river* between well and river shows **higher drawdown** because the image well *adds* to the real well's cone (note: drawdown is reduced by recharge, so points further from the river have lower $s$; here P is at 80 m from the river, well is at 100 m → P is between well and river).

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]` (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

A well discharges at $Q = 1500 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$ in a confined aquifer of $T = 200 \text{ m}^2/\text{day}$, $S = 5 \times 10^{-4}$. The aquifer is bounded by a **vertical impervious fault** located **150 m from the well**.

- (a) Using the image well theory, derive the drawdown equation and compute the drawdown at an observation well located **200 m from the pumping well on the side away from the fault** at $t = 2$ days. (7 marks)
- (b) If the same boundary were a **recharge (river) boundary** instead of an impervious fault, what would be the drawdown at the *same observation point* under identical conditions? Compare the two cases. (7 marks)

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Impervious Boundary — 7 marks**

*Derivation:* For an impervious boundary, the image well is *recharging* ($-Q$) and the drawdown is the *sum*:

$$
s = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_r) + W(u_i)\bigr]
$$

Image well is at 150 m on the *other side* of the fault. The observation point P is 200 m from the real well, on the side *away* from the fault. So P is on the *same* side as the real well. Distance from the image well to P:

$$
\rho = 200 + 2 \times 150 = 500 \text{ m}
$$

Distance from real well to P: $r = 200$ m.

$$
u_r = \frac{(200)^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{4 \times 200 \times 2} = \frac{20}{1600} = 0.0125
$$

$$
u_i = \frac{(500)^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}}{1600} = \frac{125}{1600} = 0.0781
$$

For $u = 0.0125$, $W(u) \approx \ln(2.25 T t / (r^2 S)) = \ln(2.25 \times 200 \times 2 / 20) = \ln(45) = 3.807$
For $u = 0.0781$, $W(u) \approx \ln(2.25 \times 200 \times 2 / 125) = \ln(7.2) = 1.974$ *(acceptable approximation; Theis tables give ≈ 2.21)*

Using the log approximation values:
$$
W(u_r) \approx 3.807, \quad W(u_i) \approx 1.974
$$

$$
s = \frac{1500}{4\pi \times 200}\,(3.807 + 1.974) = \frac{1500}{2513.27}\,(5.781) = 0.5968 \times 5.781 \approx 3.451 \text{ m}
$$

**$\boxed{s_P \approx 3.45 \text{ m (impervious boundary)}}$**

---

**Part (b) — Recharge Boundary — 7 marks**

Now image well is **discharging** ($+Q$), and we use the *minus* sign:

$$
s = \frac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_r) - W(u_i)\bigr] = 0.5968 \times (3.807 - 1.974) = 0.5968 \times 1.833 \approx 1.094 \text{ m}
$$

**$\boxed{s_P \approx 1.09 \text{ m (recharge boundary)}}$**

*Comparison:* The recharge boundary cuts the drawdown by a factor of $5.781 / 1.833 \approx 3.15$. The recharge boundary **reduces drawdown by about 68 %** because the river continuously supplies water, while the impervious fault **increases drawdown by 215 %** (relative to the no-boundary case of 1.09 m) by preventing the cone from spreading laterally.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost 2 to 5 marks in KTU valuation:**
>
> 1. **Wrong sign on image contribution.** A recharge boundary needs a *minus* sign, an impervious boundary needs a *plus* sign. The *most common* error is using the *plus* sign for both — resulting in over-estimated drawdown for recharge-boundary problems.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the $-Q$ sign for the image under an impervious boundary.** Many students write $W(u_r) + W(u_i)$ but use $+Q$ in the formula. The sign convention is *absorbed* in the superposition, but students sometimes explicitly substitute $Q$ for the image and get the wrong magnitude.
>
> 3. **Incorrect distance from observation point to image well.** Always draw a *separate* diagram for the *image geometry* before substituting. The image well is at the *mirror position*, not at the boundary.
>
> 4. **Using Jacob's straight-line approximation when $u > 0.01$.** For observation points *close* to the well (small $r$) or at *early times* (small $t$), the full Theis series or table values must be used. Examiner deducts 1 mark if the approximation is silently applied out of range.
>
> 5. **Mixing up units.** $T$ in m²/day, $Q$ in m³/day, $r$ in m, $t$ in days, $S$ dimensionless. Inconsistency loses 1 mark.
>
> 6. **Not stating the boundary condition satisfied.** A complete KTU answer must *state* which boundary condition is satisfied (constant head or no-flow) and *briefly verify* it on the boundary line. This is worth 1 mark on its own.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Method of Images** = mathematical trick to convert a bounded-aquifer problem into an unbounded-aquifer superposition problem.
- **Recharge boundary** → image well is *discharging* ($+Q$), sign in equation is **minus** (subtraction).
- **Impervious boundary** → image well is *recharging* ($-Q$), sign in equation is **plus** (addition).
- **Number of image wells** = depends on geometry: 1 (single straight boundary), 2 (finite-width strip), 3 (90° corner, or 45° wedge special case).
- **Image well location** = mirror image of real well across the boundary, at equal perpendicular distance $a$ on the other side; total separation = $2a$.
- **General equation:** $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}\,\sum_i \pm\, W(u_i)$ with $u_i = r_i^2 S/(4 T t)$.
- **Steady-state (long time):** $s \to \dfrac{2.303\,Q}{2\pi T}\log_{10}(\rho/r)$ for a single recharge boundary.
- **Master formulas to memorize for KTU:**
  - Single recharge: $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_r) - W(u_i)\bigr]$
  - Single impervious: $s = \dfrac{Q}{4\pi T}\bigl[W(u_r) + W(u_i)\bigr]$
  - 90° corner (both recharge): 3 images with $1, -1, -1, +1$ pattern
  - 45° wedge (both impervious): 3 images with $1, +1, -1, +1$ pattern
- **Always draw a labelled figure** showing the real well, the image well(s), the boundary, and the observation point — this alone earns 1–2 marks.
- **State and verify the boundary condition** at the end of every solution.
- **Sign convention is the #1 grading factor** — check it twice before submitting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** For any image-well problem in the exam, structure your answer as **(i) Diagram**, **(ii) Identification of boundary type & image well character**, **(iii) Distance calculation**, **(iv) $u$-values and $W(u)$ evaluation**, **(v) Superposition formula and final answer**, **(vi) Boundary-condition verification**. This six-step structure is the KTU board examiner's gold standard.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical Cases (Problems from Method of Images) and Method of Constructing Shallow Wells
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Practical Cases (Problems from Method of Images) and Method of Constructing Shallow Wells

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Method of Images — The Boundary Mirror Concept

**Formal Definition:**
The **Method of Images** is an elegant mathematical technique used in groundwater hydraulics to solve seepage and drawdown problems involving a well located in the vicinity of a **hydraulic boundary** (a straight-line boundary where either the head is constant — a recharge boundary — or the flow is parallel to the boundary — an impervious boundary). The actual well is mathematically "mirrored" across the boundary with a fictitious *image well* of appropriate sign (discharging or recharging) such that the combined flow net of the real well and its image automatically satisfies the specified boundary condition. This converts an asymmetric, bounded flow problem into a symmetric, unbounded flow problem that is easily solvable using superposition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The Method of Images is a *CO3 / CO4 mapped* topic — it tests both conceptual understanding of boundary hydraulics and the ability to apply the principle of superposition to derive drawdown expressions in bounded aquifer systems.

**Conceptual Analogy — The Mirror Image Trick:**
Imagine you are standing in a long, narrow hallway with a perfectly polished mirror wall on one side. If you clap your hands, the sound reflects off the mirror and creates a *virtual clapper* that appears to be standing the same distance behind the mirror as you are in front of it. The real clapper and the virtual clapper together produce exactly the same sound field in the hallway as the real clapper alone with the mirror wall. The mirror "boundary condition" is automatically satisfied because the sound wave sees the mirror as a wall.
In groundwater, the **hydraulic boundary** is the mirror, the **real well** is you, and the **image well** is the virtual clapper. The aquifer boundary behaves as if the image well is real, and the math becomes wonderfully simple.

### 1.2 Practical Cases Where the Method of Images Is Used

| S.No | Practical Situation | Boundary Type | Required Image Wells |
|------|--------------------|--------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Well near a river or lake | Recharge (constant-head) | One — same sign as real well |
| 2 | Well near a vertical fault / impervious rock | Impervious (no-flow) | One — opposite sign to real well |
| 3 | Well near a recharging stream meeting an impervious wall | Right-angle corner (90°) | Three image wells |
| 4 | Well in a rectangular island between two rivers | Two recharge boundaries | Three image wells (infinite series) |
| 5 | Well between two impervious parallel walls | Two impervious boundaries | Infinite image system |

### 1.3 Shallow Wells — What Are They?

**Formal Definition:**
A **shallow well** (also called a *dug well* or *open well*) is a large-diameter well, typically of depth less than **20–30 m** and diameter **1 m to 10 m**, constructed by manual or mechanical excavation through the overburden into the shallow water-table aquifer, and lined with a steining (masonry or concrete) to prevent caving. The water enters the well from the bottom (and sometimes through perforations in the lower steining), and the well is usually pumped by a bucket, a reciprocating pump, or a centrifugal suction pump (with the suction lift limited to about **6–7 m**).

**Conceptual Analogy:**
Think of a shallow well as a giant *bucket lowered into a saturated sponge*. The sponge (the water-table aquifer) holds the water; the bucket (the well steining) keeps the soil out so the water can stand clean inside. You can dip a smaller cup (bucket) into this bigger bucket to lift water to the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - Shallow well depth: typically **< 30 m**
> - Typical diameter: **1 m to 10 m**
> - Maximum suction lift for centrifugal pump: **~6.5 m** (theoretical), **~5–6 m** (practical)
> - The well must always terminate at least **3–5 m** below the static water table to ensure a usable drawdown.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Underlying Theory of the Method of Images

The Method of Images is rooted in the **theory of potential flow** applied to a 2-D horizontal isotropic aquifer. The governing equation is **Laplace's equation**:

$$\nabla^{2} \phi = 0$$

where $\phi$ is the velocity potential (or the head $h$). For a fully penetrating well in a homogeneous, isotropic aquifer of constant transmissivity, the well acts as a *singular point* in the potential field. The Method of Images works on the principle of **superposition**: the drawdown at any point is the algebraic sum of the drawdowns produced by the real well and *all* image wells acting independently.

**Operational Logic Steps:**

1. **Identify the boundary type** — Is the boundary a *constant-head recharge boundary* (e.g., a river) or a *no-flow impervious boundary* (e.g., a vertical fault filled with clay)?
2. **Place the image well** — Mirror the real well geometrically across the boundary line. The image is at the same perpendicular distance on the other side of the boundary.
3. **Assign the sign of the image well**:
   - Recharge boundary: image is a **discharge well** (same sign, same discharge $Q$).
   - Impervious boundary: image is a **recharge (injection) well** (opposite sign, $-Q$).
4. **Apply superposition** — At any point in the aquifer, the total drawdown is the sum of drawdowns due to the real well and all image wells.
5. **Verify the boundary condition** — At every point on the real boundary, the drawdown must equal **zero** (for recharge boundary) and the gradient perpendicular to the boundary must equal **zero** (for impervious boundary).

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the single most important reference for solving Method of Images problems in KTU exams. Memorize it.**

| S.No | Configuration | Boundary Type | No. of Image Wells | Total Drawdown Equation $s$ at a Point |
|-----|---------------|---------------|--------------------|----------------------------------------|
| 1 | Single well in infinite aquifer | None | 0 | $s = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\dfrac{R}{r}\right)$ |
| 2 | Well near a straight recharge boundary (river) | Constant-head | 1 image (same sign) | $s = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\dfrac{R \cdot r_{i}}{r \cdot R_{i}}\right)$ |
| 3 | Well near a straight impervious boundary (fault) | No-flow | 1 image (opposite sign) | $s = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\dfrac{R \cdot R_{i}}{r \cdot r_{i}}\right)$ |
| 4 | Well near a right-angle corner (recharge + impervious) | Mixed | 3 images (signs vary) | Superposition of three image terms |
| 5 | Well between two parallel recharge boundaries | Two constant-head | Infinite series of images | Fourier-series or infinite-image solution |
| 6 | Discharge of a well in unconfined aquifer (Dupuit) | None | 0 | $Q = \dfrac{\pi k \left(H^{2} - h^{2}\right)}{\ln(R/r)}$ |
| 7 | Discharge of a well in confined aquifer (Thiem) | None | 0 | $Q = \dfrac{2\pi k B (H - h)}{\ln(R/r)} = \dfrac{2\pi T s}{\ln(R/r)}$ |

**Where:**
- $Q$ = discharge of the well $\left[\mathrm{L^{3}/T}\right]$
- $T$ = transmissivity of the aquifer $\left[\mathrm{L^{2}/T}\right]$
- $k$ = hydraulic conductivity $\left[\mathrm{L/T}\right]$
- $B$ = saturated thickness of confined aquifer $[\mathrm{L}]$
- $H$ = static piezometric head $[\mathrm{L}]$
- $h$ = dynamic head at the well $[\mathrm{L}]$
- $s = H - h$ = drawdown $[\mathrm{L}]$
- $R$ = radius of influence $[\mathrm{L}]$
- $r$ = distance from real well to observation point $[\mathrm{L}]$
- $r_{i}$ = distance from image well to observation point $[\mathrm{L}]$
- $R_{i}$ = distance from image well to the real well's radius-of-influence circle

> [!NOTE]
> **The principle of superposition** is mathematically valid only because Laplace's equation is linear. The drawdown contributions from the real well and each image well are *linearly independent* and can simply be added.

### 2.3 Sign Convention for Image Wells

| Real Well Discharges? | Boundary Type | Image Well Type | Sign of $Q_{\text{image}}$ |
|-----------------------|---------------|-----------------|------------------------------|
| Yes (pumping) | Recharge (river) | Pumping well | $+Q$ |
| Yes (pumping) | Impervious (fault) | Recharge (injection) | $-Q$ |
| No (recharge) | Recharge (river) | Recharge | $-Q$ |
| No (recharge) | Impervious (fault) | Pumping | $+Q$ |

### 2.4 Engineering Utility

The Method of Images has tremendous practical utility in:
- Designing **water-supply wells near rivers** (bank filtration projects).
- Locating **dewatering wells** near impervious retaining structures.
- Designing **drainage wells** in confined spaces between two parallel drains.
- Interpreting **pumping test data** from wells near known hydrological boundaries.
- Predicting the **cone of depression** shape in bounded aquifer systems.

### 2.5 Shallow Wells — Construction Method Theory

A shallow well is constructed in the following logical sequence:

1. **Site selection** — based on hydrogeological survey, proximity to demand, and water quality.
2. **Excavation** — by open-pit method, typically with manual digging, using a "kerb stone" or "cutting curb" at the bottom to keep the well sinking straight.
3. **Steining construction** — the masonry or concrete lining built from the bottom upward as excavation proceeds.
4. **Bottom construction** — either left as open bottom (in cohesive soils) or provided with a gravel filter pack and concrete plug (in loose sandy soils).
5. **Top construction** — well cover / capping slab with provision for the pump, manhole, and vent pipe.
6. **Development and disinfection** — to remove fines and make the well ready for use.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Yield of a shallow well** is governed by Darcy's law applied to the hemispherical / spherical flow at the well bottom (if bottom-fed) or to the cylindrical flow through the side perforations:
> $$Q = 2\pi k r_{w} s$$ (cylindrical, after Dupuit)
> For a fully penetrating hemispherical flow domain:
> $$Q = 2\pi k r_{w} s$$  with the bottom contributing an additional term $Q_{\text{bottom}} = 4 k r_{w} s$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Drawdown at the Real Well Near a Straight Recharge Boundary

**Statement:** A fully penetrating well of discharge $Q$ is located at a perpendicular distance $d$ from a straight river (recharge boundary). The aquifer is homogeneous, isotropic, and confined, with transmissivity $T$ and radius of influence $R$. Derive the drawdown at the well.

**Setup (Mathematical Sketch):**

Let the real well $W$ be located at point $(0, 0)$ and the image well $W'$ be located at $(2d, 0)$ on the other side of the river. The river is along the $y$-axis at $x = d$. The aquifer extends to radius $R$ from the well.

**Step 1 — Write the radial distances.**
- Distance from real well to observation point $P(x,y)$:
  $$r = \sqrt{x^{2} + y^{2}}$$
- Distance from image well to observation point $P(x,y)$:
  $$r_{i} = \sqrt{(x - 2d)^{2} + y^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the principle of superposition.**
The drawdown at $P$ due to the real well alone is:
$$s_{1} = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R}{r}\right)$$

The drawdown at $P$ due to the image well (which is a *pumping* well of the same sign $+Q$) is:
$$s_{2} = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R}{r_{i}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Total drawdown by superposition.**
$$s = s_{1} + s_{2} = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \left[ \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R}{r}\right) + \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R}{r_{i}}\right) \right] = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R^{2}}{r \, r_{i}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Check the boundary condition on the river ($x = d$, $y$ arbitrary).**

At $x = d$:
$$r = \sqrt{d^{2} + y^{2}}, \quad r_{i} = \sqrt{d^{2} + y^{2}} = r$$
Therefore $r \cdot r_{i} = r^{2}$, and:
$$s_{\text{river}} = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R^{2}}{r^{2}}\right) = 0$$
The boundary condition is **automatically satisfied**. ✓

**Step 5 — Drawdown at the well face (i.e., at the real well's radius $r_{w}$).**
At the well, $r = r_{w}$ and $r_{i} = 2d$ (image is at distance $2d$ from the real well).
$$s_{w} = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R \cdot 2d}{r_{w} \cdot R}\right) = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{2d}{r_{w}}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice:** The drawdown at the well face no longer depends on the *radius of influence* $R$ when the well is very close to the river, because the river itself acts as the dominant recharge source. This is the **practical utility** of the image solution: it gives a physically meaningful result.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Drawdown at the Real Well Near a Straight Impervious Boundary

**Setup:** Same geometry as above, but the river is replaced by a vertical impervious fault at $x = d$.

**Step 1 — Assign the sign of the image well.**
For an impervious boundary, the image well is a *recharge* well of discharge $-Q$. Its contribution to drawdown is therefore:
$$s_{2} = \frac{-Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R}{r_{i}}\right) = -\frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R}{r_{i}}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Total drawdown.**
$$s = s_{1} + s_{2} = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \left[ \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R}{r}\right) - \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R}{r_{i}}\right) \right] = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{r_{i}}{r}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Check the boundary condition on the fault.**

On the fault ($x = d$), $r = r_{i}$, so $s = 0$. The head is *constant* along the fault, which is consistent with a no-flow boundary combined with a well.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student error:** Students often forget that the impervious boundary requires the image to be of *opposite* sign, not same sign. This is a guaranteed 7-mark loss in the KTU valuation scheme.

**Step 4 — Drawdown at the well.**
At the well, $r = r_{w}$ and $r_{i} = 2d$:
$$s_{w} = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{2d}{r_{w}}\right)$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Three-Image Solution: Well in a Right-Angle Corner

**Setup:** A well is located in the quadrant formed by a horizontal impervious boundary (the bedrock floor, considered a no-flow line) and a vertical recharge boundary (a river). The image system must satisfy *both* boundaries simultaneously.

**Step 1 — Place the first image.**
To satisfy the vertical recharge boundary at $x = 0$, place image $W_{1}$ at $(-d, 0)$ with sign $+Q$ (pumping).

**Step 2 — Place the second image to satisfy the horizontal impervious boundary at $y = 0$.**
Place image $W_{2}$ at $(0, -h)$ with sign $-Q$ (recharge).

**Step 3 — Place the third image to restore the symmetry of the first image with respect to the horizontal boundary.**
Place image $W_{3}$ at $(-d, -h)$ with sign $+Q$ (pumping). This third image is the image of $W_{1}$ across the horizontal boundary.

**Step 4 — Superposition.**
The total drawdown at any point is:
$$s = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \left[ \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R^{2}}{r_{1} r_{3}}\right) - \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R^{2}}{r_{2}^{2}}\right) \right]$$

where $r_{1}, r_{2}, r_{3}$ are distances to the three images from the observation point.

> [!NOTE]
> **The pattern to remember:** For a corner formed by two boundaries, the image system consists of the well's reflections in each boundary plus the "diagonal" image needed to maintain consistency. The total image count is $N^{2} - 1$ where $N$ is the number of wells in the symmetric unit cell.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:**
A well pumping at $Q = 1500\ \mathrm{m^{3}/day}$ is located at a distance of $d = 50\ \mathrm{m}$ from a straight river. The aquifer is confined, with $T = 800\ \mathrm{m^{2}/day}$, and the well radius is $r_{w} = 0.30\ \mathrm{m}$. The radius of influence is $R = 300\ \mathrm{m}$. Find the drawdown at the well face and at a point $P$ which is $25\ \mathrm{m}$ from the well on the side away from the river.

**Given:**
$Q = 1500\ \mathrm{m^{3}/day}$, $T = 800\ \mathrm{m^{2}/day}$, $d = 50\ \mathrm{m}$, $r_{w} = 0.30\ \mathrm{m}$, $R = 300\ \mathrm{m}$.

**Step 1 — Geometry for the observation point P.**
Point $P$ is $25\ \mathrm{m}$ from the well on the side *away* from the river. Let the well be at $x = 0$ and the river at $x = 50\ \mathrm{m}$. Then the image is at $x = 100\ \mathrm{m}$. Point $P$ is at $x = -25\ \mathrm{m}$, $y = 0$.

Therefore:
- $r = 25\ \mathrm{m}$ (from real well to P)
- $r_{i} = 100 - (-25) = 125\ \mathrm{m}$ (from image to P)

**Step 2 — Apply the recharge-boundary drawdown formula.**
$$s_{P} = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R^{2}}{r \, r_{i}}\right) = \frac{1500}{2\pi \times 800} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{300^{2}}{25 \times 125}\right)$$

$$s_{P} = \frac{1500}{5026.55} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{90000}{3125}\right) = 0.2984 \times \ln(28.8)$$

$$s_{P} = 0.2984 \times 3.3604 = 1.003\ \mathrm{m}$$

**Step 3 — Drawdown at the well face.**
At the well, $r = r_{w} = 0.30\ \mathrm{m}$ and $r_{i} = 2d = 100\ \mathrm{m}$:
$$s_{w} = \frac{1500}{5026.55} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{300 \times 100}{0.30 \times 300}\right) = 0.2984 \times \ln\!\!\left(\frac{100}{0.30}\right)$$

$$s_{w} = 0.2984 \times \ln(333.33) = 0.2984 \times 5.8101 = 1.734\ \mathrm{m}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation.**
The drawdown at the well face is **1.73 m** and at point $P$ (25 m from well) is **1.00 m**. These are the values that an observation well placed at $P$ would record.

### 3.5 Derivation 4 — Shallow Well Yield (Hemispherical Flow Assumption)

A shallow well in a water-table aquifer can be analyzed as a hemispherical flow domain converging on the well bottom. By Darcy's law applied to a hemispherical surface of radius $r$ at the bottom of the well:

$$Q = (2\pi r^{2}) \cdot k \cdot \frac{ds}{dr}$$

Separating variables:
$$Q \frac{dr}{r^{2}} = 2\pi k \, ds$$

Integrating between $r = r_{w}$ (well radius, head $h_{w}$) and $r = R$ (radius of zero drawdown, head $H$):
$$\begin{aligned}
-Q \int_{r_{w}}^{R} \frac{dr}{r^{2}} &= 2\pi k \int_{h_{w}}^{H} ds \\
-Q \left[ -\frac{1}{r} \right]_{r_{w}}^{R} &= 2\pi k (H - h_{w}) \\
Q \left( \frac{1}{r_{w}} - \frac{1}{R} \right) &= 2\pi k s
\end{aligned}$$

Since $R \gg r_{w}$, $\dfrac{1}{R} \approx 0$ and:
$$Q \approx \frac{2\pi k r_{w} s}{1} = 2\pi k r_{w} s$$

Solving for specific capacity:
$$s = \frac{Q}{2\pi k r_{w}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The above formula shows that the drawdown in a hemispherical-flow shallow well is *inversely proportional* to the well radius. Doubling the well diameter reduces the drawdown by half (for the same yield), which is why shallow wells are built with large diameters.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Method of Images — Configurations Map (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify the hydraulic boundary near the well] --> B{Type of boundary?}
    B -- Recharge boundary, constant head, e.g. river --> C[Place ONE image well as a pumping well of same sign]
    B -- Impervious boundary, no flow, e.g. fault --> D[Place ONE image well as a recharge well of opposite sign]
    B -- Right angle corner --> E[Place THREE image wells]
    B -- Two parallel boundaries --> F[Place INFINITE image series]
    C --> G[Apply superposition of drawdowns]
    D --> G
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H[Verify boundary condition satisfied]
    H --> I[Compute drawdown at required point]
```

### 4.2 Three-Image Layout for a Right-Angle Corner (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Quadrant_Real [REAL AQUIFER QUADRANT]
        W[Real Well at origin 0,0, pumping plus Q]
    end
    subgraph Reflected_X [Reflection across recharge boundary]
        W1[Image W1 at minus d, 0, pumping plus Q]
    end
    subgraph Reflected_Y [Reflection across impervious boundary]
        W2[Image W2 at 0, minus h, recharge minus Q]
    end
    subgraph Diagonal_Image [Diagonal image to restore symmetry]
        W3[Image W3 at minus d, minus h, pumping plus Q]
    end
    W -. draws down .-> P1[Observation point P]
    W1 -. draws down .-> P1
    W2 -. injects recharge .-> P1
    W3 -. draws down .-> P1
    P1 --> S[Total drawdown s at P equals sum of all contributions]
```

### 4.3 Shallow Well Construction Sequence (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Site selection and hydrogeological survey] --> B[Mark well center and lay kerb stone]
    B --> C[Beging excavation up to first steining course depth]
    C --> D[Build first course of brick or stone steining]
    D --> E[Continue excavation below steining and add courses]
    E --> F[Insert cutting curb at base to control sinking]
    F --> G{Soil type at bottom?}
    G -- Cohesive clay --> H[Leave bottom open, no plug]
    G -- Loose sand --> I[Provide gravel filter pack plus concrete plug]
    H --> J[Construct well cap with manhole and pump base]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Develop well by pumping and surging]
    K --> L[Disinfect with chlorine solution]
    L --> M[Commission and connect to pump]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram of a Typical Shallow Well (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Surface_Level [GROUND SURFACE]
        A1[Well cover slab with manhole and vent pipe]
    end
    A1 --> A2[Steining masonry lining thickness 300 to 600 mm]
    A2 --> A3[Perforations in lower steining for side entry]
    A3 --> A4[Well screen or open joint at bottom]
    A4 --> A5[Hemispherical flow zone in water table aquifer]
    A5 --> A6[Static water table below ground level]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
*Define the Method of Images as applied to groundwater flow. State the sign of the image well for (a) a recharge boundary and (b) an impervious boundary.*

**Model Answer:**
The Method of Images is a mathematical technique in which a real well near a hydraulic boundary is replaced by the real well plus a fictitious *image well* placed symmetrically across the boundary. The image well is of the same discharge $Q$ for a **recharge (constant-head) boundary** and of opposite discharge $-Q$ for an **impervious (no-flow) boundary**. The combined flow net of the real and image wells automatically satisfies the boundary condition at the actual physical boundary, allowing the use of the simple Thiem equation with superposition. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for recharge sign, 1 for impervious sign)**

---

**Question 2.** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
*What is a shallow well? List any three features that distinguish a shallow well from a deep well.*

**Model Answer:**
A shallow well is a large-diameter, open excavation well, typically less than 20–30 m deep, that taps the water-table aquifer. Distinguishing features:
1. It is **hand- or machine-dug** rather than bored or drilled.
2. It is **lined with masonry (steining)** to prevent caving.
3. It has a **large diameter (1–10 m)** which provides a large storage volume.
4. It can be pumped with a **simple suction pump** (lift limited to ~6 m).
**(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for features)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Comprehensive Problem on Method of Images

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023 | Module 3, 14-Mark Question]**

A fully penetrating well in a confined aquifer of transmissivity $T = 1000\ \mathrm{m^{2}/day}$ is pumped at a rate of $Q = 2000\ \mathrm{m^{3}/day}$. The well is located at a distance of $100\ \mathrm{m}$ from a straight river. The well radius is $r_{w} = 0.25\ \mathrm{m}$ and the radius of influence is $R = 500\ \mathrm{m}$.

**(a)** [7 marks] Derive the drawdown equation for a well near a recharge boundary using the Method of Images, and compute the drawdown at the well face.

**(b)** [7 marks] If the river is replaced by an impervious fault, recalculate the drawdown at the well face and comment on the engineering implications.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Setting up the image system.** For a recharge boundary (river), the image is a pumping well of the same sign $+Q$ placed at distance $2d = 200\ \mathrm{m}$ on the other side of the river.

**Step 2 — Drawdown by superposition.** Using the radial distances $r$ from the real well and $r_{i}$ from the image well:
$$s = \frac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{R^{2}}{r \, r_{i}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Verify boundary condition.** On the river, $r = r_{i}$, so $s = 0$. ✓

**Step 4 — Compute drawdown at the well face** ($r = r_{w} = 0.25\ \mathrm{m}$, $r_{i} = 200\ \mathrm{m}$):
$$s_{w} = \frac{2000}{2\pi \times 1000} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{500 \times 200}{0.25 \times 500}\right) = \frac{1}{\pi} \ln(800)$$

$$s_{w} = 0.3183 \times 6.6846 = 2.128\ \mathrm{m}$$

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Sign of the image.** For an impervious boundary, the image is a recharge well of $-Q$.

**Step 2 — Drawdown equation.** $s = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\dfrac{r_{i}}{r}\right)$.

**Step 3 — Compute drawdown at the well face** ($r = r_{w} = 0.25\ \mathrm{m}$, $r_{i} = 200\ \mathrm{m}$):
$$s_{w} = \frac{1}{\pi} \ln\!\!\left(\frac{200}{0.25}\right) = \frac{1}{\pi} \ln(800) = 2.128\ \mathrm{m}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering comment.** Interestingly, the numerical drawdown at the well face is the same in both cases, but the *spatial distribution* of drawdown is very different. With a river, drawdown is suppressed near the river (constant head acts as a source). With a fault, the fault is a *barrier* — water is prevented from crossing it, so the cone of depression is elongated parallel to the fault and the well's effective capacity is reduced. The fault is therefore an *unfavourable* boundary for well placement, while a river is *favourable*.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Question on Shallow Wells

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022 | Module 3, 14-Mark Question]**

**(a)** [7 marks] Describe, with a neat sketch, the step-by-step method of constructing a shallow open well in a water-table aquifer. Mention the role of the kerb stone, the steining, and the well cover.

**(b)** [7 marks] A shallow well of radius $0.5\ \mathrm{m}$ is to be pumped at $Q = 50\ \mathrm{m^{3}/day}$ with an allowable drawdown of $s = 2\ \mathrm{m}$. The hydraulic conductivity of the soil is $k = 12\ \mathrm{m/day}$. Verify if the hemispherical flow formula is satisfied.

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step-by-step construction sequence:**

1. **Site selection** based on hydrogeology, water quality, and proximity to demand.
2. **Excavation** by open-pit method to a depth of 1–2 m below ground.
3. **First steining course** is laid on a firm concrete or brick foundation at the bottom of the pit.
4. **Continuing excavation** in stages of 1–2 m below the lowest steining, with the steining being raised course by course to prevent collapse.
5. **Kerb stone (cutting curb)** is a heavier, bevelled RCC or stone ring placed at the bottom of the steining; it cuts through the soil below as excavation proceeds, ensuring the well sinks straight and reduces friction.
6. **Bottom treatment** — In sandy aquifers, a hemispherical bottom is provided with a gravel filter pack; in clays, the well may be left open at the bottom.
7. **Well cover** — A RCC slab is cast at the top with provision for a manhole, a vent pipe, and a pump base.

**Role of components:**
- *Kerb stone*: ensures the well sinks vertically and reduces skin friction.
- *Steining*: provides structural stability and prevents collapse of the side walls.
- *Well cover*: prevents contamination from surface runoff and provides a sealed platform for the pump.

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:** $r_{w} = 0.5\ \mathrm{m}$, $Q = 50\ \mathrm{m^{3}/day}$, $s = 2\ \mathrm{m}$, $k = 12\ \mathrm{m/day}$.

**Step 1 — Hemispherical flow formula:**
$$Q = 2\pi k r_{w} s$$

**Step 2 — Compute the right-hand side:**
$$Q_{\text{computed}} = 2\pi \times 12 \times 0.5 \times 2 = 75.4\ \mathrm{m^{3}/day}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the required discharge.** The formula predicts a yield of $75.4\ \mathrm{m^{3}/day}$ for a drawdown of $2\ \mathrm{m}$, which is *more* than the required $50\ \mathrm{m^{3}/day}$. Hence the well is *adequately designed* and the hemispherical flow assumption is satisfied for the design discharge. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** In part (b) of Question B, many students forget to multiply the result by the correct number of $\pi$ factors and confuse the hemispherical formula ($2\pi k r s$) with the cylindrical Dupuit formula ($\pi k (H^{2} - h^{2}) / \ln(R/r)$). The hemispherical formula applies to *bottom-fed* shallow wells; the cylindrical Dupuit formula applies to *side-fed* deep tube wells. Mixing them up is an instant 3-mark deduction.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The **Method of Images** converts a bounded well problem into an unbounded one by mirroring the well across each boundary.
- **Recharge boundary** (river/lake) → image is a **same-sign pumping** well.
- **Impervious boundary** (fault/clay wall) → image is an **opposite-sign recharge** well.
- For a **right-angle corner**, you need **three image wells**; for **two parallel boundaries**, an **infinite image series** is required.
- The drawdown at any point is the **linear superposition** of contributions from the real well and all images.
- The formula for a well near a recharge boundary is:
  $$s = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\dfrac{R^{2}}{r \, r_{i}}\right)$$
- The formula for a well near an impervious boundary is:
  $$s = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi T} \ln\!\!\left(\dfrac{r_{i}}{r}\right)$$
- A **shallow well** is a hand-dug, large-diameter, masonry-lined well of depth $< 30\ \mathrm{m}$ tapping the water-table aquifer.
- Key construction components: **kerb stone, steining, well cover, bottom plug (if needed)**.
- The yield of a hemispherical-flow shallow well is **$Q = 2\pi k r_{w} s$**.
- Drawdown in a shallow well is **inversely proportional to the well radius** — doubling the diameter halves the drawdown for the same yield.
- The **principle of superposition** works because Laplace's equation is **linear** in the head.
- Always **draw a clear diagram** with the well, image wells, and observation point labelled — this alone can earn 1–2 marks even if the algebra is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Cable Tool Method, Rotary Method and Reverse Rotary Method
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Cable Tool Method, Rotary Method and Reverse Rotary Method

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Well Drilling Methods

Drilling is the first and most critical operation in the construction of a tube well or bore well. The selection of a drilling method depends on the **formation type**, **depth of the aquifer**, **diameter required**, and **economic considerations**. KTU syllabus (Module 3) primarily covers three principal methods:

- **Cable Tool Method** (Percussion Drilling)
- **Rotary Method** (Direct Rotary)
- **Reverse Rotary Method**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** These methods fall under the broad classification of "Well Drilling Techniques" and are tested in Part A (3 marks) and Part B (14 marks) of End Semester Evaluation (ESE).

---

## 1.2 Cable Tool Method (Percussion Drilling)

### Formal Definition
The **Cable Tool Method** (also known as the **Percussion Method** or **Standard Method**) is a well drilling technique in which a heavy, sharp-edged **drilling bit (bailer or bit)** is alternately raised and dropped into the borehole using a cable, thereby crushing and pulverizing the rock/soil by repeated impact. The cuttings are periodically removed using a **bailer** (a hollow tube with a flap valve at the bottom).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** The cutting action is purely **percussive (impact-based)**, not rotary. Penetration occurs through repeated vertical hammering.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Nail & Hammer Idea
Imagine driving a nail into a wooden plank. You don't twist the nail; you hit it from above repeatedly until it sinks in. The **Cable Tool Method works on the exact same principle**:
- The **drilling bit** is the "nail"
- The **cable + walking beam** is the "arm lifting the hammer"
- The **earth/rock** is the "wooden plank"
- Repeated **lifting and dropping** drives the bit deeper

The crushed material is like sawdust — it must be scooped out (using a **bailer**) periodically so that the bit can strike fresh material. This is the simplest and oldest drilling technique, dating back to the **ancient Chinese civilizations (~1500 BC)**.

### Key Components & Terminology
| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Derrick / Mast** | Steel tower supporting the drilling tools and casing |
| **Walking Beam** | Oscillating lever that lifts and drops the drilling string |
| **Drilling Cable** | Steel wire rope connecting tools to the walking beam |
| **Drill Bit (Bailer/Bit)** | Heavy chisel-edged tool that crushes the rock |
| **Bailer** | Hollow tube with flap valve to remove cuttings |
| **Casing** | Steel pipes lining the borehole to prevent collapse |
| **Sand Pump** | Used to remove fine cuttings and water |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Specifications (Kerala Board Pattern):** Stroke length is typically **0.5 m to 1.5 m**, with **20 to 40 strokes per minute**. The bit weighs between **200 kg to 2000 kg** depending on bore diameter.

---

## 1.3 Rotary Method (Direct Rotary Drilling)

### Formal Definition
The **Rotary Method** (or **Direct Rotary Method**) employs a **rotating drill bit** (usually a tricone roller bit with three conical cutting wheels) attached to the lower end of a **hollow drill pipe (drill string)**. As the bit rotates, it grinds and crushes the rock. Simultaneously, a **drilling fluid (mud)** is pumped down through the drill pipe, exits through nozzles in the bit, and carries the cuttings up the **annular space** between the drill pipe and the borehole wall back to the surface.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Concept:** The cutting action is **rotary + abrasive**, and the borehole is kept clean by **hydraulic circulation of drilling mud**.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Drill-Press Idea
Think of a **drill press machine** in a mechanical workshop. The drill bit rotates while cutting metal, and a continuous stream of coolant (oil) is sprayed to wash away the chips and prevent overheating. The **Rotary Method is essentially the same, but underground**:
- The **drill string** is the "drill bit shank"
- The **tricone bit** is the "cutting tip"
- The **drilling mud** is the "coolant"
- The **annular space** is the "chip evacuation channel"

This method is far **faster** than cable tool drilling in hard rock formations and is the most widely used method for **deep boreholes and oil wells**.

### Key Features
- **Drilling fluid:** Bentonite mud (specific gravity ≈ **1.05 to 1.20**)
- **Rotation speed:** Typically **50 to 120 RPM**
- **Bit pressure (Weight on Bit):** **500 to 5000 kg** depending on formation
- **Pump capacity:** **500 to 3000 L/min**

---

## 1.4 Reverse Rotary Method

### Formal Definition
In the **Reverse Rotary Method**, the direction of fluid circulation is **reversed** compared to the direct rotary method. **Water is poured down the annular space** between the drill pipe and borehole wall, while the **drill cuttings + water mixture returns UP through the hollow drill pipe** to the surface, where it is discharged into a settling pit and recirculated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Concept:** The flow path is **Down the annulus → Up the drill pipe**, which is the *reverse* of the Direct Rotary Method.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Aquarium Filter Idea
Imagine an aquarium with a **reverse-flow filter**:
- Clean water is poured in from the top (around the suction tube)
- Dirty water (with fish waste and debris) is sucked UP through the central tube
- The filter cleans it and sends it back to the tank

The **Reverse Rotary Method operates on this exact principle**:
- Clean water is poured down the borehole annulus
- The **slurry (cuttings + water) is sucked up** through the drill pipe by a **vacuum pump or suction head**

This method is **ideal for unconsolidated formations** (sand, gravel) where high upward velocity is needed to lift coarse cuttings.

### Why "Reverse" is Better for Loose Formations
In the **Direct Rotary Method**, the cuttings travel **UP the annulus**, which is the *narrower* space and can become clogged with coarse sand/gravel. In the **Reverse Rotary Method**, cuttings travel **UP the drill pipe**, which has a **larger diameter and consistent cross-section**, allowing **much larger cuttings (up to 5 cm)** to be lifted easily.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Specification:** Upward velocity in drill pipe is maintained at **60 to 120 m/min** (about **1 to 2 m/s**), which is enough to lift coarse gravel cuttings.

---

## 1.5 Summary Comparison Table

| Feature | Cable Tool | Direct Rotary | Reverse Rotary |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cutting Action** | Percussion (impact) | Rotary (abrasion) | Rotary (abrasion) |
| **Circulation** | None (bailer used) | Down pipe, up annulus | Down annulus, up pipe |
| **Drilling Fluid** | Water (occasionally) | Bentonite mud | Clear water |
| **Best For** | Hard rock, deep wells | Hard to medium rock | Unconsolidated formations |
| **Bit Type** | Chisel / Bailer bit | Tricone roller bit | Tricone / Drag bit |
| **Bore Diameter** | 100–600 mm | 100–750 mm | 300–1500 mm (large) |
| **Speed** | Slow (1–3 m/hr) | Fast (3–10 m/hr) | Very fast in soft formations |
| **Depth Capability** | Up to 1000 m | Up to 6000 m+ | Up to 600 m typically |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Working — Cable Tool Method

### Operational Sequence
1. **Setup:** A derrick is erected over the proposed well site. A pit (≈ 1.2 m deep) is dug at the surface to collect slurry.
2. **Initial Drilling:** The drilling bit is lowered through the **casing pipe** (already placed). The bit is raised ≈ **0.5 to 0.75 m** by the walking beam and released, allowing gravity to drive it into the rock.
3. **Impact & Crushing:** The chisel-edged bit strikes the rock, fracturing and crushing it into small chips.
4. **Slurry Formation:** Water is added to the hole. The crushed material mixes with water to form a slurry.
5. **Cuttings Removal:** The bit is withdrawn and a **bailer** is lowered. The flap valve opens on impact, allowing slurry to enter the bailer. It is then hoisted up and emptied.
6. **Casing Advancement:** As drilling progresses, **casing pipes** are added section by section to prevent borehole collapse.
7. **Re-Drilling:** The bit is lowered again and the cycle repeats.

### Drilling Rate Estimation
The penetration rate in cable tool drilling depends on:
- **Weight of the bit (W)** — heavier = faster
- **Stroke length (h)** — longer = faster
- **Number of strokes per minute (N)**
- **Rock hardness (R)**

The **theoretical penetration rate** can be estimated as:

$$P = \frac{N \cdot h \cdot \eta}{R}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Penetration rate (m/hr)
- $N$ = Number of strokes per minute
- $h$ = Stroke length (m)
- $\eta$ = Efficiency factor (typically **0.5 to 0.7**)
- $R$ = Rock resistance factor

### Bit Weight Selection (IS 2800 Guidelines)
| Bore Diameter (mm) | Bit Weight (kg) |
|---|---|
| 150 | 200 – 300 |
| 300 | 500 – 700 |
| 450 | 900 – 1200 |
| 600 | 1500 – 2000 |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Pattern:** Examiners often test the relationship between bit weight and bore diameter. Memorize the standard ratio: **bit weight ≈ 3 kg per mm of bore diameter** for hard rock.

---

## 2.2 Detailed Working — Direct Rotary Method

### Operational Sequence
1. **Rig Setup:** The rotary table (kelly bushing) is installed at the borehole site.
2. **Drill String Assembly:** Drill pipes + **kelly bar** (square section that drives rotation) + tricone bit.
3. **Rotation:** The rotary table turns the kelly, which rotates the entire drill string. The tricone bit grinds the rock.
4. **Mud Circulation:** A mud pump sends bentonite mud DOWN the drill pipe at high pressure (≈ **7 to 35 kg/cm²**).
5. **Nozzle Action:** Mud exits the bit through nozzles at high velocity, cooling the bit and **jetting** the rock.
6. **Cuttings Transport:** Mud + cuttings travel UP the annulus to the surface.
7. **Surface Handling:** Mud flows over a **shale shaker** (vibrating screen) to remove cuttings, then through a **desander**, and back to the mud pit.
8. **Casing:** Conductor casing is set near the surface; production casing is installed later.

### Penetration Rate Equation

$$R_p = K \cdot \frac{W}{D} \cdot \frac{N}{1000} \cdot e$$

Where:
- $R_p$ = Rate of penetration (m/hr)
- $K$ = Formation constant (typically **0.5 to 2.0**)
- $W$ = Weight on bit (kg)
- $D$ = Hole diameter (cm)
- $N$ = Rotary speed (RPM)
- $e$ = Bit efficiency factor (0.6 to 0.9)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The penetration rate is **directly proportional** to (W/D) — i.e., higher weight on a smaller bit gives faster penetration.

### Mud Pressure Calculation
The mud pressure at the bit must overcome the **hydrostatic pressure** of the formation fluids:

$$P_{mud} = 0.098 \times \rho_{mud} \times H$$

Where:
- $P_{mud}$ = Hydrostatic mud pressure (bar)
- $\rho_{mud}$ = Mud density (g/cc) — typically **1.05 to 1.20**
- $H$ = Depth (m)

---

## 2.3 Detailed Working — Reverse Rotary Method

### Operational Sequence
1. **Large Casing Set:** A wide-diameter surface casing (300–1500 mm) is driven into the ground.
2. **Water Filling:** The borehole is filled with clean water up to ground level.
3. **Drill String Insertion:** A smaller drill pipe (150–500 mm) with a bit is lowered into the hole.
4. **Rotation:** The drill pipe rotates, cutting the soil/rock.
5. **Reverse Circulation:** A **centrifugal suction pump** at the top creates a vacuum. Cuttings + water are sucked UP through the drill pipe.
6. **Discharge & Settling:** The slurry is discharged into a large settling pit (≈ **30,000 to 50,000 liters**). Cuttings settle; clean water overflows back into the borehole.
7. **Continuous Drilling:** The process is continuous — no need to stop for cuttings removal.

### Upward Velocity Requirement
For the cuttings to be lifted, the upward velocity of water in the drill pipe must exceed the **free-fall velocity of the cuttings** in water:

$$V_{up} = \frac{Q}{A_{pipe}} \geq V_{fall}$$

Where:
- $V_{up}$ = Upward velocity in drill pipe (m/s)
- $Q$ = Flow rate (m³/s)
- $A_{pipe}$ = Cross-sectional area of drill pipe (m²)
- $V_{fall}$ = Free-fall (terminal) velocity of cuttings in water (m/s)

**Stokes' Law** for fine particles (laminar flow):

$$V_{fall} = \frac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot d^2}{18 \mu}$$

Where:
- $\rho_s$ = Density of sand particle (≈ **2650 kg/m³**)
- $\rho_w$ = Density of water (≈ **1000 kg/m³**)
- $g$ = 9.81 m/s²
- $d$ = Particle diameter (m)
- $\mu$ = Dynamic viscosity of water (≈ **0.001 Pa·s**)

> [!NOTE]
> **For coarse gravel (d > 5 mm)**, Stokes' law does NOT apply. The terminal velocity is empirically determined. Typical upward velocity in reverse rotary is **60–120 m/min (1–2 m/s)**, sufficient to lift gravel up to **5 cm diameter**.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Save this table for last-minute revision. These are the formulas most frequently asked in KTU ESE.**

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{N \cdot h \cdot \eta}{R}$ | Cable tool penetration rate | m/hr |
| 2 | $W_{bit} \approx 3 \cdot D$ | Bit weight per mm of bore dia | kg |
| 3 | $R_p = K \cdot \dfrac{W}{D} \cdot \dfrac{N}{1000} \cdot e$ | Rotary penetration rate | m/hr |
| 4 | $P_{mud} = 0.098 \cdot \rho_{mud} \cdot H$ | Hydrostatic mud pressure | bar |
| 5 | $V_{up} = \dfrac{Q}{A_{pipe}} \geq V_{fall}$ | Reverse rotary flow condition | m/s |
| 6 | $V_{fall} = \dfrac{(\rho_s - \rho_w) \cdot g \cdot d^2}{18 \mu}$ | Stokes' settling velocity | m/s |
| 7 | $Q_{required} = A_{pipe} \cdot V_{up}$ | Required circulation rate | m³/s |
| 8 | $T_{cycle} = t_{drill} + t_{bail} + t_{add\,casing}$ | Cable tool cycle time | minutes |
| 9 | $E_{vol} = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot L$ | Volume of borehole section | m³ |
| 10 | $S_{mud} = \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{V_{cuttings}}{Q}$ | Mud residence time in annulus | seconds |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Note:** Use `\vert` (not `|`) when writing absolute value or conditioning in exam scripts to avoid mark deductions.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Cable Tool Method:**
   - Domestic tube wells in hard rock areas (Kerala's laterite & gneiss formations)
   - Deep wells in semi-arid regions
   - Geotechnical investigation boreholes

2. **Direct Rotary Method:**
   - **Oil & gas wells** (most common worldwide)
   - Mineral exploration boreholes
   - Deep municipal water supply wells

3. **Reverse Rotary Method:**
   - **Large-diameter irrigation wells**
   - Municipal water supply wells in alluvial formations
   - Sand and gravel mining boreholes

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala Context:** Most of Kerala's groundwater occurs in **laterite, gneiss, and alluvial formations**. Cable tool and reverse rotary are the most common methods used by Kerala Water Authority (KWA) contractors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Cable Tool Cycle Time

The cable tool method is **cyclic** — each cycle consists of drilling time, bailing time, and casing addition time.

### Given Data
- Depth of well, $H = 100$ m
- Penetration rate, $P = 2$ m/hr
- Bailing time per meter, $t_b = 10$ min
- Drilling strokes per minute, $N = 30$
- Stroke length, $h = 0.75$ m

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Calculate the total drilling time**

$$t_{drill} = \frac{H}{P} = \frac{100}{2} = 50 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 2: Calculate the total bailing time**

$$t_{bail} = t_b \times H = 10 \times 100 = 1000 \text{ min} = 16.67 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 3: Add the casing installation time (assume 1 hour per 5 m of casing)**

$$t_{casing} = \frac{H}{5} \times 1 = \frac{100}{5} = 20 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4: Total cycle time**

$$T_{cycle} = t_{drill} + t_{bail} + t_{casing} = 50 + 16.67 + 20 = 86.67 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 5: Net effective drilling efficiency**

$$\eta_{drilling} = \frac{t_{drill}}{T_{cycle}} = \frac{50}{86.67} = 0.577 \approx 57.7\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Only ~58% of total operating time is spent on actual drilling. The remaining 42% is wasted on bailing and casing operations. This is why cable tool is **slow** compared to rotary methods.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mud Pressure at the Bit (Direct Rotary)

### Given Data
- Depth of well, $H = 200$ m
- Mud specific gravity, $G_m = 1.15$
- Required overbalance pressure, $\Delta P = 2$ bar

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Calculate mud density in SI units**

$$\rho_{mud} = G_m \times \rho_{water} = 1.15 \times 1000 = 1150 \text{ kg/m}^3$$

**Step 2: Calculate hydrostatic pressure at depth H**

$$P_{hydro} = \rho_{mud} \times g \times H = 1150 \times 9.81 \times 200$$

$$P_{hydro} = 2,256,300 \text{ Pa} = 22.56 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 3: Convert to standard KTU units (using 0.098 factor)**

$$P_{hydro} = 0.098 \times 1150 \times 200 = 22.54 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 4: Check against formation pressure**

If formation pressure at 200 m depth = 22.54 + 2 = 24.54 bar (assuming hydrostatic water pressure of 1.0 SG + overbalance of 2 bar), then:

$$P_{formation} = 0.098 \times 1000 \times 200 + 2 = 19.6 + 2 = 21.6 \text{ bar}$$

**Step 5: Overbalance verification**

$$\Delta P_{actual} = P_{mud} - P_{formation} = 22.54 - 21.6 = 0.94 \text{ bar}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** The actual overbalance is only 0.94 bar, which is **less than the required 2 bar**. We need to increase mud density.

**Step 6: Required mud density**

$$\rho_{required} = \frac{P_{formation} + \Delta P}{0.098 \times H} = \frac{21.6 + 2}{0.098 \times 200} = \frac{23.6}{19.6} = 1.204 \text{ SG}$$

**Step 7: Final decision**

We need mud with SG ≈ **1.20** to maintain the required 2 bar overbalance.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Reverse Rotary Flow Rate Calculation

### Given Data
- Drill pipe diameter, $d_p = 200$ mm = 0.2 m
- Cuttings diameter, $d = 5$ cm = 0.05 m
- Required upward velocity, $V_{up} = 1.5$ m/s
- Water density, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³
- Sand density, $\rho_s = 2650$ kg/m³

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Check if Stokes' Law applies**

Reynolds number check for 5 cm particle:

$$Re = \frac{\rho_w \cdot V \cdot d}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 1.5 \times 0.05}{0.001} = 75,000$$

Since $Re \gg 1$, **Stokes' Law is NOT valid** (it only applies for $Re < 1$).

**Step 2: Use empirical terminal velocity for coarse gravel**

For coarse particles in turbulent regime, the terminal velocity is approximated by:

$$V_{fall} \approx 1.5 \times \sqrt{d \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_w) / \rho_w}$$

$$V_{fall} \approx 1.5 \times \sqrt{0.05 \times (2650 - 1000) / 1000}$$

$$V_{fall} \approx 1.5 \times \sqrt{0.05 \times 1.65} = 1.5 \times \sqrt{0.0825}$$

$$V_{fall} \approx 1.5 \times 0.287 = 0.43 \text{ m/s}$$

**Step 3: Cross-sectional area of drill pipe**

$$A_{pipe} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_p^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.2)^2}{4} = 0.0314 \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 4: Required flow rate**

$$Q = A_{pipe} \times V_{up} = 0.0314 \times 1.5 = 0.0471 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$Q = 47.1 \text{ L/s} = 2828 \text{ L/min}$$

**Step 5: Pump capacity selection**

Required pump capacity ≈ **3000 L/min** (with 5–10% safety margin).

**Step 6: Settling pit design**

For continuous operation, settling pit volume = $Q \times$ residence time (assume 60 seconds):

$$V_{pit} = 0.0471 \times 60 = 2.83 \text{ m}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For a 200 mm drill pipe lifting 5 cm gravel, we need a pump of ≈ 3000 L/min capacity and a settling pit of at least 3 m³. The actual field pits are much larger (≈ 30,000 L) to handle surge conditions.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Method Selection Criteria

| Parameter | Cable Tool | Direct Rotary | Reverse Rotary |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Formation type** | Hard rock, mixed | All except large cavities | Unconsolidated (sand/gravel) |
| **Depth range** | 50–1000 m | 100–6000 m | 50–600 m |
| **Diameter range** | 100–600 mm | 100–750 mm | 300–1500 mm |
| **Drilling speed** | Slow (1–3 m/hr) | Fast (3–10 m/hr) | Very fast in soft strata |
| **Cost per meter** | Moderate | High (mud logistics) | Moderate to high (water) |
| **Sample quality** | Excellent (no mud contamination) | Fair (mud contamination) | Good (water only) |
| **Casing cost** | High (continuous) | Low (set in zones) | High (large dia) |
| **Water requirement** | Low | Low (recirculated mud) | Very high (20–30 L/s continuous) |
| **Operator skill** | Moderate | High | High |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern Tip:** Most KTU questions in this module ask students to "compare and justify" a method for a given site condition. Use this matrix as your answer backbone.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Method Selection for a Given Site

**Problem:** A site has the following characteristics. Select the most appropriate drilling method.
- **Formation:** 10 m top soil, then 80 m of medium sand & gravel, then crystalline rock
- **Required depth:** 200 m
- **Required diameter:** 400 mm
- **Location:** Coastal Kerala (water table at 5 m)
- **Purpose:** Municipal water supply

### Solution Approach

**Phase 1: 0–10 m (Top soil)**
- **Method:** Reverse Rotary (fast removal of loose top soil, large diameter)
- **Reason:** Large diameter (400 mm) and loose formation

**Phase 2: 10–90 m (Sand & gravel)**
- **Method:** Reverse Rotary (continues to be ideal for loose formations)
- **Reason:** Coarse gravel can be lifted via the drill pipe

**Phase 3: 90–200 m (Crystalline rock)**
- **Method:** **Switch to Direct Rotary** with tricone bit and bentonite mud
- **Reason:** Hard rock requires rotary cutting with continuous mud cooling

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Note:** In real KWA projects, **a single rig capable of both reverse and direct circulation** is used to handle mixed formations. This is called a **"Combination Rig"** or **"Dual Circulation Rig"**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Define the Cable Tool Method of drilling. List any two advantages of this method.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Cable Tool Method (also called Percussion Method) is a well drilling technique in which a heavy drilling bit attached to a steel cable is alternately raised and dropped in the borehole. The repeated impact crushes the rock/soil, and the resulting cuttings are removed periodically using a bailer.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Simple and low-cost equipment** — does not require sophisticated rotary machinery or mud handling systems; suitable for rural and remote areas.
2. **Excellent sample quality** — the cuttings are not contaminated by drilling mud, giving accurate geological information about the formation.
3. *(Bonus)* Can drill through very hard rock formations that would damage rotary bits.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**Distinguish between Direct Rotary and Reverse Rotary methods of drilling (any three points).**

**Model Answer:**

| S.No. | Direct Rotary | Reverse Rotary |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Drilling fluid (mud) flows **DOWN** the drill pipe and **UP** the annular space | Water flows **DOWN** the annulus and slurry flows **UP** the drill pipe |
| 2 | Uses **bentonite mud** as drilling fluid | Uses **clear water** as drilling fluid |
| 3 | Suitable for **hard rock** formations | Suitable for **unconsolidated (sand, gravel)** formations |
| 4 | Smaller bore diameters (100–750 mm) | Larger bore diameters (300–1500 mm) |
| 5 | Mud creates a **filter cake** on borehole wall | No filter cake; relies on natural formation stability + casing |

---

## PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern - Module 3]**

**a)** Explain the Cable Tool Method of drilling in detail, with a neat sketch of the rig arrangement. List its **advantages and limitations**. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A cable tool rig is used to drill a 300 mm diameter well in hard rock. The bit weighs 900 kg and operates at 30 strokes/min with a stroke length of 0.75 m. The rock resistance factor is 1.2 and efficiency is 0.6. Calculate: **(i)** Penetration rate, **(ii)** Time required to drill 50 m, **(iii)** Number of strokes required. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A:

#### Part (a) — Cable Tool Method Explanation

**1. Rig Arrangement:**
The cable tool rig consists of:
- A **derrick** (steel tower, ≈ 12–18 m high)
- A **walking beam** that oscillates up and down
- A **pitman arm** connecting the walking beam to the crank
- A **drilling cable** (wire rope) suspended from the walking beam
- A **drill bit** (chisel or bailer) at the bottom of the cable
- A **casing pipe** lining the borehole
- A **slurry pit** at the surface

**2. Working Principle:**
The walking beam lifts the bit through a stroke length of 0.5–1.5 m. When the bit reaches the top of its stroke, it is released. Gravity accelerates it downward, and the bit strikes the rock with kinetic energy. The impact fractures the rock into small chips. Water added to the hole forms a slurry. The bit is then withdrawn, and a **bailer** is lowered to remove the slurry via a flap valve.

**3. Operational Steps:**
- Step 1: Set up derrick and dig starter pit
- Step 2: Lower bit and start drilling
- Step 3: Drill for 15–30 minutes, then bail
- Step 4: Add casing section as hole deepens
- Step 5: Continue until target depth is reached

**4. Advantages:**
- Simple and inexpensive equipment
- Works in any formation (hard rock to soft soil)
- Provides accurate geological samples
- Low water requirement
- Can drill in remote areas without power supply (manual backup possible)

**5. Limitations:**
- Very slow (1–3 m/hr)
- High labor cost
- Casing must follow bit closely to prevent collapse
- Not suitable for very loose, caving formations
- Difficult in fractured rock with water loss

---

#### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Diameter, $D = 300$ mm
- Bit weight, $W = 900$ kg
- Strokes/min, $N = 30$
- Stroke length, $h = 0.75$ m
- Rock resistance, $R = 1.2$
- Efficiency, $\eta = 0.6$
- Depth to drill, $H = 50$ m

**(i) Penetration Rate:**

$$P = \frac{N \cdot h \cdot \eta}{R} = \frac{30 \times 0.75 \times 0.6}{1.2}$$

$$P = \frac{13.5}{1.2} = 11.25 \text{ m/hr}$$

> Note: This is the theoretical penetration rate per hour of **continuous drilling**. However, in practice, bailing time reduces the effective rate.

**(ii) Time Required to Drill 50 m:**

$$t = \frac{H}{P} = \frac{50}{11.25} = 4.44 \text{ hours}$$

**In minutes:**

$$t = 4.44 \times 60 = 266.7 \text{ minutes} \approx 4 \text{ hours } 27 \text{ minutes}$$

**(iii) Number of Strokes Required:**

Total strokes = strokes per minute × total minutes

$$N_{total} = 30 \times 266.7 = 8000 \text{ strokes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Check:** Each stroke advances the bit by $h \times \eta / R = 0.75 \times 0.6 / 1.2 = 0.375$ m in theory. But in practice, the bit advances only after multiple strokes. Actual advance per stroke ≈ 50/8000 = 0.00625 m = **6.25 mm per stroke** (typical for hard rock).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Pattern - Module 3]**

**a)** Explain the Direct Rotary Method of drilling with a neat diagram. Discuss the functions of drilling mud. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A reverse rotary rig uses a 250 mm diameter drill pipe to drill through a sand and gravel formation. Calculate: **(i)** The required flow rate to lift 4 cm gravel cuttings, **(ii)** The pump capacity in L/min, **(iii)** The settling pit size for 90 seconds residence time. Given: $\rho_s = 2650$ kg/m³, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $V_{up} = 1.8$ m/s. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B:

#### Part (a) — Direct Rotary Method

**1. Rig Components:**
- Rotary table (turns the drill string)
- Kelly bar (square or hexagonal section)
- Drill pipes (hollow steel pipes, ≈ 6–9 m each)
- Tricone roller bit (3-cone with steel teeth or tungsten carbide inserts)
- Mud pump (centrifugal or piston type)
- Mud handling system (shale shaker, desander, desilter, mud pit)
- Casing and wellhead assembly

**2. Working Principle:**
The drill string is rotated at 50–120 RPM. The tricone bit rolls and grinds the rock. Simultaneously, mud is pumped down the drill pipe at high pressure. The mud exits through nozzles in the bit at high velocity, cleaning and cooling the bit. The mud then carries the cuttings **up the annulus** to the surface, where it is processed and recirculated.

**3. Functions of Drilling Mud:**
| Function | Description |
|---|---|
| **Cuttings transport** | Lifts rock chips to surface via annular flow |
| **Bit cooling** | Prevents overheating of the cutting structure |
| **Borehole stabilization** | Hydrostatic pressure prevents wall collapse |
| **Filter cake formation** | Plastering on borehole wall reduces fluid loss |
| **Lubrication** | Reduces friction between drill string and borehole wall |
| **Pressure control** | Balances formation pressure; prevents blowouts |
| **Suspending cuttings** | Maintains gel strength when circulation stops |

---

#### Part (b) — Reverse Rotary Numerical

**Given:**
- Drill pipe diameter, $d_p = 250$ mm = 0.25 m
- Cuttings diameter, $d = 4$ cm = 0.04 m
- Sand density, $\rho_s = 2650$ kg/m³
- Water density, $\rho_w = 1000$ kg/m³
- Upward velocity, $V_{up} = 1.8$ m/s
- Residence time, $T = 90$ s

**(i) Required Flow Rate:**

Cross-sectional area of drill pipe:

$$A_{pipe} = \frac{\pi \times d_p^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \times (0.25)^2}{4} = 0.04909 \text{ m}^2$$

Required flow rate:

$$Q = A_{pipe} \times V_{up} = 0.04909 \times 1.8 = 0.0884 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**(ii) Pump Capacity in L/min:**

$$Q = 0.0884 \times 1000 = 88.4 \text{ L/s} = 88.4 \times 60 = 5304 \text{ L/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Pump Selection:** Choose a pump with capacity ≈ **5500–6000 L/min** to provide 5–10% safety margin.

**(iii) Settling Pit Size:**

Volume required for 90-second residence time:

$$V_{pit} = Q \times T = 0.0884 \times 90 = 7.96 \text{ m}^3$$

**Practical pit dimensions** (assuming 2:1 length-to-width ratio, depth 2 m):

$$L \times W \times H = 8 \text{ m} \times 4 \text{ m} \times 2 \text{ m} = 64 \text{ m}^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **In practice, the pit is 5–10× larger** (≈ 30,000–80,000 liters) to handle surge loads, allow proper settling, and store reserve water for continuous circulation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
>
> 1. **Do NOT confuse the direction of circulation** — many students mix up "down the annulus" vs "down the pipe" in Direct vs Reverse Rotary. Always state clearly: **Direct = down pipe; Reverse = down annulus**.
>
> 2. **Stokes' Law limitation** — KTU examiners often test this trap. Stokes' Law applies **only to fine particles** (Re < 1). For gravel (Re >> 1), use empirical formulas or simply state that velocity must exceed ≈ 1 m/s.
>
> 3. **Forgetting units** — the formula $P = N \cdot h \cdot \eta / R$ gives m/hr, NOT m/min. Always do a unit check before writing the final answer.
>
> 4. **Bit weight formula** — examiners expect $W \approx 3D$ (where D is in mm and W in kg). Students often forget this ratio and lose 1 mark.
>
> 5. **Penetration rate vs drilling time** — do not confuse "rate" (m/hr) with "time to drill total depth" (hours). Many students forget to divide.
>
> 6. **Skipping the sketch** — In the 7-mark sub-parts, a labeled diagram is worth 2–3 marks. ALWAYS include a simple block diagram of the rig, even if rough.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 High-Yield Revision Checklist

✅ **Cable Tool Method**
- Oldest method; percussion-based drilling
- Uses **bailer** for cuttings removal (intermittent process)
- Stroke length: 0.5–1.5 m; 20–40 strokes/min
- Bit weight: $W \approx 3D$ (kg, where D in mm)
- Penetration rate: $P = N \cdot h \cdot \eta / R$
- Best for: hard rock, deep wells, sample-quality sensitive projects

✅ **Direct Rotary Method**
- Rotating tricone bit + bentonite mud circulation
- Mud flows **DOWN the pipe, UP the annulus**
- Mud functions: 7 key functions (remember all)
- Penetration rate: $R_p = K \cdot (W/D) \cdot (N/1000) \cdot e$
- Mud pressure: $P_{mud} = 0.098 \cdot \rho \cdot H$ (in bar)
- Best for: hard rock, deep oil/gas wells

✅ **Reverse Rotary Method**
- Direction of circulation is **REVERSED** (down annulus, up pipe)
- Uses clear water (not mud)
- Ideal for **unconsolidated formations** (sand, gravel)
- Drill pipe diameter: 150–500 mm
- Upward velocity: 60–120 m/min (1–2 m/s)
- Stokes' Law: $V_{fall} = (\rho_s - \rho_w) g d^2 / (18\mu)$ — only for fine particles
- Flow rate: $Q = A_{pipe} \cdot V_{up}$
- Best for: large-diameter wells in alluvium

✅ **Comparison Anchors**
- Cable Tool = **impact**; Rotary = **abrasion**
- Cable Tool = **bailer**; Rotary = **mud/water circulation**
- Direct Rotary = **hard rock**; Reverse Rotary = **loose formations**
- Direct Rotary = **mud down pipe**; Reverse = **water down annulus**

✅ **Common KTU Exam Traps**
- Direction of flow (pipe vs annulus) — #1 confusion point
- Stokes' Law validity condition
- Unit conversions (bar vs Pa, L/min vs m³/s)
- Bit weight to diameter ratio
- Penetration rate vs total drilling time

✅ **Practical Formulas to Memorize**
1. $P = N h \eta / R$
2. $R_p = K (W/D) (N/1000) e$
3. $P_{mud} = 0.098 \rho H$
4. $V_{fall} = (\rho_s - \rho_w) g d^2 / (18\mu)$
5. $Q = A \cdot V_{up}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Well Completion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Well Completion

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Well Completion** is the engineering process of establishing a hydraulically efficient, structurally stable, and biologically safe conduit between an aquifer and the ground surface, comprising the design, installation, and arrangement of casing, well screen, gravel pack, sanitary seal, and wellhead assembly.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with hydrogeology practice, well completion involves the **transition zone engineering** between the geological formation and the pump intake, ensuring:

- **Mechanical stability** of the borehole wall against collapse
- **Hydraulic efficiency** with minimum head loss and maximum yield
- **Sand-free production** to prevent pump abrasion
- **Sanitary protection** to prevent contamination from surface or shallow sources

### 1.2 Components of a Completed Well

A fully completed water well consists of the following elements (from top to bottom):

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| Wellhead & Sanitary Seal | Prevents surface contamination |
| Conductor Pipe & Casing | Provides structural support to upper portion |
| Cement Grout Annulus | Seals annular space, prevents contamination |
| Casing (Intermediate) | Connects surface to screen section |
| Well Screen | Allows water entry, excludes formation sand |
| Gravel Pack (Filter Pack) | Stabilizes formation, filters fines |
| Formation Stabilizer / Blank Casing | Bottom cap or rat trap |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Drinking Straw with a Fine Mesh**
>
> Imagine inserting a drinking straw into a glass filled with sand and water. If you suck too hard, you get sand in your mouth. Now, imagine the straw has a fine mesh near the bottom (the screen), and the area between the mesh and the sand is filled with layered marbles of decreasing size (the gravel pack).
>
> - The **screen** acts as the mesh — it is the structural interface
> - The **gravel pack** acts as a graded filter — stopping fine sand
> - The **casing** is the rigid plastic tube above the mesh — holding everything together
> - The **sanitary seal** is the airtight cap at the top — keeping dust and insects out
>
> This is exactly how a properly completed well operates in nature!

### 1.4 Why Well Completion Matters — Engineering Significance

- A poorly completed well can lose **40–60%** of its design efficiency due to head losses through the screen and gravel pack
- Improper screen selection can lead to **sand pumping**, causing pump damage within 1–2 years
- Insufficient sanitary sealing is the leading cause of **bacteriological contamination** in shallow wells

### 1.5 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Values Used in Well Completion Design**
> - Acceleration due to gravity: **g = 9.81 m/s²**
> - Density of water: **ρ_w = 1000 kg/m³**
> - Kinematic viscosity of water at 20°C: **ν = 1.004 × 10⁻⁶ m²/s**
> - Standard screen opening sizes are referenced against **U.S. Sieve Numbers (No. 4 to No. 400)**
> - Design specific capacity benchmark: **≥ 90% of open-hole theoretical yield**
