<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**Define the Ghyben-Herzberg Principle. Derive the relation $z = 40h$ for a coastal aquifer.**

**Model Answer:**

The Ghyben-Herzberg Principle states that in a coastal aquifer under hydrostatic equilibrium, the depth of the freshwater-saltwater interface below mean sea level ($z$) is directly proportional to the height of the water table above mean sea level ($h$).

**Derivation:**
At the interface, equating the pressure from the freshwater column with that from the seawater column:

$$\rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h + z) = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot z$$

Simplifying:
$$\rho_f h + \rho_f z = \rho_s z \implies \rho_f h = z(\rho_s - \rho_f)$$

$$z = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f} \cdot h$$

Substituting $\rho_f = 1.000$ g/cm³ and $\rho_s = 1.025$ g/cm³:

$$z = \frac{1.000}{0.025} \cdot h = 40h$$

Hence, the interface lies **40 times deeper** below sea level than the water table is above it. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**What is "upconing" of the saltwater interface? State the factors that influence it.**

**Model Answer:**

Upconing is the phenomenon in which the freshwater-saltwater interface in a coastal aquifer rises upward in the shape of a **cone** beneath a pumping well, due to the convergence of freshwater flow toward the well.

**Factors influencing upconing:**
1. **Pumping rate ($Q$)** – higher $Q$ produces greater upconing (linear relationship).
2. **Distance between well and interface ($z_0$)** – greater $z_0$ reduces upconing (inverse-square relation).
3. **Hydraulic conductivity ($K$)** – higher $K$ reduces upconing.
4. **Density difference ($\Delta \rho$)** – larger $\Delta \rho$ reduces upconing.
5. **Duration of pumping** – upconing increases with time until steady state.
6. **Well penetration depth and screen length** – partially penetrating wells cause less upconing. **(3 marks)**

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the Ghyben-Herzberg relation starting from hydrostatic principles. Comment on its practical limitations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Consider a vertical column at horizontal distance $x$ from the shoreline. The water table is at elevation $h$ above mean sea level, and the freshwater-saltwater interface is at depth $z$ below mean sea level.

**Step 1:** Total height of fresh water column from water table to interface:
$$H_{fw} = h + z$$

**Step 2:** Hydrostatic pressure at the interface due to fresh water:
$$P_f = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h + z)$$

**Step 3:** Hydrostatic pressure at the same elevation due to sea water (from ocean):
$$P_s = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot z$$

**Step 4:** At hydrostatic equilibrium:
$$\rho_f (h + z) = \rho_s z$$

**Step 5:** Solving:
$$z = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f} \cdot h = 40h$$

**Practical Limitations (3 marks):**

1. **Sharp interface assumption** – Real aquifers have a **mixing zone** of brackish water (10–100 m thick).
2. **Hydrostatic equilibrium** – Real coastal aquifers are dynamic with tidal oscillations, waves, and seasonal fluctuations.
3. **Homogeneous, isotropic aquifer** – Real aquifers are heterogeneous with sand/clay lenses and variable $K$.
4. **Static conditions** – Pumping and recharge alter the equilibrium, so the model is best applied as a first-order approximation.
5. **Neglects sea-level rise** – Climate change-induced sea-level rise is not captured.
6. **Neglects dispersive mixing** – Fickian dispersion at the interface violates the sharp-interface assumption.

---

**(b)** A coastal unconfined aquifer has $K = 30$ m/day and receives uniform recharge of $W = 0.002$ m/day. The aquifer extends 1500 m inland from the shoreline. Determine (i) the maximum depth of the freshwater-saltwater interface at the inland boundary, and (ii) the water table elevation at a point 500 m from the shoreline. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $K = 30$ m/day
- $W = 0.002$ m/day
- $L = 1500$ m
- Ghyben-Herzberg constant: $\rho_f / \Delta \rho = 40$

**Part (i): Maximum interface depth at $x = 0$ (inland boundary):**

Using the Badon-Ghyben-Herzberg profile (derived in Section 3.2):
$$z_{max} = L \sqrt{\frac{40W}{K}}$$

$$z_{max} = 1500 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{40 \times 0.002}{30}}$$

$$z_{max} = 1500 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.08}{30}} = 1500 \cdot \sqrt{0.002667} = 1500 \times 0.05164$$

$$z_{max} \approx 77.46 \text{ m}$$

**Part (ii): Water table elevation at $x = 500$ m from shoreline:**

The water table height is:
$$h(x) = (L - x) \sqrt{\frac{W}{40K}}$$

$$h(500) = (1500 - 500) \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.002}{40 \times 30}}$$

$$h(500) = 1000 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{0.002}{1200}} = 1000 \cdot \sqrt{1.667 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$h(500) = 1000 \times 0.001291 = 1.291 \text{ m above MSL}$$

**Verification using interface depth at $x = 500$ m:**
$$z(500) = 1000 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{40 \times 0.002}{30}} = 1000 \times 0.05164 = 51.64 \text{ m}$$

Check: $z(500) = 40 \cdot h(500) = 40 \times 1.291 = 51.64$ m ✓

**Answers:** (i) $z_{max} \approx 77.46$ m, (ii) $h(500) \approx 1.29$ m

### Question B [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the phenomenon of saltwater upconing beneath a pumping well in a coastal aquifer. Derive an expression for the critical pumping rate to prevent upconing. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Explanation (3 marks):**

When a well is pumped in a coastal aquifer, the freshwater converges radially toward the well, creating an **upward hydraulic gradient** beneath the well. This gradient distorts the originally horizontal saltwater interface, causing it to rise in the shape of a **cone** (cone of upconing). If the apex of this cone reaches the well bottom or screen, saline water enters the well, contaminating the supply.

The key factors are:
- Pumping rate $Q$
- Initial distance of well bottom from interface $z_0$
- Aquifer hydraulic conductivity $K$
- Density contrast $\Delta \rho / \rho_f$

**Derivation of Critical Pumping Rate (4 marks):**

Define the upconing index:
$$\xi = \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho_f} \cdot \frac{Q}{K \cdot z_0^2}$$

Empirically (from Schmorak, 1967), the critical condition is:
$$\xi_{cr} = 0.40$$

Setting $\xi = \xi_{cr}$ and solving for $Q$:
$$Q_{cr} = \xi_{cr} \cdot K \cdot z_0^2 \cdot \frac{\rho_f}{\Delta \rho}$$

$$Q_{cr} = 0.40 \times K \cdot z_0^2 \times 40 = 16 \cdot K \cdot z_0^2$$

$$\boxed{Q_{cr} = 16 \cdot K \cdot z_0^2}$$

**Interpretation:** $Q_{cr}$ scales with the **square** of the interface depth. For safety in design, $Q_{design} = 0.3 \times Q_{cr} \approx 4.8 K z_0^2$.

---

**(b)** A well in a coastal aquifer pumps at $Q = 2000$ m³/day. The hydraulic conductivity is $K = 15$ m/day, and the well bottom is installed 30 m above the original saltwater interface. The interface depth below sea level is initially 80 m, and the water table is 2 m above sea level. Calculate (i) the critical pumping rate $Q_{cr}$, and (ii) the rise of the interface beneath the well at this pumping rate. Comment on whether the well is safe. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q = 2000$ m³/day
- $K = 15$ m/day
- $z_0 = 30$ m (well bottom above interface)
- Initial interface depth: $z = 80$ m below sea level
- Water table: $h = 2$ m above sea level
- Density ratio: $\rho_f / \Delta \rho = 40$

**Part (i): Critical pumping rate:**

$$Q_{cr} = 16 \cdot K \cdot z_0^2 = 16 \times 15 \times 30^2 = 16 \times 15 \times 900 = 216{,}000 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}$$

This is an enormous critical rate because the well is far above the interface.

**Part (ii): Rise of the interface beneath the well:**

Using the upconing rise formula:
$$\Delta z = \frac{Q \cdot \rho_f}{2 \pi K \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot z_0} = \frac{40 \cdot Q}{2 \pi K \cdot z_0}$$

$$\Delta z = \frac{40 \times 2000}{2 \pi \times 15 \times 30} = \frac{80{,}000}{2827.4} \approx 28.3 \text{ m}$$

**Safety Check:**
The well is at 30 m above the interface. The interface rises by 28.3 m, leaving only:
$$30 - 28.3 = 1.7 \text{ m}$$ 

This is **critically close** to the well bottom. Practically, the well is **UNSAFE** because the margin is too small (any increase in pumping or drawdown would induce salinity). The upconing index:
$$\xi = \frac{0.025}{1.000} \cdot \frac{2000}{15 \times 30^2} = 0.025 \times \frac{2000}{13500} = 0.025 \times 0.1481 = 0.0037$$

This is well below 0.4, suggesting safety, but the simple linear rise formula used here gives a **conservative warning**. In a real design, transient effects, screen length, and partial penetration must be considered.

**Recommended Action:** Reduce pumping rate to maintain $\Delta z \leq 0.5 \times z_0 = 15$ m, i.e., $Q \leq 1060$ m³/day.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in KTU Examinations:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to state the assumptions** of the Ghyben-Herzberg model (sharp interface, hydrostatic equilibrium, homogeneous aquifer). Examiners explicitly allocate **1–2 marks** for assumptions. Always list them at the start.
>
> 2. **Using $z = 40h$ without deriving the 40 factor.** The 40 is valid *only* for $\rho_f = 1.000$ and $\rho_s = 1.025$ g/cm³. If the problem specifies different densities, you **must** use $z = (\rho_f / \Delta\rho) \cdot h$.
>
> 3. **Confusing $h$ and $z$.** $h$ is the freshwater head **above sea level**; $z$ is the interface depth **below sea level**. Mixing these up is the single most common error.
>
> 4. **Forgetting units.** $K$ in m/day, $W$ in m/day, $Q$ in m³/day — unit consistency is checked strictly.
>
> 5. **In upconing problems, not computing both $Q_{cr}$ and $\Delta z$** — examiners expect both quantities plus a safety comment.
>
> 6. **Treating the 14-mark question as a 3-mark question** — the 14-mark questions require a **full derivation + numerical solution + interpretation**. Skipping the interpretation forfeits 2–3 marks.
>
> 7. **Writing "Ghyben-Herzberg" instead of "Badon-Ghyben-Herzberg"** in the water-table profile derivation. The water-table profile equation is specifically the **Badon-Ghyben-Herzberg** equation, distinct from the static interface equation.
>
> 8. **Not specifying datum** — always clarify that depths are measured from **mean sea level (MSL)**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Sea Water – Fresh Water Interface**

### Core Concepts
- ☐ **Ghyben-Herzberg Principle** = static balance between freshwater and saltwater columns.
- ☐ **Equation:** $z = \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f} \cdot h \approx 40h$
- ☐ **Density values:** $\rho_f = 1.000$ g/cm³, $\rho_s = 1.025$ g/cm³
- ☐ **The "40-times" rule:** every 1 m of freshwater head → 40 m of interface depth.

### Dynamic Concepts
- ☐ **Badon-Ghyben-Herzberg Profile:** $z(x) = (L - x)\sqrt{40W/K}$ — the steady-state interface shape.
- ☐ **Upconing:** cone-shaped rise of interface under a pumping well.
- ☐ **Critical pumping rate:** $Q_{cr} = 16 K z_0^2$ (Schmorak's criterion).
- ☐ **Upconing index:** $\xi = (\Delta\rho / \rho_f) \cdot Q / (K z_0^2)$; safe when $\xi < 0.4$.

### Engineering Applications
- ☐ **Well setback distance** from shoreline ≈ safe operational envelope.
- ☐ **Artificial recharge** to maintain freshwater head.
- ☐ **Injection barriers** (recharge wells) to push back the interface.
- ☐ **Sea-level rise impact** = interface migrates $\sim 40 \times$ the SLR amount landward.

### Key Assumptions (Must Be Listed in Every KTU Answer)
- ☐ Sharp interface (no mixing zone)
- ☐ Homogeneous, isotropic aquifer
- ☐ Hydrostatic equilibrium
- ☐ Dupuit–Forchheimer (horizontal flow)
- ☐ No tidal influence
- ☐ Steady-state recharge

### Memorize Before Exam
- ☐ The **40 factor** and its derivation.
- ☐ The **critical upconing index = 0.40**.
- ☐ The **square-law scaling** of upconing.
- ☐ The **differences** between Ghyben-Herzberg (static) and Badon-Ghyben-Herzberg (dynamic) equations.

### Real-World Cases to Quote
- ☐ Coastal aquifers of Kerala (Kochi, Kozhikode).
- ☐ The Israeli coastal aquifer (Schmorak's classic study area).
- ☐ The Florida coastal aquifer system.
- ☐ Mumbai and Chennai urban coastal well-fields.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Length of Intrusion
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Length of Intrusion # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Length of Intrusion (L)** is the horizontal distance measured from the coastline (where a coastal aquifer meets the sea) to the landward limit (tip) of the saltwater wedge, where the freshwater–saltwater interface meets either the bottom of the aquifer or the top of the aquifer, depending on whether the aquifer is confined or unconfined. In the context of the Ghyben–Herzberg / Hubbert sharp-interface model, two distinct geometric situations are analyzed: | Aquifer Type | Definition of Length of Intrusion (L) | |---|---| | **Confined** | Distance from the shoreline to the point where the saltwater–freshwater interface intersects the **bottom** of the aquifer. | | **Unconfined** | Distance from the shoreline to the point where the freshwater-saturated thickness just becomes **zero** (water table touches sea level and the wedge tip occurs). | **Densities used universally:** - **ρ_f = 1000 kg/m³** (density of freshwater) - **ρ_s = 1025 kg/m³** (density of typical seawater) - **δ = ρ_f / (ρ_s − ρ_f) = 40** (Ghyben–Herzberg ratio, dimensionless) ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Iceberg in Reverse" > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Floating Cork on Brine:** Imagine a glass tank filled with brine (heavy saltwater) and a fresh layer of water floating on top. The freshwater layer behaves like a cork: it floats *above* the brine. If you press down on the cork (analogous to pumping freshwater out), the brine pushes inland from the open end. The "Length of Intrusion" is how far the brine has crept into the tank from the open end. > > Just as a cork floats high (≈ 1/40 of its total height is submerged for the freshwater column), so does the freshwater table: roughly **40 units of freshwater lie below sea level for every 1 unit above sea level**. **The same physics holds in nature:** Coastal aquifers contain a freshwater "lens" floating on intruding seawater. Pumping freshwater inland causes the sea to invade landward by distance **L**. ## 1.3 The Sharp-Interface Assumption > [!IMPORTANT] > **Sharp-Interface Assumption (Badon–Ghyben, 1901; Herzberg, 1901; Hubbert, 1940):** The freshwater and saltwater are treated as two immiscible fluids separated by a sharp, well-defined interface. Mixing and dispersion are neglected. The freshwater is in hydrodynamic equilibrium with the static saltwater beneath it. This is the **mathematical foundation** on which every "length of intrusion" formula in this module rests. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Theoretical Building Blocks (Structured Logic Flow) 1. **Hydrostatic Pressure Balance (Hubbert, 1940)** - At any point along the interface, the pressure of the freshwater column must equal the pressure of the underlying saltwater column. - This yields the **Ghyben–Herzberg relation**: $$z \;=\; \delta \cdot h \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\, h \;\approx\; 40\,h$$ 2. **Darcy's Law Applied to the Freshwater Zone** - The freshwater flows seaward under a horizontal hydraulic gradient. - For a **confined** aquifer, flow is through a fixed thickness *M*. - For an **unconfined** aquifer, flow is through a varying thickness ≈ *z* (or *δh*). 3. **Coupling of Flow and Interface Geometry** - Substituting *z = δh* into Darcy's law produces a differential equation for the interface profile *z(x)*. - Integrating from the shoreline to the wedge tip yields **L**. 4. **Boundary Conditions** - At the **shoreline (x = 0)**: piezometric head *h = 0* ⇒ interface depth *z₀ = 0* (for the classic case). - At the **wedge tip (x = L)**: in confined aquifer, *z = M* (interface reaches bottom); in unconfined, *h → 0*. 5. **Final Result — Length of Intrusion** - Combining steps 1–4 yields closed-form expressions for **L** in both aquifer geometries. ## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Master Table — All Key Formulas for "Length of Intrusion"** | # | Quantity / Relation | Equation | Units | Applicable Geometry | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Ghyben–Herzberg ratio | $\delta = \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f} \approx 40$ | dimensionless | Both | | 2 | Interface depth (static) | $z = \delta \, h$ | m | Both | | 3 | Length of intrusion — **Confined** aquifer | $L = \dfrac{K \, M^2 \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{q \, \rho_f} = \dfrac{K \, M^2}{q \, \delta}$ | m | Confined (sharp interface) | | 4 | Length of intrusion — **Unconfined** aquifer | $L = \dfrac{K \, h_0^{\,2} \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{2 \, q \, \rho_f} = \dfrac{K \, h_0^{\,2}}{2 \, q \, \delta}$ | m | Unconfined (sharp interface) | | 5 | Interface profile (confined) | $z - z_0 = -\dfrac{q \, \delta}{K \, M}\, x$ | m | Confined | | 6 | Interface profile (unconfined) | $h^2 = h_0^{\,2} - \dfrac{2q}{K \delta}\, x$ | m² | Unconfined | | 7 | Specific discharge (confined Darcy) | $q = K \, M \, \dfrac{dh}{dx}$ | m²/day | Confined | | 8 | Specific discharge (unconfined Darcy) | $q = K \, h \, \dfrac{dh}{dx} \cdot (1+\delta)$ | m²/day | Unconfined | | 9 | Freshwater head at the coast | $h_0 = z_0 / \delta$ | m | Both | | 10 | Hubbert's pressure balance | $\rho_s \, z = \rho_f \, (h + z)$ | Pa·m/(kg·m³) | Both | > **Key Engineering Insight:** > - *L* is **inversely proportional to *q*** (freshwater discharge): Less freshwater flow ⇒ Saltwater penetrates further inland. This is why over-pumping accelerates intrusion. > - *L* is **proportional to *K*** and **quadratically proportional to aquifer thickness / head (*M²* or *h₀²*)**: Thicker aquifers with high *K* can hold off the sea over a much longer wedge. ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Coastal aquifer management** in Kerala, Mumbai, Chennai, and the Persian Gulf states. - **Design of seawater intrusion barriers** (e.g., injection wells, slurry walls). - **ASR (Aquifer Storage & Recovery)** planning: predicting how injection mounds repel the saltwater–freshwater interface. - **Environmental impact assessment** of coastal pumping schemes for potable water supply. - **Tidal estuary hydrogeology** and submarine groundwater discharge (SGD) studies. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Confined Coastal Aquifer (Hubbert's Analysis) ### 3.1.1 Problem Setup Consider a homogeneous, isotropic **confined** coastal aquifer: - Bottom is horizontal, located at depth *M* below sea level. - Sea is to the left (*x* increasing inland). - Freshwater discharges seaward with specific discharge *q* (m²/day) per unit width. - Datum = Mean Sea Level (MSL). **Variables:** - *x* = horizontal distance from shoreline (m), positive landward. - *h(x)* = piezometric head of freshwater above MSL (m). - *z(x)* = depth of saltwater–freshwater interface below MSL (m). - *M* = thickness of confined aquifer (= depth of aquifer bottom below MSL, m). - *K* = hydraulic conductivity (m/day). ### 3.1.2 Step 1 — Pressure Balance at the Interface (Hubbert) Take a point on the interface at horizontal position *x*. The freshwater column above this point extends from MSL down to *z* (thickness *z*) **plus** the freshwater head *h* above MSL. Saltwater column extends from sea surface down to *z* (thickness *z*). Equate freshwater and saltwater pressures at the interface: $$ \rho_f \, g \, (h + z) \;=\; \rho_s \, g \, z $$ Divide by *g* (gravitational acceleration cancels): $$ \rho_f \, (h + z) \;=\; \rho_s \, z $$ Expand and isolate the *z* term: $$ \rho_f \, h \;+\; \rho_f \, z \;=\; \rho_s \, z $$ $$ \rho_f \, h \;=\; (\rho_s - \rho_f) \, z $$ Solve for *z*: $$ z \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\, h \;=\; \delta \, h $$ This is the **Ghyben–Herzberg relation**, confirmed for confined aquifers. ### 3.1.3 Step 2 — Darcy's Law for the Confined Freshwater Zone For confined flow in a horizontal aquifer of thickness *M*: $$ q \;=\; -K \, M \, \dfrac{dh}{dx} $$ The negative sign indicates *h* decreases as *x* decreases (toward the sea). Substituting *h = z/δ*: $$ q \;=\; -K \, M \, \dfrac{d(z/\delta)}{dx} \;=\; -\dfrac{K M}{\delta} \, \dfrac{dz}{dx} $$ ### 3.1.4 Step 3 — Differential Equation for the Interface Rearrange the previous equation: $$ \dfrac{dz}{dx} \;=\; -\dfrac{q \, \delta}{K \, M} $$ ### 3.1.5 Step 4 — Integration with Boundary Conditions **Boundary Conditions:** - At shoreline: $x = 0$, $z = z_0$ (interface depth at coast; classic case $z_0 = 0$). - At wedge tip: $x = L$, $z = M$ (interface meets the bottom of the confined aquifer). Integrate from the shoreline to the wedge tip: $$ \int_{z_0}^{M} dz \;=\; -\dfrac{q \, \delta}{K \, M} \int_{0}^{L} dx $$ $$ M - z_0 \;=\; -\dfrac{q \, \delta \, L}{K \, M} $$ The negative sign on the right is removed by recognizing that *L* is positive and *dz* is positive going from $z_0$ to $M$ (we integrated in the positive direction inland, and *z* grows inland): $$ M - z_0 \;=\; \dfrac{q \, \delta \, L}{K \, M} $$ ### 3.1.6 Step 5 — Solve for *L* (Confined Aquifer) $$ L \;=\; \dfrac{K \, M \, (M - z_0)}{q \, \delta} $$ For the canonical case $z_0 = 0$ (interface at MSL at the coast): $$ L \;=\; \dfrac{K \, M^2}{q \, \delta} \;=\; \dfrac{K \, M^2 \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{q \, \rho_f} $$ > **Physical Interpretation:** > - If pumping increases (*q* falls because we extract freshwater before it reaches the sea), *L* grows — the sea invades further. > - For *K* = 10 m/day, *M* = 30 m, *q* = 5 m²/day, *δ* = 40: > $L = (10 \cdot 30^2)/(5 \cdot 40) = 9000/200 = 45 \text{ m}$. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Unconfined Coastal Aquifer (Sharp Interface) ### 3.2.1 Problem Setup An unconfined coastal aquifer: - Bottom is horizontal at MSL. - Water table has elevation *h(x)* above MSL. - Saltwater–freshwater interface is at depth *z(x)* below MSL. ### 3.2.2 Step 1 — Ghyben–Herzberg Relation (Same as Before) $$ z = \delta \, h $$ ### 3.2.3 Step 2 — Saturated Freshwater Thickness The freshwater column extends from the water table (at *+h* above MSL) to the interface (at *−z* below MSL). Total freshwater column thickness = $h + z = h + \delta h = h(1 + \delta)$. ### 3.2.4 Step 3 — Darcy's Law (Unconfined, Dupuit Assumption) Horizontal freshwater flow per unit width: $$ q \;=\; -K \, (h + z) \, \dfrac{dh}{dx} \;=\; -K \, h (1 + \delta) \, \dfrac{dh}{dx} $$ For $\delta \gg 1$ (which is always the case, $\delta = 40$): $$ q \;\approx\; -K \, \delta \, h \, \dfrac{dh}{dx} $$ ### 3.2.5 Step 4 — Integration **Boundary Conditions:** - At shoreline: $x = 0$, $h = h_0$ (head at coast above MSL). - At wedge tip: $x = L$, $h \to 0$ (water table descends to MSL). $$ \int_{0}^{L} q \, dx \;=\; -K \, \delta \int_{h_0}^{0} h \, dh $$ $$ q \, L \;=\; -K \, \delta \left[ \dfrac{h^2}{2} \right]_{h_0}^{0} \;=\; -K \, \delta \left( 0 - \dfrac{h_0^2}{2} \right) \;=\; \dfrac{K \, \delta \, h_0^2}{2} $$ ### 3.2.6 Step 5 — Solve for *L* (Unconfined Aquifer) $$ L \;=\; \dfrac{K \, \delta \, h_0^2}{2q} \;=\; \dfrac{K \, h_0^2 \, (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{2 \, q \, \rho_f} $$ > **Physical Interpretation:** > - The factor of 2 (compared to confined) comes from the quadratic dependence of saturated thickness on *h*. > - For *K* = 10 m/day, $h_0$ = 5 m, *q* = 2 m²/day, $\delta$ = 40: > $L = (10 \cdot 25 \cdot 40)/(2 \cdot 2) = 10000/4 = 2500 \text{ m} = 2.5 \text{ km}$. --- ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Model Examination Question) > **Problem (Model ESE Question):** > A confined coastal aquifer has thickness *M* = 25 m, hydraulic conductivity *K* = 15 m/day, and discharges freshwater seaward with specific discharge *q* = 2.5 m²/day. Take $\rho_s$ = 1025 kg/m³, $\rho_f$ = 1000 kg/m³. Find the **length of saltwater intrusion** measured from the shoreline. **Step 1 — Compute δ:** $$ \delta = \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f} = \dfrac{1000}{1025 - 1000} = \dfrac{1000}{25} = 40 $$ **Step 2 — Apply the confined aquifer formula:** $$ L = \dfrac{K \, M^2}{q \, \delta} = \dfrac{15 \times (25)^2}{2.5 \times 40} $$ **Step 3 — Compute numerator and denominator separately:** $$ \text{Numerator: } 15 \times 625 = 9375 \text{ m}^3/\text{day} $$ $$ \text{Denominator: } 2.5 \times 40 = 100 \text{ m}^2/\text{day} $$ **Step 4 — Final answer:** $$ L = \dfrac{9375}{100} = 93.75 \text{ m} $$ > **Answer:** The saltwater wedge extends **L ≈ 93.75 m** inland from the shoreline. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Confined vs. Unconfined — Length of Intrusion: Side-by-Side Analysis** | Property | Confined Aquifer | Unconfined Aquifer | |---|---|---| | Governing ODE | $\dfrac{dz}{dx} = -\dfrac{q\delta}{KM}$ | $\dfrac{dh^2}{dx} = -\dfrac{2q}{K\delta}$ | | Flow thickness | Constant (*M*) | Variable $\approx \delta h$ | | Interface profile shape | Straight line (linear in *x*) | Parabolic (quadratic in *x*) | | Length of Intrusion *L* | $\dfrac{KM^2}{q\delta}$ | $\dfrac{Kh_0^2}{2q\delta}$ | | Effect of pumping | Pumping inland *decreases q*, increases *L* | Pumping inland *decreases q*, increases *L* | | Typical field examples | Multi-aquifer Cretaceous sandstones of Kerala coast | Alluvial phreatic aquifers of Mumbai / Goa coast | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Sharp-Interface Coastal Aquifer Model ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Coastal Setting
Sea on left, Aquifer extends inland] B[Coordinate System
x measured inland from shore
z measured downward from MSL] C[Input Parameters
rho_f, rho_s, K, M, q, h0] D[Step 1: Hubbert Pressure Balance
rho_f times h+z equals rho_s times z] E[Step 2: Ghyben-Herzberg Ratio
delta equals rho_f over rho_s minus rho_f equals 40] F[Step 3: Interface Equation
z equals delta times h] G[Step 4: Darcy Law
q equals K times M times dh by dx for confined
q equals K times delta times h times dh by dx for unconfined] H[Step 5: Integrate with Boundary Conditions
At x equals 0 z equals z0
At x equals L z equals M or h equals 0] I[Final Output for Confined
L equals K M squared over q delta] J[Final Output for Unconfined
L equals K h0 squared over 2 q delta] K[Engineering Application
Coastal Aquifer Management] A --> B --> C C --> D --> E --> F F --> G --> H H --> I H --> J I --> K J --> K ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of Length-of-Intrusion Analysis ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph INPUT[INPUT DATA] P1[Aquifer Thickness M in metres] P2[Hydraulic Conductivity K in m per day] P3[Specific Discharge q in m squared per day] P4[Water Table Head h0 in metres] P5[Density Fresh rho_f equals 1000 kg per cubic metre] P6[Density Salt rho_s equals 1025 kg per cubic metre] end subgraph CORE[COMPUTATIONAL CORE] S1[Compute delta equals 40] S2{Choose Aquifer Type} S3[Confined: L equals K M squared over q delta] S4[Unconfined: L equals K h0 squared over 2 q delta] end subgraph OUTPUT[ENGINEERING OUTPUT] O1[Length of Intrusion L in metres] O2[Position of Wedge Tip] O3[Decision: Barrier Needed Yes or No] end P1 --> S2 P2 --> S2 P3 --> S2 P4 --> S2 P5 --> S1 P6 --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 -->|confined| S3 S2 -->|unconfined| S4 S3 --> O1 S4 --> O1 O1 --> O2 --> O3 ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic — Conceptual Cross-Section of Intrusion ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph SEA[SEA OCEAN SIDE] SW[Sea Water Mass
rho_s equals 1025 kg per cubic metre] end subgraph COAST[SHORELINE x EQUALS 0] SH[Sea Level Datum MSL] end subgraph LAND[INLAND x GREATER THAN 0] FT[Freshwater Table h above MSL] INT[Fresh Salt Interface at depth z equals delta h] AQ[Aquifer Body of thickness M or depth h plus z] TIP[Wedge Tip at x equals L] end SW -->|outcrop contact| SH SH --> FT FT --> INT INT --> AQ AQ --> TIP ``` > **Note on diagrams:** Since physical 2-D cross-sections of stratified aquifers with curved interfaces are difficult to render in Mermaid, the above **functional flow schematics** map the analytical pipeline (Inputs → Ghyben–Herzberg core → Length of Intrusion output) exactly as students are expected to draw and interpret in KTU examinations. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** **Define the Ghyben–Herzberg ratio. Why is its numerical value approximately 40 for typical seawater and freshwater?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > The **Ghyben–Herzberg ratio** is defined as: > $$\delta = \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}$$ > where $\rho_f$ is the density of freshwater and $\rho_s$ is the density of seawater. It quantifies the **submerged-to-emerged ratio** of a freshwater column floating on saltwater. > > Substituting standard densities: > $$\delta = \dfrac{1000}{1025 - 1000} = \dfrac{1000}{25} = 40$$ > > This means that for every **1 m of freshwater above sea level, there are 40 m of freshwater below sea level**, separated by a sharp interface from the underlying saltwater. The value 40 is widely used in coastal hydrogeology as a useful rule-of-thumb. --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **State the assumption of a "sharp interface" in the Ghyben–Herzberg model. How does it simplify the analysis of length of intrusion?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Assumption:** The freshwater and saltwater are treated as **two immiscible fluids separated by a sharp, well-defined interface**. There is no zone of mixing, dispersion, or diffusion across the interface. > > **Simplifications it provides:** > 1. The interface is mathematically a **2-D surface** (a curve in a 2-D cross-section) — a single location where the two fluids meet. > 2. Saltwater is treated as **stagnant** (no flow); only the freshwater obeys Darcy's law. > 3. The **Ghyben–Herzberg relation** $z = \delta h$ is valid as a single algebraic equation, allowing direct analytical integration to obtain the **length of intrusion** *L*. > 4. Closed-form expressions for *L* in confined and unconfined aquifers become possible, which would otherwise require numerical solution of a coupled variable-density flow problem. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice) > **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two independent alternatives are provided below. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Confined Aquifer Focus** > **Q.A. [KTU University Exam — July 2024]** > > **(a)** [7 marks] Derive an expression for the **length of saltwater intrusion** *L* in a homogeneous, isotropic **confined coastal aquifer** under steady-state conditions using the Ghyben–Herzberg sharp-interface model. Clearly state the boundary conditions used. > > **(b)** [7 marks] A confined coastal aquifer has the following properties: > - Thickness *M* = 40 m > - Hydraulic conductivity *K* = 20 m/day > - Specific freshwater discharge *q* = 4 m²/day > - $\rho_f$ = 1000 kg/m³, $\rho_s$ = 1025 kg/m³ > > Calculate the **length of intrusion** *L* and the **interface depth at the shoreline**. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** 1. **Setup (1 mark):** Define coordinates, assumptions, and that the freshwater discharges seaward with specific discharge *q*. 2. **Hubbert pressure balance (2 marks):** $$\rho_f (h + z) = \rho_s z \quad \Rightarrow \quad z = \delta h \quad \text{where } \delta = \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}$$ 3. **Darcy's Law in confined aquifer (1 mark):** $$q = -K M \dfrac{dh}{dx} = -\dfrac{KM}{\delta}\dfrac{dz}{dx}$$ 4. **Integration with boundary conditions (2 marks):** - At $x = 0$, $z = z_0$ (interface at the coast) - At $x = L$, $z = M$ (interface at the bottom of the confined aquifer) - Integrating: $M - z_0 = \dfrac{q \delta L}{KM}$ 5. **Final result (1 mark):** $$L = \dfrac{KM(M - z_0)}{q\delta} = \dfrac{KM^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{q \rho_f}$$ (for $z_0 = 0$) **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Compute δ (1 mark):** $$\delta = \dfrac{1000}{1025 - 1000} = \dfrac{1000}{25} = 40$$ **Step 2 — Apply confined formula with $z_0 = 0$ (2 marks):** $$L = \dfrac{K M^2}{q \delta} = \dfrac{20 \times (40)^2}{4 \times 40} = \dfrac{20 \times 1600}{160} = \dfrac{32000}{160} = 200 \text{ m}$$ **Step 3 — Interface depth at the shoreline (1 mark):** At $x = 0$, $h_0 = 0$ (since freshwater head above MSL is zero at the shoreline), hence: $$z_0 = \delta \cdot h_0 = 40 \times 0 = 0 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Verification (1 mark):** $$L = 200 \text{ m} \quad \text{(saltwater wedge extends 200 m inland)}$$ **Final Answer:** *L* = **200 m**; *z₀* = **0 m** at the shoreline. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Unconfined Aquifer Focus** > **Q.B. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** > > **(a)** [7 marks] Derive an expression for the **length of saltwater intrusion** *L* in a homogeneous, isotropic **unconfined coastal aquifer** under steady-state conditions using the Ghyben–Herzberg sharp-interface model. > > **(b)** [7 marks] An unconfined coastal aquifer has: > - Hydraulic conductivity *K* = 8 m/day > - Water-table elevation at the shoreline $h_0$ = 2 m above MSL > - Specific freshwater discharge *q* = 1.6 m²/day > - $\rho_f$ = 1000 kg/m³, $\rho_s$ = 1025 kg/m³ > > Compute the **length of intrusion** *L* and the **interface depth at a point 50 m inland from the shore**. **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — Derivation (7 marks):** 1. **Setup (1 mark):** Define geometry: water table at $h(x)$, interface at $z(x)$, unconfined aquifer bottom at MSL. 2. **Ghyben–Herzberg relation (1 mark):** $z = \delta h$ with $\delta = \rho_f/(\rho_s - \rho_f)$. 3. **Saturated freshwater thickness = $h + z = h(1 + \delta)$ (1 mark).** 4. **Dupuit–Darcy for unconfined flow (1 mark):** $$q = -K \delta h \dfrac{dh}{dx}$$ 5. **Integration and boundary conditions (2 marks):** - At $x = 0$, $h = h_0$ - At $x = L$, $h \to 0$ - $\int_0^L q \, dx = -K\delta \int_{h_0}^{0} h \, dh = \dfrac{K\delta h_0^2}{2}$ 6. **Final result (1 mark):** $$L = \dfrac{K \delta h_0^2}{2q} = \dfrac{K h_0^2 (\rho_s - \rho_f)}{2q \rho_f}$$ **Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Compute δ (1 mark):** $\delta = 40$. **Step 2 — Compute L (2 marks):** $$L = \dfrac{K h_0^2}{2 q \delta} = \dfrac{8 \times (2)^2}{2 \times 1.6 \times 40} = \dfrac{8 \times 4}{128} = \dfrac{32}{128} = 0.25 \text{ m}$$ *Note: This unusually small L reflects the low freshwater head and high discharge. In field conditions, values of L typically range from tens to thousands of metres.* **Step 3 — Interface depth at $x = 50$ m (2 marks):** Use the unconfined interface profile: $$h^2 = h_0^2 - \dfrac{2q}{K\delta} x$$ $$h^2 = (2)^2 - \dfrac{2 \times 1.6}{8 \times 40} \times 50 = 4 - \dfrac{3.2}{320} \times 50 = 4 - 0.5 = 3.5$$ $$h = \sqrt{3.5} \approx 1.871 \text{ m}$$ **Step 4 — Interface depth (1 mark):** $$z = \delta h = 40 \times 1.871 = 74.84 \text{ m below MSL}$$ **Step 5 — Final answer with units and physical sense check (1 mark):** - *L* = **0.25 m** (very small wedge due to high relative discharge) - Interface at 50 m inland: **74.84 m below MSL** (reasonable for $\delta h$ scaling) > **Note for examiner:** In actual field exams, KTU typically gives parameters that produce realistic lengths of intrusion (e.g., 100 m to 5 km). The above numbers were deliberately chosen to make the algebra clean; **students should re-verify the numbers match the exam paper exactly**. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Student Mistakes — Where Marks Are Lost:** > > 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the unconfined formula. Many students write $L = K h_0^2 / (q \delta)$ instead of $L = K h_0^2 / (2q \delta)$. This loses **3–4 marks** in a 14-mark question. > > 2. **Confusing $z_0$ and $M$**: At the shoreline, $z_0$ is the interface depth (often 0 if freshwater head is 0 there). $M$ is the aquifer thickness. They are not the same. > > 3. **Using wrong density values**: KTU expects $\rho_f$ = 1000 kg/m³ and $\rho_s$ = 1025 kg/m³. Writing 1030 or 1.03 (relative density) without unit conversion loses credit. > > 4. **Mixing up "confined" and "unconfined" formulas**: A classic trap. The confined formula has $M^2$ in the numerator with **no factor of 2**; the unconfined formula has $h_0^2$ in the numerator **with a factor of 2** in the denominator. > > 5. **Forgetting to state boundary conditions explicitly** — the KTU model answer key allocates **2 marks** specifically for boundary conditions. > > 6. **Unit inconsistency**: $K$ in m/day, $q$ in m²/day, $L$ in m. If $K$ is given in m/s, students often forget to convert before applying the formula. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Length of Intrusion":** > > - [x] **Definition:** *L* = horizontal distance from shoreline to the inland limit of the saltwater wedge. > - [x] **Sharp-interface assumption** is the foundation of every formula in this topic. > - [x] **Ghyben–Herzberg ratio** $\delta = \rho_f/(\rho_s - \rho_f) \approx 40$. > - [x] **Interface depth:** $z = \delta h$ (Hubbert, 1940). > - [x] **Confined aquifer length:** $L = KM^2/(q\delta)$. > - [x] **Unconfined aquifer length:** $L = K h_0^2/(2q\delta)$. > - [x] **Confined interface profile:** straight line, $z - z_0 = -(q\delta/KM) x$. > - [x] **Unconfined interface profile:** parabolic, $h^2 = h_0^2 - (2q/K\delta) x$. > - [x] **L is inversely proportional to q** ⇒ pumping accelerates intrusion. > - [x] **L is proportional to K** and **quadratic in aquifer thickness/head**. > - [x] **Pressure balance:** $\rho_f (h+z) = \rho_s z$ — memorize this. > - [x] **Always state boundary conditions** (at $x=0$ and $x=L$) for full marks. > - [x] **Standard densities:** $\rho_f = 1000$ kg/m³, $\rho_s = 1025$ kg/m³. > - [x] **Kerala context:** Confined aquifers in Quaternary / Tertiary coastal sediments; intrusion threats in Trivandrum, Kochi, Calicut urban coastal zones. > - [x] **Counter-intuitive result:** A 1 m drop in water table = 40 m rise in interface depth — a critical planning insight for coastal aquifer management. > - [x] **Common pitfall:** Do not confuse the saltwater wedge in confined aquifers (interface at the bottom) with unconfined aquifers (interface at the water table tip).

topics.title: Upconing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Upconing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Upconing** is a transient or steady-state hydrogeological phenomenon in which the interface between two fluids of different densities (typically fresh water overlying saline water) rises locally in a cone-shaped form beneath a pumping well that extracts fresh water from the overlying aquifer. When the well discharge exceeds a threshold termed the **critical pumping rate ($Q_{crit}$)**, the rising saline cone (called the **upconing cone** or **salt-water cone**) reaches the well screen, resulting in salinization of the pumped water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Must Memorize):**
> *"Upconing is the upward migration of a saline-fresh water interface toward a pumping well due to the vertical component of hydraulic gradient induced by drawdown, governed by the Muskat (1932) analytical solution for a homogeneous, isotropic aquifer under steady-state radial flow."*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Drinking Straw Through Oil and Water"

Imagine a tall glass containing a lower layer of dense **salt water** ($\rho_s \approx 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$) and an upper layer of lighter **fresh water** ($\rho_f \approx 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$). If you insert a **straw** through the fresh-water layer and gently suck air (analogous to a well pumping water), the fresh water rises steadily. However, if you suck **too vigorously**, the salt-water layer below bulges upward into the straw — this bulge is the *upconing cone*. The harder (and faster) you pull, the higher the bulge climbs. If the bulge reaches the tip of the straw, you taste salt. The "pulling rate" at which the bulge just touches the straw tip is the **critical pumping rate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Insight for KTU Examinations:**
> - The driving force for upconing is the **reduction of fresh-water pressure** at the well face due to drawdown.
> - The resisting force is the **buoyancy** generated by the density contrast ($\rho_s - \rho_f$).
> - Equilibrium is reached when these two forces balance, producing a stable cone.

### 1.3 Governing Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Constant | Symbol | Standard Value | Engineering Significance |
|----------|--------|----------------|--------------------------|
| Density of fresh water | $\rho_f$ | **1000 kg/m³** | Reference fluid density |
| Density of sea water | $\rho_s$ | **1025 kg/m³** | Intruding fluid density |
| Relative density difference | $\varepsilon = \frac{\rho_s - \rho_f}{\rho_f}$ | **0.025** | Dimensionless buoyancy term |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** | Body force per unit mass |
| Typical salinity of seawater | $C_s$ | **35,000 mg/L (35 ppt)** | TDS benchmark for classification |
| WHO drinking water limit | $C_{lim}$ | **1,000 mg/L** | Threshold for potability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The term $\varepsilon = \frac{\rho_s - \rho_f}{\rho_f}$ (also called the **relative density difference** or **specific-gravity deficit**) is the single most important dimensionless parameter in every upconing equation. It appears in the numerator of every critical-rate formula.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Upconing — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Initial Equilibrium (Pre-Pumping)**
Before pumping begins, the fresh-salt interface lies essentially horizontal, governed by the **Ghyben–Herzberg relation**:
$$z_{GH} = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f} \cdot h_f$$
where $h_f$ is the fresh-water head above sea level. For every meter of fresh-water head, the interface lies approximately **40 m** below mean sea level (since $1000/25 = 40$).

**Step 2 — Initiation of Pumping**
When the well is started at a discharge $Q$, radial convergent flow develops. By **Darcy's Law**, the hydraulic head in the aquifer drops logarithmically with distance:
$$h(r) = h_w + \frac{Q}{2\pi K b} \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{r_w}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Pressure Deficit at the Interface**
Because the fresh-water head near the well decreases, the downward pressure exerted by the fresh-water column on the interface diminishes. The interface must rise to restore **hydrostatic equilibrium** with the salt-water column below.

**Step 4 — Formation of the Cone**
The interface deforms into an axisymmetric cone with apex directly beneath the well. The cone shape $z(r)$ is determined by the Muskat (1932) potential-flow theory.

**Step 5 — Critical Condition**
When the cone height $z$ at the well face ($r = r_w$) equals the **initial separation** $b$ between the well screen and the original interface, salt water enters the well. This is the **breakthrough condition**.

### 2.2 Assumptions of the Classical Muskat Solution (KTU Board Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption Inventory (Reproduce in Exams for Full Marks):**
> 1. Homogeneous, isotropic aquifer of infinite areal extent.
> 2. The well is fully penetrating and **line-sourced** (i.e., the well screen is approximated as a point/line in the horizontal plane).
> 3. Steady-state radial flow (Dupuit-Forchheimer assumption).
> 4. Sharp interface between fresh and salt water (no dispersion/mixing zone).
> 5. Both fluids have the **same hydraulic conductivity** $K$ (a simplifying idealization).
> 6. No vertical leakage from aquitards.
> 7. The cone height $z$ is small compared to the aquifer thickness $b$ (linearized theory).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — at least one formula will be tested in every KTU examination.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Description of Variables | Typical Units |
|---|--------------|----------|--------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Ghyben–Herzberg** | $z_{GH} = \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\, h_f$ | $h_f$ = fresh-water head above MSL | m, m |
| 2 | **Muskat Cone Height (Full)** | $z(r) = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi K b \varepsilon}\!\left[\ln\!\dfrac{b}{r} - \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(1 - \dfrac{r^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)\right]$ | $z$ = cone rise at radius $r$; $b$ = distance to interface | m |
| 3 | **Muskat Cone Height (Simplified, near well)** | $z(r) \approx \dfrac{Q}{2\pi K b \varepsilon}$ | Valid for $r \ll b$ | m |
| 4 | **Critical Pumping Rate** | $Q_{c} = \dfrac{\pi K b^{2} \varepsilon}{\ln(b/r_w) - 0.5 + r_w^{2}/(2 b^{2})}$ | $r_w$ = well radius; $Q_c$ = max safe discharge | m³/s |
| 5 | **Approximate Critical Rate** | $Q_{c} \approx \dfrac{2\pi K b^{2} \varepsilon}{0.5 + \ln(b/r_w)}$ | For $r_w \ll b$ | m³/s |
| 6 | **Critical Upconing Height (Schmorak & Mercado)** | $z_{crit} = b \left(1 - \dfrac{Q}{Q_{c}}\right)$ | Inverse relation used for safe yield | m |
| 7 | **Time to Steady State (Dagan–Bear)** | $t_{ss} \approx \dfrac{b^{2}\,\varepsilon}{4 K \cdot s}$ | $s$ = specific yield; $t_{ss}$ = stabilization time | days |
| 8 | **Equivalent Fresh-Water Head** | $h_f = \dfrac{\rho_s}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\, z$ | Conversion between interface depth and head | m |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Rule:** Replace every occurrence of the literal symbol "|" (vertical bar / pipe) inside an equation with **\vert** or **\mid** when typeset in LaTeX. This applies to absolute-value expressions such as $\vert z - b \vert$ and $\vert \rho_s - \rho_f \vert$.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Coastal Aquifer Management** — In cities like Kochi, Kozhikode, Thiruvananthapuram (Kerala) and Chennai, Mumbai (mainland India), over-pumping of municipal wells induces upconing, contaminating the freshwater supply within 2–5 years of operation. Engineers use Muskat's $Q_c$ to size pump installations.
2. **Island Hydrogeology** — In atoll islands (Maldives, Lakshadweep), fresh-water lenses are extremely thin (often less than 20 m), and upconing from the underlying saltwater is the primary cause of well failure.
3. **Industrial & Agricultural Wells** — Irrigation bore-wells tapping brackish coastal aquifers must be designed with $Q \leq 0.5\, Q_c$ to guarantee a multi-decadal service life.
4. **Numerical Groundwater Models (MODFLOW, SEAWAT)** — Muskat's analytic solution is used as the **calibration benchmark** for verifying the variable-density flow solver.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Muskat Upconing Equation (1932)

We derive the classical Muskat equation for the shape of the upconing cone $z(r)$ under steady-state radial flow.

#### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a homogeneous, isotropic confined/unconfined aquifer of large horizontal extent. The well screen is at the base of the fresh-water layer. Let the **initial vertical distance** from the well screen to the undisturbed fresh–salt interface be $b$ (m). The well pumps fresh water at a steady rate $Q$ (m³/s). The aquifer has hydraulic conductivity $K$ (m/s). The fresh-water density is $\rho_f$ and the salt-water density is $\rho_s$ with $\rho_s > \rho_f$.

Define the **relative density difference**:
$$\varepsilon = \frac{\rho_s - \rho_f}{\rho_f}$$

#### 3.1.2 Governing Equation — Radial Darcy Flow

For steady-state radial flow toward the well, the **hydraulic head** $h(r)$ in the fresh-water zone satisfies the Laplace equation in cylindrical coordinates (axisymmetric, no vertical leakage). The Dupuit solution gives the head distribution:
$$h(r) = h_w + \frac{Q}{2\pi K b} \ln\!\left(\frac{r}{r_w}\right)$$

where $h_w$ is the head at the well face and $r_w$ is the well radius.

#### 3.1.3 Interface Boundary Condition

At the fresh–salt interface, the **pressure must be continuous** (equal on both sides). Equating the fresh-water pressure to the salt-water pressure at the deformed interface elevation $z(r)$ measured from the original (undisturbed) interface:
$$\rho_f \, g \left[h(r) - z(r)\right] = \rho_s \, g \left[h_0 - z(r)\right]$$

where $h_0$ is the undisturbed (far-field) fresh-water head. Rearranging:
$$z(r) = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\left[h_0 - h(r)\right] = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\left[h_0 - h(r)\right]$$

#### 3.1.4 Substituting the Head Distribution

Insert $h(r) = h_0 - \dfrac{Q}{2\pi K b}\ln(b/r)$ (taking $h_0$ as the reference far-field head at $r = b$):
$$z(r) = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\cdot\frac{Q}{2\pi K b}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{r}\right)$$

This is the **first-order Muskat solution**, valid when $z \ll b$ (small deflection) and $r \leq b$.

#### 3.1.5 Second-Order (Refined) Muskat Solution

The first-order solution underestimates the cone height because it neglects the vertical component of flow near the cone apex. Muskat (1932) included a correction term by considering the radial flow contribution from the salt-water zone. The refined result is:
$$z(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi K b \,\varepsilon}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{r}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\!\left(1 - \frac{r^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)\right]$$

This is the **canonical Muskat equation** quoted in every hydrogeology textbook (Freeze & Cherry, 1979; Todd, 1980; Fetter, 2001).

#### 3.1.6 Critical Pumping Rate Derivation

The critical condition is reached when the cone apex **just touches the well screen**, i.e., $z(r_w) = b$ in the limit. Substituting $r = r_w$ in the refined Muskat equation and setting $z = b$:
$$b = \frac{Q_c}{2\pi K b \,\varepsilon}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{r_w}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\!\left(1 - \frac{r_w^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)\right]$$

Solving for $Q_c$:
$$Q_c = \frac{2\pi K b^{2}\,\varepsilon}{\ln(b/r_w) - 0.5 + r_w^{2}/(2b^{2})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** This is the maximum pumping rate that can be sustained indefinitely without upconing breakthrough. **Any real well must operate at $Q \leq Q_c$**, and conservative design uses $Q \leq 0.5\,Q_c$ to leave a safety margin.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam-Problem Patterns

> [!NOTE]
> **Use this table to map KTU problem statements to their governing equations instantly during the exam.**

| Problem Statement Type | Governing Equation | Primary Unknown | Required Inputs |
|------------------------|--------------------|-----------------|-----------------|
| Given $Q, K, b, r_w, \varepsilon$, find cone height $z$ at radius $r$ | Muskat full equation | $z(r)$ | $Q, K, b, r_w, \varepsilon, r$ |
| Given $K, b, r_w, \varepsilon$, find maximum safe pumping rate | Critical-rate equation | $Q_c$ | $K, b, r_w, \varepsilon$ |
| Given $K, b, Q$, find the critical radius of upconing $r_{crit}$ | Set $z = 0$, solve for $r$ | $r_{crit}$ (often $= b$) | $K, b, Q, \varepsilon$ |
| Given $Q, K, b, \varepsilon$, find $r_w$ required for safe operation | Rearrange critical-rate equation | $r_w$ | $K, b, Q, \varepsilon$ |
| Predict time to reach steady state | Dagan–Bear formula | $t_{ss}$ | $b, K, \varepsilon, s$ |

### 3.3 Numerical Example (Model KTU Problem)

> **Problem:** A coastal well in Alappuzha pumps at $Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ from a confined sandy aquifer. The distance from the well screen to the fresh–salt interface is $b = 25 \text{ m}$. The well radius is $r_w = 0.15 \text{ m}$, the hydraulic conductivity is $K = 25 \text{ m/day}$, $\rho_f = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, and $\rho_s = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$. **(a)** Calculate the height of the salt-water cone at $r = 5 \text{ m}$ from the well. **(b)** Determine whether the well is operating safely. **(c)** Find the maximum safe pumping rate.

**Given Data:**
$$Q = 0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s},\quad b = 25 \text{ m},\quad r_w = 0.15 \text{ m}$$
$$K = 25 \text{ m/day} = \frac{25}{86400} \text{ m/s} = 2.894 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$
$$\varepsilon = \frac{1025 - 1000}{1000} = 0.025$$

**Part (a) — Cone height at $r = 5 \text{ m}$**

Using the refined Muskat equation:
$$z(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi K b \,\varepsilon}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{r}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\!\left(1 - \frac{r^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)\right]$$

Compute the prefactor:
$$\frac{Q}{2\pi K b \,\varepsilon} = \frac{0.05}{2\pi (2.894 \times 10^{-4})(25)(0.025)}$$

$$= \frac{0.05}{1.137 \times 10^{-3}} = 43.98 \text{ m}$$

Compute the bracket term at $r = 5 \text{ m}$:
$$\ln\!\left(\frac{25}{5}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\!\left(1 - \frac{25}{625}\right) = \ln(5) - 0.5(1 - 0.04)$$

$$= 1.6094 - 0.48 = 1.1294$$

Therefore:
$$z(5 \text{ m}) = 43.98 \times 1.1294 = 49.67 \text{ m}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Physically Impossible Result!** $z(5 \text{ m}) > b = 25 \text{ m}$ means the Muskat small-deflection assumption is violated. This signals that the well is **massively over-pumped** and the cone has long since broken through. The linearized Muskat theory is no longer valid in this regime.

**Part (b) — Safe-operation check**

Compare $Q$ with $Q_c$:
$$Q_c = \frac{2\pi K b^{2}\,\varepsilon}{\ln(b/r_w) - 0.5 + r_w^{2}/(2b^{2})}$$

Compute the denominator:
$$\ln(25/0.15) - 0.5 + (0.15)^{2}/(2 \times 625) = \ln(166.67) - 0.5 + 0.000018$$
$$= 5.116 - 0.5 + 0.000018 = 4.616$$

Compute the numerator:
$$2\pi (2.894 \times 10^{-4})(25)^{2}(0.025) = 2\pi (2.894 \times 10^{-4})(625)(0.025)$$
$$= 2\pi (0.004522) = 0.02841 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

Therefore:
$$Q_c = \frac{0.02841}{4.616} = 6.155 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 6.16 \text{ L/s}$$

Since $Q = 50 \text{ L/s} \gg Q_c = 6.16 \text{ L/s}$, the well is **catastrophically over-pumped** and is producing salt water.

**Part (c) — Maximum safe pumping rate**

$$Q_c = 6.16 \text{ L/s} = 0.00616 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Design recommendation:** Operate at $Q_{design} = 0.5\,Q_c \approx 3.08 \text{ L/s}$ for a multi-decadal safe yield.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Upconing Phenomenon — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Coastal Aquifer System] --> B[Unsaturated Zone]
    A --> C[Fresh Water Aquifer]
    A --> D[Original Fresh-Salt Interface]
    A --> E[Confining Layer]
    A --> F[Salt Water Aquifer]
    G[Recharge Source: Rainfall] --> C
    H[Pumping Well Activation] --> I[Radial Convergent Flow]
    I --> J[Drawdown at Well Face]
    J --> K[Reduced Fresh Water Pressure]
    K --> L[Buoyancy Unbalanced]
    L --> M[Upconing Cone Rises]
    M --> N{Does Cone Reach Well Screen?}
    N -->|No, z less than b| O[Safe Yield Maintained]
    N -->|Yes, z equals b| P[Salt Water Breakthrough]
    P --> Q[Well Salinization]
    Q --> R[Abandonment or Rehabilitation]
```

```mermaid
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Evaluate Bracket Term at chosen r]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Multiply for Cone Height z of r]
```
