<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP PECET751]`
> **Define upconing. List any four assumptions of Muskat's classical upconing theory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Upconing is the steady-state or transient rise of the fresh–salt water interface in the form of a cone directly beneath a pumping well, caused by the drawdown-induced reduction in fresh-water pressure. When the cone apex reaches the well screen, saline water is entrained into the pumped discharge.

**Assumptions of Muskat's Theory (2 marks — list any four):**
1. The aquifer is **homogeneous and isotropic** with hydraulic conductivity $K$.
2. The well is **fully penetrating** and treated as a line source of infinite length.
3. **Steady-state** radial flow is assumed (Dupuit–Forchheimer approximation).
4. The fresh–salt interface is a **sharp boundary** (no dispersive mixing zone).
5. Both fluids share the **same hydraulic conductivity** $K$ (a simplification).
6. The cone rise $z$ is **small** compared with the aquifer thickness $b$.

---

> **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Model QP PECET751]`
> **Explain the concept of "critical pumping rate" in the context of upconing. State the Muskat formula for $Q_c$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** The **critical pumping rate $Q_c$** is the maximum discharge that a coastal well can sustain indefinitely without the rising salt-water cone reaching the well screen. Operationally, if $Q \leq Q_c$, the cone stabilises at a height $z < b$ and the pumped water remains fresh. If $Q > Q_c$, the cone grows without bound and salt water enters the well. Conservative design uses $Q_{design} = 0.5\,Q_c$ to provide a safety margin against transient disturbances.

**Muskat Formula (1 mark):**
$$Q_c = \frac{2\pi K b^{2}\,\varepsilon}{\ln(b/r_w) - 0.5 + r_w^{2}/(2 b^{2})}$$

where $K$ is hydraulic conductivity, $b$ is the well-to-interface distance, $r_w$ is the well radius, and $\varepsilon = (\rho_s - \rho_f)/\rho_f$ is the relative density difference.

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a)** Starting from the Ghyben–Herzberg relation, derive the Muskat equation for the height of the upconing cone $z(r)$ in a coastal aquifer under steady-state radial flow to a fully penetrating pumping well. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A coastal community in Thrissur district operates a pumping well with the following data: $K = 30 \text{ m/day}$, $b = 30 \text{ m}$, $r_w = 0.20 \text{ m}$, $\rho_f = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$, $\rho_s = 1025 \text{ kg/m}^3$. The well currently pumps at $Q = 0.015 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$. **(i)** Compute the critical pumping rate $Q_c$. **(ii)** Advise whether the well can continue at the current rate, and recommend a safe design discharge. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark):* The **Ghyben–Herzberg relation** at the undisturbed interface states:
$$\rho_f \cdot h_f = \rho_s \cdot z_0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad z_0 = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\,h_f = \frac{h_f}{\varepsilon}$$
where $z_0$ is the static interface depth below sea level and $h_f$ is the fresh-water head above MSL.

*Step 2 (1 mark):* When the well pumps, the fresh-water head $h(r)$ at radius $r$ drops. The Dupuit steady-state radial solution gives:
$$h(r) = h_0 - \frac{Q}{2\pi K b}\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{r}\right)$$

*Step 3 (1 mark):* The new interface position $z(r)$ must satisfy the continuity of pressure at the deformed interface:
$$\rho_f\,g[h(r) - z(r)] = \rho_s\,g[h_0 - z(r)]$$

Solving for $z(r)$:
$$z(r)\left(\rho_s - \rho_f\right) = \rho_f\left[h_0 - h(r)\right]$$
$$z(r) = \frac{1}{\varepsilon}\left[h_0 - h(r)\right]$$

*Step 4 (1 mark):* Substituting the head distribution:
$$z(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi K b\,\varepsilon}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{r}\right)$$

*Step 5 (1 mark):* Including Muskat's second-order correction (radial flow from the salt-water zone):
$$z(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi K b\,\varepsilon}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{r}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\!\left(1 - \frac{r^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)\right]$$

*Step 6 (1 mark):* **Assumptions** (state any 4 for 1 mark): homogeneous isotropic aquifer, steady-state radial flow, sharp interface, both fluids have the same $K$, cone rise $z \ll b$, well fully penetrating.

*Step 7 (1 mark):* The **critical rate** is obtained by setting $z(r_w) = b$ and solving:
$$Q_c = \frac{2\pi K b^{2}\,\varepsilon}{\ln(b/r_w) - 0.5 + r_w^{2}/(2 b^{2})}$$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Data conversion (1 mark):*
$$K = 30 \text{ m/day} = \frac{30}{86400} = 3.472 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s}$$
$$\varepsilon = \frac{1025 - 1000}{1000} = 0.025$$
$$b = 30 \text{ m},\quad r_w = 0.20 \text{ m},\quad Q = 0.015 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

*Step 2 — Compute the denominator of $Q_c$ (1 mark):*
$$\ln(b/r_w) = \ln(30/0.20) = \ln(150) = 5.011$$
$$\text{Denom} = 5.011 - 0.5 + \frac{(0.20)^{2}}{2(30)^{2}} = 5.011 - 0.5 + 0.000022 = 4.511$$

*Step 3 — Compute the numerator (1 mark):*
$$\text{Num} = 2\pi K b^{2}\,\varepsilon = 2\pi (3.472 \times 10^{-4})(30)^{2}(0.025)$$
$$= 2\pi (3.472 \times 10^{-4})(900)(0.025) = 2\pi (0.007812) = 0.04909 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

*Step 4 — Critical pumping rate (1 mark):*
$$Q_c = \frac{0.04909}{4.511} = 1.088 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 10.88 \text{ L/s}$$

*Step 5 — Compare with operating $Q$ (1 mark):*
$$Q = 0.015 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 15 \text{ L/s} > Q_c = 10.88 \text{ L/s}$$
The well is **operating at 1.38 $Q_c$** — it is **over-pumped and at risk of salt-water breakthrough**.

*Step 6 — Recommendation (2 marks):* The well should be **throttled to $Q_{design} = 0.5\,Q_c = 5.44 \text{ L/s}$** to ensure long-term safety. Alternatively, the well can be **deepened** to increase $b$ (since $Q_c \propto b^{2}$, doubling $b$ quadruples the safe rate), or a **cluster of shallower wells** spaced laterally can be used to keep individual $Q$ values below $Q_c$.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Unit conversion** ($K$ from m/day → m/s) is where most students lose 1–2 marks. Always show the conversion explicitly.
> - Students frequently **forget the $-0.5$ correction term** in the denominator of $Q_c$. The full Muskat form is mandatory, not the simple $\ln(b/r_w)$ approximation.
> - Do **not** state that $z < 0$ is acceptable; the linearized Muskat equation is valid **only for $z \ll b$**. If the calculated $z > b$, the linearized theory has broken down and a numerical (SEAWAT) model is required.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024, Adapted]`

**(a)** Discuss, with a neat sketch, the physical mechanism of upconing beneath a pumping well in a coastal aquifer. Explain the roles of (i) drawdown, (ii) density contrast, and (iii) the parameter $b$ in controlling the cone height. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A monitoring study near Kollam reports the following field data: a well with $r_w = 0.25 \text{ m}$ taps a coastal aquifer with $K = 20 \text{ m/day}$, $b = 22 \text{ m}$, and $\varepsilon = 0.025$. **(i)** Using the simplified critical-rate formula $Q_c = \frac{2\pi K b^{2}\varepsilon}{0.5 + \ln(b/r_w)}$, compute $Q_c$ in L/s. **(ii)** If the field pumping test shows salt-water breakthrough at $Q = 7 \text{ L/s}$, compute the **field-measured** $Q_c^{field}$ and determine what fraction of the theoretical $Q_c$ is actually realized. Discuss possible reasons for the discrepancy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Physical Mechanism (7 marks):**

*Sketch description (2 marks):* The cross-section shows: (top) ground surface with recharge, (middle) fresh-water aquifer of thickness $H_f$ above mean sea level, (lower) the fresh-salt interface at depth $z_{GH} = h_f/\varepsilon$, and a pumping well whose screen is at distance $b$ above the interface. Beneath the well, the interface has risen into a cone of height $z(r)$ with apex pointing upward at $r = r_w$.

*Mechanism (3 marks):*
- **(i) Drawdown:** The pumping well lowers the fresh-water head in its vicinity. Lower head ⇒ lower downward pressure on the interface ⇒ interface rises. The cone height is **directly proportional** to the drawdown.
- **(ii) Density contrast $\varepsilon$:** Salt water ($\rho_s$) is denser than fresh water ($\rho_f$). The buoyancy force resisting upconing is $\Delta\rho g = \varepsilon \rho_f g$. A larger $\varepsilon$ ⇒ stronger restoring force ⇒ smaller cone. Hence the cone height is **inversely proportional** to $\varepsilon$.
- **(iii) Distance $b$:** This is the vertical separation between the well screen and the original interface. The cone height scales as $z \propto 1/b$ (first-order), while the critical rate scales as $Q_c \propto b^{2}$. Hence a **deeper** interface (larger $b$) dramatically increases the safe pumping rate.

*Concluding summary (2 marks):* The dimensionless upconing number $U_Q = Q/Q_c$ determines the safety status: $U_Q < 1$ safe, $U_Q > 1$ unsafe. Practical design enforces $U_Q \leq 0.5$.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Data (1 mark):*
$$K = 20 \text{ m/day} = 2.315 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m/s},\quad b = 22 \text{ m}$$
$$r_w = 0.25 \text{ m},\quad \varepsilon = 0.025,\quad Q_{breakthrough} = 7 \text{ L/s} = 0.007 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

*Step 2 — Theoretical $Q_c$ denominator (1 mark):*
$$\ln(b/r_w) = \ln(22/0.25) = \ln(88) = 4.477$$
$$\text{Denom} = 0.5 + 4.477 = 4.977$$

*Step 3 — Theoretical $Q_c$ numerator (1 mark):*
$$\text{Num} = 2\pi (2.315 \times 10^{-4})(22)^{2}(0.025) = 2\pi (2.315 \times 10^{-4})(484)(0.025)$$
$$= 2\pi (0.002801) = 0.01760 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

*Step 4 — Theoretical critical rate (1 mark):*
$$Q_c^{theory} = \frac{0.01760}{4.977} = 3.537 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 3.54 \text{ L/s}$$

*Step 5 — Field-measured $Q_c$ (1 mark):* The breakthrough occurs at $Q = 7 \text{ L/s}$, so:
$$Q_c^{field} \approx 7 \text{ L/s} = 0.007 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

*Step 6 — Discrepancy and reasons (2 marks):*
$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{Q_c^{field}}{Q_c^{theory}} = \frac{7}{3.54} = 1.98$$

The field $Q_c$ is **approximately twice the Muskat prediction**. This is consistent with the well-documented finding that the linearized Muskat theory **underestimates** the true critical rate. Possible reasons for the discrepancy:
1. **3-D cone geometry:** The real cone is three-dimensional; Muskat assumes a 2-D radial section.
2. **Mixing zone:** Real aquifers have a finite transition zone (not a sharp interface), and dispersion delays breakthrough.
3. **Heterogeneity:** Lenses of lower-permeability clay retard the cone's vertical migration.
4. **Partial penetration:** A partially penetrating well produces less drawdown at the interface than a fully penetrating well.
5. **Aquifer storage:** The pumped water comes partly from storage, delaying the cone's rise.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - Sketch must include labels for $b$, $z$, $\rho_f$, $\rho_s$, and the well screen position. **Unlabelled diagrams = zero marks.**
> - The phrase "field $Q_c$ is greater than theoretical" is **not** a sign of calculator error — it is the expected physical reality. Students who "adjust" their numbers to match the theory lose marks.
> - In Part (b), the simplified critical-rate formula is given; do **not** substitute the refined Muskat denominator by mistake. Read the question stem carefully.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Memorize before every KTU examination on this topic.**

- **Definition:** Upconing = cone-shaped rise of salt-water interface beneath a pumping well.
- **Key density term:** $\varepsilon = (\rho_s - \rho_f)/\rho_f = 0.025$ for typical seawater.
- **Ghyben–Herzberg:** $z_{GH} = h_f / \varepsilon = 40\,h_f$ (for sea water).
- **Muskat Equation (full form):**
  $$z(r) = \frac{Q}{2\pi K b \varepsilon}\!\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{b}{r}\right) - \frac{1}{2}\!\left(1 - \frac{r^{2}}{b^{2}}\right)\right]$$
- **Muskat Equation (simplified, near well):** $z \approx \dfrac{Q}{2\pi K b \varepsilon}$
- **Critical Rate Formula (full):**
  $$Q_c = \frac{2\pi K b^{2}\,\varepsilon}{\ln(b/r_w) - 0.5 + r_w^{2}/(2b^{2})}$$
- **Critical Rate Formula (simplified):**
  $$Q_c \approx \frac{2\pi K b^{2}\,\varepsilon}{0.5 + \ln(b/r_w)}$$
- **Scaling Laws:** $z \propto Q$ and $Q_c \propto b^{2}$.
- **Design Rule:** Operate at $Q \leq 0.5\,Q_c$ for multi-decadal safety.
- **Assumptions (always reproduce):** homogeneous, isotropic, steady-state, sharp interface, same $K$ for both fluids, fully penetrating well, $z \ll b$.
- **Validity Limit:** Linearized Muskat holds only for $z \ll b$; for large cones use numerical (SEAWAT) models.
- **Unit Conversion Pitfall:** $K$ in **m/day** must be converted to **m/s** by dividing by 86,400 before use.
- **Field vs Theory:** Real aquifers show $Q_c^{field} \approx 1.5$ to $2.0$ times the Muskat prediction.
- **Control Measures:** Reduce $Q$, increase $b$ (deepen well), cluster wells laterally, artificial recharge, injection barriers.
- **Connection to Module:** Upconing is the **local**, well-induced analogue of the **regional** seawater intrusion governed by the Ghyben–Herzberg / Henry / Glover models covered elsewhere in Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Preventive Measures (Problems from Sea Water Intrusion)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET751 - GROUND WATER ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Preventive Measures (Problems from Sea Water Intrusion)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Sea Water Intrusion — Formal KTU Definition

**Sea Water Intrusion (Salt Water Intrusion)** is the landward movement of saline ocean water into a coastal freshwater aquifer, caused when the natural or pumping-induced hydraulic gradient is reversed, allowing denser sea water to displace the lighter fresh ground water.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the phenomenon is studied under **coastal hydrogeology** and is governed by the **Ghyben–Herzberg principle**, the **Hubbert relation**, and the **concept of the fresh–salt water interface**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):**
> Preventive measures include *(a) Modification of pumping pattern, (b) Artificial recharge, (c) Injection wells, (d) Extraction barriers (pump-back wells), and (e) Subsurface barriers (cut-off walls, grout curtains).* Numerical problems frequently ask for the depth of interface, position of interface under pumping, and recharge volume needed.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Iceberg" of Fresh Water

Imagine a giant **iceberg** floating in the sea. The visible tip above the water is small, but the submerged mass is enormous. Now picture a similar body of **fresh water** "floating" on the underlying **salt water** in a coastal aquifer.

- The fresh water is **lighter** (density ≈ **1.000 g/cm³**).
- The sea water is **heavier** (density ≈ **1.025 g/cm³**).
- Because the densities are so close, a small "bump" of fresh water above sea level is held in balance by a **very deep** column of fresh water below sea level.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Golden Rule of Ghyben–Herzberg:** For every **1 metre** of fresh water head above Mean Sea Level (MSL), there is approximately **40 metres** of fresh water below MSL. The factor 40 arises directly from the density ratio.

If we pump too much fresh water near the coast, the "bump" shrinks, the "iceberg" rises, and the **salt water rises faster** (40× the rate!). This is why coastal wells can turn saline abruptly — a phenomenon called **upconing**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants (Must Memorize for KTU Exams)

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value |
|---|---|---|
| Density of fresh water | $\rho_f$ | **1.000 g/cm³** or **1000 kg/m³** |
| Density of sea water | $\rho_s$ | **1.025 g/cm³** or **1025 kg/m³** |
| Density ratio factor | $\frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}$ | **≈ 40** |
| Acceleration due to gravity | $g$ | **9.81 m/s²** |
| Ghyben–Herzberg constant (K) | $K$ | **40** |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Ghyben–Herzberg Hydrostatic Balance

The classic formulation assumes a **static (no-flow) condition** where the fresh water floats on salt water in a U-tube manner.

### Step-by-Step Logic

- Consider a coastal aquifer with a water table at height **$h$** above Mean Sea Level (MSL).
- At a point on the interface located at depth **$z$** below MSL, the fresh water column from the water table down exerts pressure $\rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h+z)$.
- The sea water column on the seaward side of the same horizontal level exerts pressure $\rho_s \cdot g \cdot z$.
- **Equating the two pressures** (otherwise fresh water would flow out or sea water would intrude) gives the fundamental Ghyben–Herzberg relation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The relation is the basis for computing safe pumping rates in coastal aquifers, designing artificial-recharge schemes to maintain the interface at a desired depth, and locating extraction barriers.

## 2.2 Hubbert's Rigorous Modification (Ghyben–Herzberg–Hubbert)

The original Ghyben–Herzberg equation assumes **hydrostatic equilibrium** and a **sharp interface**. **Hubbert (1940)** showed that the same equation is also derivable from the more general **groundwater flow theory** by equating the **equipotentials** in the fresh-water and salt-water regions.

The result is identical in form but is now **grounded in Darcy's law**:

$$z = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f} \cdot h$$

Hubbert also introduced the **critical abstraction rate** and the **position of the stagnation point** for a pumping well near the coast.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical KTU Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $z = \dfrac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\,h \;\approx\; 40\,h$ | Depth of interface below MSL | Direct calculation in 3–7 mark problems |
| 2 | $h = \dfrac{\rho_s - \rho_f}{\rho_f}\,z \;\approx\; 0.025\,z$ | Height of water table above MSL | Reverse calculation |
| 3 | $h^2 - z^2 = \dfrac{2\,Q\,x}{K\,L}$ (Dupuit–Ghyben–Herzberg combined) | Interface profile near coast | Steady-state interface shape |
| 4 | $\Delta z = 40 \cdot \Delta h$ | Rise of interface per unit drop in water table | Pumping impact analysis |
| 5 | $Q_{inj} = K \cdot A \cdot i$ | Injection well recharge rate | Sizing recharge wells |
| 6 | $i_c = \dfrac{\rho_s - \rho_f}{\rho_f} \approx 0.025$ | Critical hydraulic gradient to prevent intrusion | Pumping-pattern design |
| 7 | $V = A \cdot n \cdot \Delta h$ | Volume of recharge water needed | Volume-based problems |
| 8 | $q_{critical} = \dfrac{2 \pi K b (H - h_w)}{\ln(R/r_w)}$ | Maximum safe pumping rate near coast (after Schmorak–Dagan) | Well-spacing problems |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Tip:** The numerical factor **40** is *only* an approximation. Always show the calculation with densities when the problem states non-standard values (e.g., brackish water with $\rho = 1.010$).

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the System

- **Municipal water supply:** Design of safe yield in coastal cities (Kochi, Kozhikode, Mumbai, Chennai).
- **Agriculture:** Determining whether irrigation wells are vulnerable to salinization.
- **Island hydrogeology:** Lens-type aquifers in Lakshadweep / Maldives.
- **Nuclear-waste repositories:** Evaluating long-term stability of fresh water against deep saline formations.
- **Coastal protection:** Designing **freshwater ridge / extraction barrier** systems to keep the interface offshore.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Ghyben–Herzberg Relation

Consider a vertical column of an unconfined coastal aquifer extending from the water table down to the fresh–salt water interface.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**
- Let **$h$** = height of the water table above Mean Sea Level (MSL), in metres.
- Let **$z$** = depth of the fresh–salt interface below MSL, in metres.
- Both measured positive downward from MSL on the seaward side.

**Step 2 — Compute the pressure at the interface due to the fresh water column.**

The fresh water column extends from the water table (at elevation $+h$) down to the interface (at elevation $-z$). The total vertical length of this column is $(h + z)$.

$$P_f = \rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h + z)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the pressure at the same horizontal level due to sea water.**

The sea water column on the seaward side extends from MSL (elevation 0) down to the same horizontal level at depth $z$.

$$P_s = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot z$$

**Step 4 — Apply the hydrostatic equilibrium condition.**

If the interface is in equilibrium, the fresh water pressure pushing the interface downward must equal the sea water pressure pushing it landward. Setting $P_f = P_s$:

$$\rho_f \cdot g \cdot (h + z) = \rho_s \cdot g \cdot z$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the common factor $g$.**

$$\rho_f \cdot (h + z) = \rho_s \cdot z$$

**Step 6 — Expand the left-hand side.**

$$\rho_f \cdot h + \rho_f \cdot z = \rho_s \cdot z$$

**Step 7 — Collect terms containing $z$ on the right-hand side.**

$$\rho_f \cdot h = \rho_s \cdot z - \rho_f \cdot z = z \cdot (\rho_s - \rho_f)$$

**Step 8 — Solve for $z$.**

$$z = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\,h$$

**Step 9 — Substitute numerical values.**

Using $\rho_f = 1.000$ and $\rho_s = 1.025$:

$$\frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f} = \frac{1.000}{1.025 - 1.000} = \frac{1.000}{0.025} = 40$$

Therefore:

$$z = 40\,h$$

This is the celebrated **Ghyben–Herzberg relation**. ✅

## 3.2 Derivation of the Interface Position under a Uniform Recharge (Ghyben–Herzberg–Dupuit Combined)

In reality, fresh water is not static. The Dupuit–Forchheimer assumption approximates the flow to be **horizontal**, with the hydraulic gradient equal to the slope of the water table.

**Step 1 — Apply Darcy's law in 1-D horizontal flow near the coast.**

$$q = -K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dx}$$

where $q$ is the discharge per unit width of aquifer, and $x$ is the horizontal distance from the coast (positive landward).

**Step 2 — Apply continuity in the presence of a uniform recharge $w$ (m/day) on the landward side.**

For a strip of unit width extending from the coast ($x=0$) inland to a location $x$ where the water-table height is $h$:

$$q \cdot 1 = w \cdot x$$

(All recharge between 0 and $x$ discharges seaward through the cross-section at $x$.)

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions for $q$.**

$$-K \cdot h \cdot \frac{dh}{dx} = w \cdot x$$

**Step 4 — Re-arrange and integrate.**

$$h \cdot dh = -\frac{w}{K}\,x \cdot dx$$

Integrating from the coast ($x=0$, $h=0$ at the interface tip — but in practice $h=0$ at the shoreline) to a point $(x,h)$:

$$\int_0^h h\,dh = -\frac{w}{K}\int_0^x x\,dx$$

$$\frac{h^2}{2} = -\frac{w x^2}{2K}$$

$$h^2 = -\frac{w x^2}{K}$$

The negative sign simply reflects that the water table rises as we go inland. Re-writing for the interface position using $z = 40\,h$:

$$z^2 = 1600 \cdot \frac{w x^2}{K}$$

$$\boxed{\,z = 40\,x \sqrt{\frac{w}{K}}\,}$$

**Step 5 — Physical interpretation.**
The interface forms a **parabolic profile**, deepest at the inland end and rising to meet the sea at the coast. Any reduction in recharge $w$ (or increase in pumping) will **lift the entire interface**, threatening coastal wells.

## 3.3 Numerical Problem Solved Model

> **Problem (KTU-style):** A coastal aquifer has a water table at **+2.5 m** above MSL. The aquifer bottom is **150 m** below MSL. Compute the depth of the fresh–salt water interface below MSL. Will the aquifer fully contain the fresh-water lens?

### Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the Ghyben–Herzberg relation.**

$$z = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\,h = 40 \times 2.5 = 100\,\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — Compare with the aquifer bottom.**

The aquifer bottom is at 150 m below MSL. The interface is at 100 m below MSL. Since $100 < 150$, the interface is well above the bottom — the aquifer fully contains the lens.

**Step 3 — Compute the total fresh-water thickness.**

Total fresh water column = $h + z = 2.5 + 100 = 102.5$ m.

**Step 4 — Margin of safety.**

$$150 - 100 = 50\,\text{m} \;\;\text{(safety against full intrusion)}$$

If pumping lowers the water table to $+1.0$ m, the new interface depth is $40 \times 1.0 = 40$ m, which is much safer, but pumping $10\%$ more lowers it to $40 \times 2.25 = 90$ m (still safe). However, pumping down to $0$ m would push the interface all the way to MSL.

## 3.4 Preventive-Measures Numerical (Recharge to Restore Interface)

> **Problem:** A coastal well field pumps at **$5 \times 10^4$ m³/day**. The water table has dropped by **0.5 m** from its natural position of $+3.0$ m. By how much must the water table be raised to push the interface back to its original position? If the areal extent of the cone-of-depression is **$20$ km²** and specific yield $S_y = 0.20$, how much water must be recharged to achieve this?

### Solution

**Step 1 — Original interface position.**

$$z_{orig} = 40 \times 3.0 = 120\,\text{m}$$

**Step 2 — New interface position after drawdown of 0.5 m.**

$$h_{new} = 3.0 - 0.5 = 2.5\,\text{m}$$
$$z_{new} = 40 \times 2.5 = 100\,\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Required water-table rise to restore original interface.**

We need $h$ such that $40\,h = 120$, i.e. $h = 3.0$ m. So we must raise the water table by **$\Delta h = 0.5$ m** back to its natural level.

**Step 4 — Volume of recharge required.**

$$V = A \cdot S_y \cdot \Delta h$$
$$V = 20 \times 10^6\,\text{m}^2 \times 0.20 \times 0.5\,\text{m}$$
$$V = 2.0 \times 10^6\,\text{m}^3$$

This volume of fresh water must be recharged (e.g., through **injection wells, spreading basins, or recharge pits**) to push the salt-water interface back to its original position. ✅

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Because of the 40× amplification, even a small drop in the water table leads to a **large rise of the interface**. A 0.5 m drop in $h$ → a 20 m rise in $z$. This is why **early detection and prompt artificial recharge** are crucial in coastal aquifers.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Sea-Water Intrusion Prevention

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **decision-flow and engineering actions** for preventing sea water intrusion in a coastal aquifer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Coastal Aquifer at Risk] --> B{Density Imbalance Test}
    B --> C[Check rho_f and rho_s]
    C --> D[Apply Ghyben-Herzberg: z = 40 h]
    D --> E{Is interface safe below well screens?}
    E -- No --> F1[Reduce Pumping Rate]
    E -- No --> F2[Shift Wells Landward]
    E -- No --> F3[Artificial Recharge via Spreading Basins]
    E -- No --> F4[Injection Wells along Coastline]
    E -- No --> F5[Extraction Barrier Pump-Back Wells]
    E -- No --> F6[Subsurface Barrier Grout Curtain]
    E -- No --> F7[Natural Barrier Confining Clay Layer]
    F1 --> G[Monitor Salinity and Interface Position]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    F6 --> G
    F7 --> G
    G --> H[Long-Term Aquifer Management]
    E -- Yes --> I[Operate Normally with Periodic Monitoring]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow: From Detection to Control

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stepA[Stage1 Detection Chloride and EC Monitoring] --> stepB[Stage2 Diagnosis Compare h and z against safe limits]
    stepB --> stepC[Stage3 Choice of Preventive Measure]
    stepC --> stepD[Stage4 Design Recharge Volume V = A Sy delta_h]
    stepD --> stepE[Stage5 Implementation]
    stepE --> stepF[Stage6 Verification via Resistivity and Sampling]
```

        M3[Measure3 Injection Wells: Treated water injected upgradient]
        M4[Measure4 Extraction Barrier: Line of pumpback wells along coast]
        M5[Measure5 Subsurface Barrier: Slurry trench grout curtain sheet pile]
    end
    M1 --> Effect1[Effect Maintains positive seaward gradient]

## 4.4 Physical Schematic (ASCII Block Diagram) of Ghyben–Herzberg Setup

```
        +  +h  +-----------------------  Water table
        |       \                       /
        |        \      Aquifer        /
        |         \  Fresh Water      /
        |          \                 /
        |           \               /
        |            \             /
    MSL |-------------*----+--+---*------  Mean Sea Level
        |              \   |  |   \
        |               \  |  |    \
        |                \ |  |     \      (z = 40h)
        |                 \|  |      \
        |                  \  |       \
        |                   \ |        \
        |                    \|         \
        |                     *  Interface (Fresh-Salt)
        |                    /  \________

        |_________________/________________\____ Aquifer bottom
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation for KTU diagrams:** The interface is **NOT a fixed surface**; it is a *transition zone* of finite thickness (due to dispersion and tidal mixing). The Ghyben–Herzberg model approximates it as a **sharp boundary** for engineering calculations — this approximation is acceptable for KTU 2024 Scheme problems.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**
> State the **Ghyben–Herzberg relation** and explain its significance in the design of coastal aquifers. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The Ghyben–Herzberg relation expresses the depth of the fresh–salt water interface below Mean Sea Level as

$$z = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\,h \approx 40\,h$$

where $h$ is the height of the water table above MSL. Its significance lies in establishing the **density-controlled hydrostatic balance** between fresh and salt water. For every metre of water-table rise, the interface is depressed by approximately 40 m, making the fresh-water lens very sensitive to even small changes in $h$. The relation guides the **safe yield, well location, and artificial-recharge design** in coastal aquifers.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
> List any **three preventive measures** to control sea water intrusion in coastal aquifers. *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Modification of pumping pattern** — reducing the pumping rate and shifting wells landward to maintain a seaward hydraulic gradient.
2. **Artificial recharge** — augmenting the fresh-water mound by surface spreading or injection wells, raising $h$ and depressing the interface.
3. **Subsurface barriers** — installation of grout curtains, slurry trench cut-offs, or sheet piles along the coast to physically obstruct lateral salt-water movement.

(Other acceptable answers: extraction/pump-back barrier wells, exploitation of natural confining clay layers, creation of a freshwater ridge.)

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module-Internal Choice)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Style]*

**(a)** Derive the Ghyben–Herzberg relation from first principles. State clearly the assumptions involved. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A coastal unconfined aquifer has its water table at **+4 m** above MSL. The aquifer extends down to **200 m** below MSL. If the chloride concentration of pumped water begins to rise, and the water table drops to **+2 m**, determine:
  - (i) the new depth of the fresh–salt interface,
  - (ii) the rise of the interface,
  - (iii) the total fresh-water column thickness now, and
  - (iv) whether the aquifer bottom is still safely below the interface. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(a)

**Step 1 — State the assumptions.**
1. Hydrostatic equilibrium (no flow).
2. Sharp interface between fresh and salt water.
3. Homogeneous and isotropic aquifer.
4. Salt water is static at the seaward boundary.

**Step 2 — Pressure balance at the interface (depth $z$ below MSL).**

$$P_f = \rho_f g (h+z) \quad ; \quad P_s = \rho_s g z$$

**Step 3 — Equate.**

$$\rho_f (h+z) = \rho_s z$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $z$.**

$$z = \frac{\rho_f}{\rho_s - \rho_f}\,h = 40\,h \quad \text{(proved)}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Question A(b)

**(i) New interface depth:**

$$z_{new} = 40 \times 2 = 80\,\text{m below MSL}$$

**(ii) Rise of interface:**

$$z_{old} = 40 \times 4 = 160\,\text{m}$$
$$\Delta z = 160 - 80 = 80\,\text{m} \quad (\text{interface rose 80 m upward})$$

Note: the water table dropped by $\Delta h = 4 - 2 = 2$ m, but the interface rose by $40 \times 2 = 80$ m. The **40× amplification** is the key insight.

**(iii) Total fresh-water column thickness:**

$$h + z = 2 + 80 = 82\,\text{m}$$

**(iv) Safety check:**

Aquifer bottom is at 200 m below MSL; interface is at 80 m below MSL. Since $80 < 200$, the aquifer bottom is **safely below the interface**, and the fresh-water lens is fully contained within the aquifer. However, the **margin of safety has shrunk from 40 m to 120 m** — wait, recompute: original margin = $200 - 160 = 40$ m; new margin = $200 - 80 = 120$ m. *The interface has moved up, so the fresh-water column is now thicker, and the bottom is relatively safer with respect to bottom leakage. The real danger is that the interface is closer to the well screens and to land surface.*

**Examiner's note:** Re-read the question — "safely below" means the interface is *above* the bottom and the aquifer can still hold the lens. The answer is **YES**, the aquifer is safe against bottom-intrusion, but **vigilance is required** at the well screens.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]* (Alternative Choice)

**(a)** Explain any **four preventive measures** to control sea water intrusion in coastal aquifers, with neat sketches. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A coastal town pumps **$3 \times 10^4$ m³/day** of fresh water from a strip aquifer of length **5 km** parallel to the coast. The aquifer transmissivity is **$T = 1500$ m²/day**, specific yield $S_y = 0.15$. Pumping has caused the water table to drop by **0.6 m** below its natural level of $+2.4$ m. Estimate:
  - (i) the rise of the salt-water interface,
  - (ii) the volume of fresh water that must be recharged to restore the original interface, and
  - (iii) the minimum number of injection wells (each rated at 1000 m³/day) required for a 90-day recharge programme. *(7 marks)*

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(a)

**Measure 1 — Modification of pumping pattern:**
The simplest and most cost-effective measure. By reducing the pumping rate, lowering the pumping duration, or shifting wells **landward** of the original line, the hydraulic gradient is restored to flow **seaward**, preventing the salt-water front from advancing.

**Measure 2 — Artificial recharge:**
Surface techniques include **spreading basins, recharge pits, and recharge shafts**. Fresh surface water (river or treated wastewater) is ponded on permeable land and allowed to infiltrate, raising the water-table height $h$ and consequently **pushing the interface downward** by $40 \times \Delta h$.

**Measure 3 — Injection (recharge) wells:**
Treated fresh water is injected under pressure through wells located **between the pumping wells and the coast**. This creates a **freshwater ridge** (also called a "groundwater mound") that physically opposes the encroaching salt water. The injection rate $Q_{inj}$ must be designed to maintain $h$ above a critical value such that $z = 40\,h$ stays deeper than the well-screen depth.

**Measure 4 — Extraction (pump-back) barrier:**
A line of wells is installed **between the coast and the production wells**. These barrier wells continuously pump saline water (which is disposed of to the sea) and thereby lower the salt-water head on the seaward side, **creating a depression in the interface** that protects the production wells.

**Measure 5 — Subsurface barriers:**
Physical cut-offs such as **slurry trench walls, grout curtains, or steel sheet piles** are constructed from the surface down into an impervious stratum. They physically block the **lateral flow** of salt water. Although capital-intensive, they are permanent and effective where space is limited.

**Measure 6 — Natural barriers:**
Existing impervious clay layers or confining beds (e.g., the Vaikom clay or the Alleppey clay sequences along the Kerala coast) act as natural aquitards. Wells should be screened **above** such layers to leverage their protective effect.

---

#### Model Solution — Question B(b)

**(i) Rise of the interface:**

$$\Delta h = 0.6\,\text{m}$$
$$\Delta z = 40 \times \Delta h = 40 \times 0.6 = 24\,\text{m}$$

The salt-water interface has risen **24 m** vertically due to a water-table drop of just 0.6 m. This is the central danger of coastal pumping.

**(ii) Recharge volume needed:**

For a strip aquifer of length $L = 5$ km = 5000 m and width approximated by the area of cone-of-depression. In many KTU problems, the area is taken as $A = L \times W$ where $W$ is the effective inland width of influence. Since the problem does not give the width directly, we compute based on the **areal extent of the cone** assumed as $A = 5\,\text{km} \times 1\,\text{km} = 5 \times 10^6$ m² (typical for a 0.6 m drawdown in a $T=1500$ m²/day aquifer).

$$V = A \cdot S_y \cdot \Delta h$$
$$V = 5 \times 10^6 \times 0.15 \times 0.6$$
$$V = 4.5 \times 10^5\,\text{m}^3$$

**(iii) Number of injection wells for 90-day programme:**

Total injection required in 90 days = $4.5 \times 10^5$ m³.

$$\text{Number} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^5}{1000 \times 90} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^5}{9 \times 10^4} = 5 \text{ wells}$$

A minimum of **5 injection wells** operating continuously for 90 days will suffice to restore the water table and depress the interface. ✅

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 40× amplification.** Many students compute $\Delta z = \Delta h$ instead of $40 \Delta h$ — *this is the most common mistake and costs full marks.*
> 2. **Using sea-water density for the fresh-water column** (or vice-versa) in the pressure-balance equation. Always re-check: $\rho_f g (h+z) = \rho_s g z$.
> 3. **Confusing "depth below MSL" with "depth below ground surface."** KTU problems usually mean the former.
> 4. **Ignoring the assumption of a sharp interface** — write it explicitly in derivations to earn full marks on assumptions questions.
> 5. **Forgetting to specify the units** in the final answer (e.g., m³ vs litres, m³/day vs m³/year). The KTU key often penalises missing units.
> 6. **Treating the factor 40 as exact.** When densities differ from the standard, use $\rho_f / (\rho_s - \rho_f)$ directly.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Sea water intrusion = landward movement of saline water into a coastal fresh-water aquifer due to density imbalance and reduced seaward gradient.
- **Key equation:** $z = 40\,h$ (Ghyben–Herzberg; Hubbert verified it via flow theory).
- **Density values:** $\rho_f = 1.000$ g/cm³, $\rho_s = 1.025$ g/cm³ — memorize these.
- **The 40× rule:** A 1 m change in water-table height = 40 m change in interface position. This is the most-tested fact.
- **Preventive measures (memorize the five):**
  1. Modification of pumping pattern
  2. Artificial recharge (spreading, pits, shafts)
  3. Injection wells (create freshwater ridge)
  4. Extraction / pump-back barrier wells
  5. Subsurface barriers (grout curtains, sheet piles, slurry walls)
- **Recharge volume formula:** $V = A \cdot S_y \cdot \Delta h$.
- **Number of injection wells:** $N = V / (Q_{well} \times t)$.
- **Interface profile under uniform recharge:** $z = 40\,x \sqrt{w/K}$ (parabolic shape).
- **Hubbert's contribution:** Derived the same equation rigorously using equipotential surfaces in flow theory.
- **Schmorak–Dagan formula:** For maximum safe pumping rate near coast, useful for advanced KTU problems.
- **Assumption to state explicitly in every answer:** *Sharp interface, hydrostatic equilibrium, homogeneous aquifer, static sea water.*
- **Real-world relevance:** Every coastal city in Kerala (Kochi, Kollam, Alleppey, Kozhikode) faces this issue. Mention this in long answers to earn extra credit.
- **Limitations of the model:** Real interface is a *transition zone* (due to dispersion and tidal mixing), not a sharp boundary.
- **Key dimensionless ratio:** $\dfrac{\rho_s - \rho_f}{\rho_f} \approx 0.025$ — useful when densities are given in unusual units.
